x: ... I'm going to write you a letter and put it in with these tapes.
And then I thought, well, I might as well talk to you for a while on this other tape instead of writing a letter.
This is November the 28th, Thursday, November the 28th.
And the Phyllis has the parcels ready to ship -- Christmas parcels ready to ship to Toronto,
and Janey, and Calgary, I presume.
No, Calgary, they'll be down here.
They'll pick theirs up here.
Anyway, everything's going fine here.
We've got nice weather now the last three, four days.
I've been doing some work on my water softener.
The valve -- shut off valve on the water line.
It wouldn't shut off anymore.
It was all corroded.
And the one going into the pan on the humidifier has been leaking.
And I couldn't get it adjusted.
So I'm putting in a new valve, a new float valve.
Believe it or not, the little plastic thing, $12.75.
And I don't know how long it'll last.
The other one lasts about five, six years.
And then the orifice goes on them.
And you could put a new rubber gasket in, but it won't shut the water off.
And I had to run around and pick up the parts.
You've got to go here and you've got to go there.
One guy has one thing and the next guy has the other thing.
And between the two shopping malls here, I've been able to gather up the stuff I need.
And what else am I doing?
Yeah, I'm going to put a plastic, a stiff plastic cover in front of the
fireplace.
I've done some testing.
I put cardboard in there back when it was so cold.
What a difference it made in this familyroom.
You can't imagine the cold air that came out through that fireplace.
It's not a draft.
It's just a continuous cold coming in.
I put a cardboard in there and shut it all off tight
and you could sit in here and it was, 10, 15 below zero.
And your feet were nice and warm.
Where before that, just sit in here and your feet were always cold.
I talked to George the other day.
He phoned me, oh ya to tell me that he had been to the annual meeting of the Elks
where they give out their pins for the years.
Next year, I'm up for my 45th year as an Elk.
George is coming into his 50th in a year or so.
He's keeping fine.
Sounded good.
Said he had been talking to you guys.
I better check on this; see if it's taping properly.
Yeah, it seemed to be taping,
so all I have to do is lay back in the lazy boy here and talk.
If I can think of what to say, it's, you know,
unless you have some special topic to talk on,
it's pretty hard to keep on a continuous conversation.
I'm getting quite a kick out of all
the "bad news".
You never hear anything good on TV,
it's always bad.
Poor Reagan, he's into it up to his knees.
And I don't think our boys in Ottawa are much better.
They're trying to patch up here and patch up there
to make it look good but oh boy.
The old dept keeps on piling up, piling up.
Sinclair Stevens is having a hell of a time.
I laugh at when they're at the coffee roll.
I say, they shouldn't be so hard on that guy,
you know, I shook hands with him about 20 years ago
and he's a pretty nice fella.
You probably don't know that.
It was at the time that I was a signer for the Western Bank of Canada
that Stevens and Klein were going to start up.
And they were down here and had a meeting
in the Saskatchewan Hotel.
And all of us that were signers for were invited there.
You know, just to hear them talk.
But it's in the end, they never start the bank.
They got squabbling amongst themselves
who is going to control it.
And that's where it stopped.
But right now, it seems that Stevens has got himself
into plenty of a mess.
If what you can go by what you see here on TV.
It looks pretty rotten.
And that both him and his wife are just sitting there
telling one bit after the other.
Deny, deny, deny.
The fella that loaned her, what, 2.6 million
without knowing who she was.
Yeah.
I think he was lying a little bit, too.
However, that's all past -- let'er go.
Right today on the news, it came over -
you talk about bad news -
and the news for this morning, I heard that there was
a policeman in Calgary that had robbed 20,
had staged 20 bank holdups in Calgary
in the last couple of years.
He's a policeman.
33 years ago.
Father of a family of seven, and a very unassuming guy.
And I don't know how they got suspicious, but they,
I guess they followed him after hours or something
and caught him in the act and tagged 20 robberies
off to him.
Whether he was responsible for all of them, I don't know.
That's some more bad news.
The first minister's meeting in Vancouver,
I listened to it for nine hours, one day
and everyone they all did a magnificent job of presenting their case.
But each one ended up the next day,
then holding his hand out, give us some money,
give us some money, we need this, we need money to do that.
Well, Rudy said, God, fellas, I'm trying to hold
the line, he said, "There's got to be and end to handouts."
However, in some ways, somehow,
they're all keeping it going, keep on rolling.
Then, oh, yeah, I was talking to Hugo the other day,
stopped in there for coffee in the morning
when I was out for a walk.
He's keeping fine.
Always mad at the government.
He said "Their all a bunch of crooks."
Then he said, was in Vancouver trying to negotiate a deal for.
some of these -- one of these machines that makes steel bins.
And they had some complicated deal that he had a chance
to buy $15,000 worth of steel for $9,000.
But he couldn't find anybody that needed a bin.
So I guess that's put on the back burner.
Other than that, he didn't have anything to say about Denny.
And the farmers here are suffering.
Of course, they holler like hell.
It always was that way.
Farming was always a tough deal in Saskatchewan.
My God, I remember when I was home on the farm,
the years that you went through hardly
got enough to pull you through.
But Dad never asked for anything.
We never got 5 cents from the government.
Dad worked for 17 years.
And they never went to the municipality or anybody to get seed
or help or anything.
They always stayed on top of the pile.
Of course, he had a lot of the manpower around there to help,
I suppose that didn't hurt any.
Although there were other families around there
with just as big a family as we had that never made it through at all.
After five, six years, they'd pull it up and head back to the States
where they came from.
That's the last you'd hear of them.
But farmers, sure, they're not going to make it big.
Those that brought land at $400, $500, $600 an acre,
they're in trouble.
There's no question about it.
You can't pay that for land and then sell wheat for $2.50 or $3 a bushel.
Right now, a lot of this grain,
where they have the big, heavy crop, particularly in the Regina area here,
if they didn't get it off before the rain,
it's going to go for about grade 4, 5 or feed.
And they only get a couple dollars a bushel for it.
Well...
that doesn't count up very fast.
Those that got theirs off first,
I guess they get about better than $3 a bushel.
But their quota's only 2.9 bushels to the acre,
so they can only sell enough to pay the gas bill,
and that's about all.
The banker and the implement company will have to wait.
Yesterday, Don Horstman, one of my Rummy friends,
picked me up and we went out for coffee.
The Rummy games haven't been going on for about 3 months now.
Bill Harkson, one of the stable, steady guys,
he's got vision problems the same as I have.
We're both about equal visually.
He's been in the hospital for almost 3 months now.
He just had -- he's 81 years old, and he just had a stomach operation,
and his kidneys are gone.
He's in a hell of a shape.
He told me a month ago, he said,
"I'll never get out of here."
He could be right,
but he's in a pretty bad shape.
He's pouring blood into him, and keep him going.
I'm going up to see him this afternoon.
He's been in intensive care.
I haven't seen him now for about 8 days.
Well, I have a chat with him -
it's hard to miss with a guy,
when he's been laying in bed like that.
You know, they lose interest.
Doesn't have TV or radio or anything.
They sort of lose interest in what's going on in the outside world.
And what else could I say...
Oh, I guess we told you that Alan Akin,
Mac's wife, passed away.
Yes, we told you that.
And I was talking to Ethel.
At that time, I phoned Ethel to let her know about it.
She's keeping fine up in Edmonton.
Jimmy Lawrence getting married is, you know,
on the 6th of December.
You'll probably have an announcement.
He wasn't sending out invitations.
He said, "We didn't expect you guys to come that far to
just spend an afternoon at a wedding."
And he did the same with us.
He said, "I don't expect you people to come up."
He said, it's...
A lot of fuss for -
and this time of the year, and the weather's so uncertain,
I don't think that Wally and Willa are going up.
If anything, Wally may go.
But Willa, she settled down there.
She'd be feeling pretty good since she got back down there.
She was not in very good shape the last time she was up here,
which was just before the end of October.
She was...
Not herself at all.
She was thin and uninterested and hard to converse with.
But now, we talked to her on the phone the other night
on Wally's birthday, which was on the 17th of November.
She sounded really good.
She said she was feeling good, too.
And, oh, yeah, the big city of Halbrite got on the news
night before last.
The guy that delivers mail from here down to Weyburn
and all the towns along the line,
to Estevan and I don't know how far beyond.
But when he's at Halbrite about 5 o'clock in the morning,
I guess he must have a key to put the mail bags in the post office.
And two or three guys jumped him and took him into the post office
and tied him up and took his truck, a load of mail,
and went about nine miles to north of Halbrite out into a field
and opened up all the mail bags and searching -
I guess they got a bunch of money that was to go up to the co-op
or credit union in Estevan.
And they smashed the headlights on the truck, I don't know why,
and let it stand out in the field.
Somebody saw it, I guess, went out there and see what was happening
and found out it was the mail truck.
They have no clue whatever excepting from the driver who said,
that he thought there were three guys that jumped in and blindfolded him
and tied him up and put a rope around his neck to start with him.
He could be implicated in the thing too, you never know.
Anyway, that's the way it goes.
The snow is all gone here excepting on the north side of the building
there's a little.
The backyard might be two inches.
All the streets are icy, the sidewalks are icy.
Didn't all melt.
Didn't totally disappear, sort of melted a little during the day.
The sun don't get very high these days.
My God, I just said to Phyllis the other day,
I said it's going to have to keep working pretty hard to stay above the horizon
by the 21st of December the way we get up in the morning at 9 o'clock.
It's just barely showing up at 9 o'clock in the morning.
I don't know how it is with you fellas you're farther south.
Yeah, you'd see quite a bit
more of it than they do here, the days would be longer.
Oh! I got my reading machine.
Boy, I can really read the fine print with it.
When I was installing those parts on the water softer,
the print is so fine, you know.
And I'd put them there, and I could pull it up,
and I could read just as plain as anything.
So I opened my mail today, and the mail came in.
When I opened my letters, I was able to read the letters
and see what was in them.
So I'll have that to keep me busy.
I won't be spending so much time on the tape.
During the day, I don't pay any attention to TV,
excepting I get an hour of news at noon.
And then I don't bother with TV until half past five.
I turn it on for the news from Yorkton.
And at six o'clock, the Regina news comes on.
So I watch it about an hour then.
And then I watch all these sports.
Football.
I'm still enjoying my football.
It's much better on TV than it was at the game.
Because you see all the replays.
I'm just going to stop for a minute.
I had to stop and clear my throat.
When I talk very much, my voice gets foggy.
Because I don't talk that much.
Around here, Phyllis and I are alone.
You don't get much talking.
So when you get a chance to talk,
for 10, 15 minutes straight,
your voice starts to go foggy right away.
I noticed that
yesterday, when I was up at Hugo's,
sat there for half an hour talking,
and my voice almost totally
faltered by the time I left there.
And I think it's just from a lack of exercise.
I'll have to do some taping to myself
and play them back and erase them
and tape them over again.
Oh yeah, I'm sending these tapes to you.
That's a second version of my life's history.
I wasn't happy with the first one.
It was badly taped.
Anyway, Jane and Joe,
they've got the first version of it.
And then I took and I run that over
and re-edited the whole thing.
And that's what you're getting now.
And when Danny gets a tape deck
I'll send him one.
I'll send him a copy, too.
In the meantime, he can listen to yours.
That's four hours of tape.
And,
you'll find some of it interesting,
some of it sort of monotonous.
Verlyn gave me
the dickens. She said, "
"You talked for four hours and
you never mentioned one thing about your family."
I said, "This was my life that I was talking about."
My working life.
Not that I,
not my family or the social side of my life.
It's my working life that I was taping,
and I'm sure that you and Dan
will get quite a bit out of it,
having both worked for the company
and has some notion of what it's all about,
what goes on out there.
And yeah, what else?
Oh yeah, at Ellen's funeral,
Lyle and family were down there.
And,
Lyle's still pretty,
pretty hefty feeling alright,
still working.
I know at one time that he was going to take a
last -
early retirement,
but I guess he's still
able to carry on.
I don't know
what's going to happen to dome.
They're still hanging on,
pushing the cart to heaven.
Or dragging it behind them,
whichever.
Every once in a while,
you hear something ...
They're in trouble,
they need refinancing
constanly, or financing ...
But just as long as they
keep throwing his stuff down there.
And I haven't heard from Milton.
I wrote him a long letter about,
oh, about the end of August.
And he's still out in
Langley, B.C.
That's the way it is.
Had a letter from
Ken and Peggy Williams.
They're heading south
for Palm Springs
for four months.
So Norm,
if you want to find out something
about Palm Springs,
you can give them a call someday.
All they do is golf.
They get a place right by the golf course,
and I guess they've got a golf -
what you call it -
membership for the four months they're there,
and they're out on the golf course
every day.
I guess they're tired; in B.C.
they can golf all winter,
but it's just pretty wet.
And they have to put on rubber boots.
But they - the ardent golfers,
they'll go out and bat that ball around
even if it is wet.
On December the 9th,
just got a letter today
Central Line Contractors.
That's the one that I helped organize.
I was the first president
of the Central Line Contractors.
of Saskatchewan.
They're having a,
hospitality hour
in the Hotel Saskatchewan
on December the 9th,
so that'll be an evening out for me.
Get to see and hear what's going on
in the power business.
I don't hear much.
It's all very quiet.
Oh, Bud Waltz dropped in yesterday.
He's working in this area,
and he dropped in for a cup of coffee.
He's 57 years old.
He said, "I can't wait till I'm 60.
I want to get out."
He said, retire at 60.
It's getting tougher every day.
The older you get,
he's in the gas department now.
To get down on your knees
and work on these gas furnaces
isn't any joke.
I,
I've seen them come into my place here,
and if a guy's -
you know, you're in crowded quarters
and you have to get down on your knees.
It's hard work.
Bud would say,
"I haven't been off the shop.
I told you that ... Lumber
burnt down.
Just the office portion
of it burnt up,
and they knocked all the rest of it down.
There was nothing but a bare
lot there now."
Oh, we had some sad news
the day before yesterday.
Just that,
Clarence Herd,
and his wife, Agnes,
died suddenly.
This was in the obituaries.
Died suddenly on November the 20th,
And, private funeral.
He has three daughters,
one in Hawaii,
one in White Rock, B.C.,
and one in Winnipeg,
and a private funeral,
and then it comes out
that his wife had Alzheimer's,
and Alzheimer's,
and arthritis very badly,
and he carried her out to the garage.
Now, this is a story we get,
but it's true or not.
Carried her out to the garage,
and put her in the backseat of the car,
and he got in the front seat
and started the motor up,
and that was it.
I don't know how long it would take
you to get gassed out in the garage.
He would only have a single garage.
Probably wouldn't take very long.
So Clarence put an end to it.
He was about 74 or 5 years old,
and you know who he was,
he was on the ... Lumber
across the corner from me.
I had known him for 20 years.
But I was in business there,
And, of course, another 20 since that so I've known him for about a 40 years.
Real nice type of a guy.
Couldn't find a nicer man.
Good living.
And I guess the reason he went with her, they were a very close couple.
Throughout the years, he was quite a home man.
And I guess you couldn't take it anymore.
And Gordon South died.
I think Danny will have that in a letter...
He can't sell much of it.
Well, I guess he can if he's got a quota of 2.9 bushels per acre.
And that's for every cultivated acre you have.
So he could sell maybe most of his wheat.
The amount of acres he has cultivated between him and his boys.
They work in one big operation.
And he said he took him.
Last year, he took him $4,500 of oil.
And he doesn't own an oil well.
That was in surface rights.
They pay you $4,500 a well now.
And when they drill a well, I think they give you $5,000 or $6,000 for the initial year.
And then he had two or three dugouts.
And he's selling water.
Sells $1,000 worth of water
to Drill a well.
So he said that took him $45,000 of oil.
And don't own an oil well.
Just from the surface rights alone.
So that helped him.
But he's had a tough, tough, tough time.
Of expanding,
and a big family.
Want to keep the boys on the farm.
He had a family of 13, you know.
Same as we had.
And they lived on that same farm.
And they're all pretty well grown up.
The youngest one, maybe 12, 13 years old.
And when I was that age, I was doing a man's work around the farm.
Everything from driving an outfit
in the field to ...
you know, hauling manure.
Feeding the cows.
Milking the cows.
Feeding the lot.
You work
10, 12 hours a day ...
Unless you're at school.
Got away from some of it, then.
I think I told Danny about hunting.
I haven't heard a word about hunting for two years now.
Except Kevin Murphy, I know, goes out for a deer every year.
But I never see him.
I hear this from a friend of his.
And I'd see up to the Elks Club.
That they're going out
around Eastend for mule deer.
And this fella goes with Kevin.
And the old Murphy, oldest Murphy girl, had her second baby just the other day.
They've got two babies, in about one year.
I think Phyllis said
12, 13 months or something.
And the other one, Patsy, she was working as a nurse.
She had a sick spell a while back.
She wasn't going to work.
But I'd see her car is away now
so I guess she's back to work.
Nothing else
changed in the neighbourhood.
I never see the Sernics.
I saw Kenny and Mr. Sernic about six weeks ago.
I was out for a walk.
I went down Sunset.
And Kenny was out doing something to his car.
And I went over and talked to him.
I talked to him quite a while before.
I thought it was Wayne.
I had talked to him quite a while before.
I said, "You're Kenny."
And he said, yeah.
And he said, "I didn't think you recognized me."
I had poor eyesight again.
Mr. Sernic happened to come out to ask Kenny something while I was there.
I had a few words with him.
The Brown boys - haven't heard a thing for over a year now,
maybe a year and a half.
But Damien dropped it here one day.
And I wasn't home.
And he visited with Phyllis for a while.
But I understand he's the head Maître d' at ...
Unless he's changed in the last couple of months.
So that's sort of climbing up the ladder in a hurry.
I can't see in him as being the Maître d' at even a.
second rate Chinese Restaurant.
Well, I guess if I saw the way with people,
... up the ladder.
I'm not talking very fast, now. I might as well keep this thing going because you have to run it to the end anyway to get back to Tape 1.
So I can't think of anything else to say. Lots to say.
Uh, yeah, what else? The girls - Lynda called the other night wanting to know how to make dough for apple pie. A crust.
She was having problems couldn't get it to stick together. I couldn't believe it. At her age, she didn't know how to make a pie crust.
Phyllis told her and she did all the motions, but it was over a telephone so.
I don't know if Lynda got how you had to work it and everything.
... we didn't hear any more about that.
Pension cheque's come in; I've got to go down, put them in the bank to pay my bills.
They always come in four or five days before the end of the month. Thank goodness.
Phyllis was upstairs getting ready an hour ago.
What the time is it? It's going on to two o'clock, yeah.
Going on to two o'clock and shortly we'll be pulling out.
I don't know. I've been feeling good.
I walked, yesterday I walked 15 blocks.
Yesterday morning when I left for, walked down to Canadian Tire.
Well, first I walked, yeah, I walked for the bus to go for coffee.
By the time I get in for coffee, I walk up to the Optimist Club. That's four blocks.
Then catch the bus.
And then by the time I get in for coffee, that's six blocks.
Then I caught another bus and went down to Canadian Tire.
And then I went up to see Hugo and Tilly, had coffee there.
And I hope I figured out I had walked 15 blocks.
And it didn't bother me a bit.
But today I walked just about the same.
I walked down to Canadian Tire and back again.
That amounts to about 14 blocks.
My legs are standing up good.
In fact, I get some muscles that are hardening up,
they're always so flabby.
Now they're starting
to get some muscle.
I'm keeping my weight up.
I'm probably up a couple of pounds right now,
the way my pants fit.
I can't see the scales, so they're not ... me
going in and looking at it.
Unless I call Phyllis in to tell me how much I weigh
and I think she'd probably not tell me the truth
because she always tells me I should put on more weight.
And I said, "Yeah, then I'd have to
go out and buy new clothes.
Or else get them readjusted around the race band."
So I said, "I'm heavy enough."
When Bud Waltz saw me yesterday.
He was here when I came in from coffee.
And he looked happy.
He said, "Boy, you could put on - take some of Phyllis's excess weight around you;
and you'd look better
and she could look better."
I don't know how Phyllis liked it,
she I didn't say anything.
If I put on any weight
... it's time to slack up at the breakfast table and at the supper table ...
We're all set now
for Grey Cup on Sunday.
I'm afraid Hamilton will have trouble with Edmonton.
... pretty solid ... quarterback.
He copied from Moon, running with the ball.
They asked Parker the last game.
Don't you worry about him when he runs like that?
He said, that's the way
he wants to play it,
that's the only way he can get ...
so I let him go.
So far, so good.
But Hamilton played one hell of a game against Toronto
last Sunday. But the most impossible thing happened. I was up there the other night in Miami. It
wiping the jets out. Three points to, what, forty-five?
... kept on going, marking them up and marking them up.
Well, I'm about halfway through this tape I'm running,
put it on fast-forward and run it to the end,
throw it in the box and put it in the mail.
When you play this tape, when you play the big tape over,
when you run it, you have Danny there,
and I'm sure with the two of you, you'll get more out of it,
and you'll be able to add some comments about this and about that.
I often lay back and think now that some of the things that happened
in the course of my lifetime, it's hard to explain to somebody
that hasn't been there and done the same thing.
And...
My thrashing days, that don't mean anything to you fellas.
Well, that was the thing.
The harvest time, you know, that's when everything happened.
It was really something
for a young fellow like me to go to Raleigh about 19 years ago, started one-hand
thrashing machinery.
And it was always the stories that go around that this guy never, his machine never stops, or
that guy he's stopping half the time.
So if you were a good operator, everybody in the country knew you.
There's no question about that.
And as I've said right now, we've all been taught before that I always separate the grain
clean.
When they start cuffing in the elevators, the elevator man couldn't believe that
grain would come that clean out of the thresher.
To me,
it was just the only way you do it ...
Other than that,
you keep working at it until you get
everything set so that
it came out good.
... you can't
have a note -
notebook with you - taking notes about this and about that.
I can give you the details.
Verlyn said, how can you remember back 50 years?
Or 60, 70 years?
I said, "I don't know."
I can remember in detail things that happened 40, 50 years ago.
And remember the dates and everything.
No problem.
Ask me what I had for breakfast
or supper last night and I have to think like hell.
To remember what I ate for supper last night.
That was part of growing old.
Oh, yeah.
I had a letter from Cascade, Iowa from the cousins that were up here this summer.
And they mentioned about Uncle Hiller that was my mother's youngest brother who is still
living at 104 years of age.
He fell last spring and cracked his hip.
But he's back up walking again.
His mind, I guess, is sort of straying a bit.
She said - I had put a message in the letter to her for her dad
and she read it out to him -
She said he couldn't remember much about the Achens excepting Lorie.
Well, that's the oldest brother.
He and Lorie were just about the same age.
So his memory must be failing a bit,
which one can I expect
at that age.
Remark ...
He was never a strong man. He was always a kind of a frail looking fellow.
I remember, back of 1925 when I first met him,
he had a crippled arm -
one-hooked arm.
The elbow was stiffened.
He used that one.
He could tip -
he was a good drinker -
He could tip a drink with it alright.
But on the other hand ... he was a frail looking man, man.
He was a man never weighed over
140, 145 pounds.
And all the rest of the Smiths
were big huge fellows.
Up to 250 pounds.
So I wish you well.
Including you, Linda, and all the kids.
A very, very, Merry Christmas and a Happy New Year.
Well, I should be talking to you
at that time.
So as they say,
Over and out.
Just now I was wondering what all that noise was.
And I find out I had the microphone in my hand.
And there's the cord bent
a little bit that does all that clicking and whatnot.
So I'll know better the next time I set the mic down and talk into it.
Where you were born?
Oh, I was born in a little town of Remsen, Iowa, about 20 miles out of Sioux City, Iowa, in what was known as the Corn Belt of Iowa.
In what year?
So in the Depression, you'd be how old?
Well, as far as the Depression, the roof sort of fell.
The roof fell in about...
In 1929, I was 27 years old at that time.
And where were you then?
I was at Halbrite.
Saskatchewan.
In the, you can say, Weyburn-Estevan area.
And we had a farm.
I was brought up on a farm at Halbrite.
Your whole family was there then?
Oh, yeah.
We were a big family when we came up from Iowa.
We were, what, father and mother and 12 children, and one was born after we came.
Why did they move from Iowa?
Why did they move up?
That's a good question.
I've asked that many times during the dry years when the country blew out and everything was against you.
Why did Dad ever leave Iowa?
But he homesteaded, didn't he?
No, he didn't.
We bought land and he paid a big price for it.
Prairie, you might say, and broke it up with horses.
and did everything the hard way.
But he had a lot of manpower, this growing family.
The oldest one was 20 years old when we moved up here.
You were in the middle.
We were nine boys.
No, I was the seventh son.
And then there were girls after you.
Three girls before me and one right after me.
So you were near the end, then.
So we were a big family.
So a lot of those boys stayed on the farm?
Oh, yes.
In those days, you stayed home and worked for your keep until you were 21 years old.
And then you flew the coop.
Not like they do nowadays.
They want to go at 16 or 17.
We stayed home and you worked without wages until you were 21 years old.
And that was the standard.

okay so now you were 28 or 29 in the beginning of the depression when the depression started yeah and you had that home i left home when i was 24 years old my father died when i was 21 years old and i was the oldest one home and i took over the management of the farm so what were you doing then when the depression started where were you i was uh uh i was back in the area
I went into the power business, or working at power, in 1927.
And I started in Iowa, as queer as it may seem.
I started in Iowa, went to Nebraska, then South Dakota, then North Dakota, and finally I wound up at Esseland in 1929.
You were working for yourself?
With the power.
For yourself?
No, no, I wasn't a contractor.
I was working for a power company.
Oh, a power company.
Yeah.
and started without any education or anything, just started from the bottom.
This is a private power company?
Yes, at that time we had private power companies in Saskatchewan.
The power commission didn't start until 1928 when it was formed.
So what did you do?
What did I do?
A small power company, not really small either.
We had about 70 employees throughout the province.
And when I started in power, I started digging holes right from the bottom, on the end of the shovel, so to speak.
So in 1930, what were you doing?
I was a lineman.
By that time, I had graduated up, and I was what they called a hot lineman.
I could work anything.
And how much money did you make?
In 1930, when I started Estevan, I was making 82 cents an hour and paying my own keep.
Was that good wages?
That was good, yes, extremely good.
Now, did your wages stay the same all the way through the Depression?
Well, no.
I have to stop and think now.
In 29, it was just by chance that I got on with this power company.
In the meantime, I had gone back on the farm.
My brother and I started farming, and then mother wanted to come back on the farm.
So I ran into this power job, and I went back to it.
But only worked in the fall.
On the power corporation?
Yeah.
And then in the next spring, Mother wanted to come back on the farm, and I went back to the power company.
Wages were 55 cents an hour.
They had dropped in?
Yeah, that's when the roof really fell in.
I'd got 82 the fall before, and 55 when I come back this spring.
And I, independent of me, no way am I going to work for 55 cents an hour.
And I packed up and went back to the farm.
And did you stay there?
Well, I stopped in town on the way home, and I bought a rifle, which I still have today.
And I went out shooting golfers.
And on the farm, you could always earn your keep, you know.
We always had livestock, so you always helped.
And we had a good home on the farm.
You said your mom had moved off the farm then, in the meantime, had she?
Just for a year.
She was going to retire and go into labor, and she couldn't take it.
And who was on the farm in the meantime?
Your brother?
My brother, just younger than myself, took over the farm, yes.
Hmm.
So, you were saying that you had how many in your family?
Thirteen.
Thirteen.
Oh, my goodness.
She had fifteen.
Yeah, that's what my mother said.
The first time she had gone to a doctor after Dad died, she took sort of a nervous breakdown and
She came into my room.
I was thinking about that this morning.
It's funny that I woke up and here was Mother standing by the bed and said, I think I'm going to die.
This was about two months after Dad had died.
And so I didn't know what to do.
Just my two brothers and myself were there.
They were both younger.
So I phoned up the neighbor lady, who was a widow woman, and I said, "

mother was sick and whatever she would come with me and go to the doctor in wayburn so we took her to wayburn and doctor sent her away on a holiday that's all she need to get away after raising 15 or 13 kids oh and i have i'm coming back to that now the doctor said, "Looking her over and you had 13 children"
And mother said, I was afraid to tell him I'd had 15.
Do him more.
We're going to make any difference.
OK. How much schooling did you get?
Part.
What grade did you go to in school?
Oh.
Well, I went through grade six and about six weeks of grade seven.
And then why did you stop?
Well, the teacher we had at the time, this was country school, and I couldn't see eye to eye.
I was at the ornery age, I guess, eh?
Well, like Jeffrey.
Yeah.
Well, no, oh no, I was older than that, because I look back now and I say I was in grade five when I was at the convent, and I had turned 11 that fall.
And I don't know where the years went, because it seemed to me I was about 15 when I quit.
No, I think maybe kids didn't go as far.
Mine may have taken five twice.
I don't remember that.
Okay, so now you were in, you were saying, working for the Power Corporation in 1930, and you had a room and board, did you?
You rented a room?
At that time, you could go to a private home almost anywhere and get room and board there.
Paid my own room and door.
Yeah?
What would you have to pay for a room and door?
Oh, so reasonable you wouldn't believe it, about maybe a dollar a day or $35 a month.
And that included your board and the whole business?
Yeah, and even throwing the laundry some places.
I wouldn't agree.
How did they make any money?
Well, you'll find in that folder you have there where they're selling six loaves of bread for 54 cents.
Oh, that's true.
That tells you part of the story.
Okay, now even on your own farm then, did they own the farm by that time?
They didn't have a mortgage on it?
You know, people ask me that today.
In fact, the man that's living on the farm today has 13 children, which is sort of a coincidence.
He was at our home here about five years ago talking about the farm.
He said, "How did your dad do it?"
You know, he's into it right up to here, keeps buying more land and getting people.
I said, "I don't know."
I knew nothing about the financing, how Dad arranged that.
But I said, Dad went in there and paid a big price for that prairie, put buildings on it, put fences around it, put a well on it that never went dry.
It cost thousands of dollars to get a well.
And I said, he died 17 years later, and it was all paid for.
He didn't know.
How big a farm was it?
Just a section.
One section of the land.
So nobody was bothered then about losing the farm or mortgaging or anything?
Oh, yes, yeah.
We were one of the lucky ones because, again, that great pool of manpower there.
You know, you had lots of manpower to do everything.
Right, because everybody had their own job.
And all it cost us, we grew our own garden, our own meat and vegetables and everything.
And I remember, this was during the Depression,
when I wasn't working, and we'll come to that later, when Mother would keep the four of us for $10 a month, and we ate like every meal was a feast.
Okay, now you said, okay, you were in 1930 and you were working for the Power Corporation.
Yeah.
Stopped, went back to the farm for a little while, and then went back and found out the wages were 55 cents.
Then I, no, then I went back to the,
I went on strike, a one-man strike.
I didn't have a union behind me.
I went on strike, and I went out to the farm.
And the company come back after me.
I met the general manager on the train one day, and he said, "I thought you were working."
I said, "No."
I said, "They only offered me $0.45 or $0.55 an hour, and I'm not going to work for $0.55 an hour."
I was worth $0.82 an hour.
It was independent of me.
I was worth $0.82 an hour last fall, and I'm worth that much now.
When they got back town a week later, I got a phone call, come to work.
So I went to work.
And my first check came through at 75 cents an hour.
And I went to the foreman on the crew.
I said, "Look, how come I'm only getting 75 cents an hour?"
He said, "When I saw your work, he says, I told them to give you the same wages you had last year."
But he said, somebody else cut it back to 75 cents.
Well, I said, "Right now I'm doing 75 cents
worth of work an hour and no more, that's all.
And I was looking for the superintendent when he comes through.
And he hit town on a Saturday night, and he was looking for me.
And when I found him, I was going to really have it out with him.
The first thing he said to me, look, he said, "Do you think you can run the crew?"
I said, "Do you think I can?"
He said, yes, I do.
Well, he said, "Bill Wilson broke his leg, and I want you to take the crew Monday morning."
He said, your wages would be $0.85 an hour.
So you didn't have to fight with them?
That was the end of the fight.
And I worked that whole summer running the crew.
And that winter they put me taking over service work in Estevan.
And then I saw the Power Commission advertising for people.
So I went to my boss and I said, "Look, I said, what about that?"
They want servicemen.
Well, he said, "I'll tell you, they'll be around here when we're gone."
Now, I knew nothing about
the government going to take over all the power companies.
But he knew that was coming.
So he said, "Why don't you put in your application?"
If you get a job, OK.
If you don't, we'll keep you here.
So I put in my application.
I had a phone call in a couple of days to come up to Regina.
And then I started working for the government.
You worked here in Regina?
They had head office here.
And you worked out of here?
No, they sent me to Saskatoon.
How long did they stay there?
Well, I'm going to say to you like I said to my fourth cousin, Lisa.

She was only 16 years old, and she started quizzing me one night, like you're quizzing me now, except that she is quizzing me about my private life, why I didn't get married until I was 38 years old and all that.
And I said, "Look, you know more about me than most people know."
I said, "You've asked me these kind of questions, and you're getting around to that now."
However, it's still a secret.
How long did I stay in Saskatoon?
Yes, when I was hired in Saskatoon, they had another fellow that was a practical man, but had university education.
I was a practical man with a grade seven education, or six and a half, whatever you want to call it.
So I got the class B district.
which was Saskatoon.
Class A district was Rosetown.
When I went into Saskatoon, I was working directly under the superintendent.
And this would be what year with Dixie?
That was in '31.
I went up there in January of '31.
And after I was there, I had an easy job, an awful easy job.
They didn't want me to be traveling because they paid me 10 cents a mile for my car, and that cost money.
They felt the pinch too easy.
So I had three areas to look after going out of Saskatoon, north and east and south and east.
And 11 towns I had to look after.
So I could handle that.
There was no problem.
You said you had a car.
Did you buy it when you started working?
Yes, I had a car.
Now, to come back to that, I had a windfall.
My mother was making money on the farm.
That was the years that I was in the States, and she had $2,000 accumulated, and she gave each of us kids $500.
And you bought a car with yours?
So I bought a car, not a new car.
I bought a second-hand car for, I think, $650.
It was in good shape, almost like new.
So, in other words, you didn't find that the Depression really changed things that much?
It wasn't affecting me, no.
Really, I didn't... Well, you're always short on money, but you didn't spend a dollar unless you had to.
But you had a job then all the way through the Depression?
No.
Then I come to that now.
I left Saskatoon.
In four months in Saskatoon, the superintendent asked me if I wanted to go out to Rosetown.
And I said, "Why Rosetown?"
Well, he said,
"Jimmy Jackson's got things so messed up out there, he says, we've got to let him go."
So I was transferred to Rosetown.
I said, "I want the Class A wages, too."
I was getting $155 a month at the power, and the Class A was $165 a month.
So I went to Rosetown and took over there, and did join the work.
I was young and ambitious.
The power lines had been built in the wintertime, and the poles were leaning all over the place.
And so I'd go out and straighten all this up on my own.
I used to hire a young fellow in every town, pay him 30 cents an hour.
And the two was, we were a two-man crew.
I sent a bill in for the wages, and oh, the guy in Regina, he just raised a dick, and what authority did I have to hire a man?
So when he came out one day through the district and he saw what I was doing, he didn't say, boy, that looks really good.
He just made all his other district men do what I had been doing.
So that burned me up.
At another time, I found some trouble on a bridge at a river which was just outside of my territory, and I reported that, and I got a nasty letter telling me to stay in my own territory.
Then they sent somebody else.
to fix the trouble that I had filed.
But you were to mind your own business.
Well, I was independent as hell.
So I told the superintendent in Saskatoon, I said, "You tell that guy in Regina that he better pull in his horns or I'll tell him to stick his job."
That's what I'll tell him.
What's in Regina?
Pardon?
Chickling.
Oh.
Saving money, you know, I had money in the bank.
Oh, I bought a brand new car.
Then I traded my old one and then bought a brand new car.
It sounds like you were having a good time in the Depression.
Oh, you should have been there.
You should have been there.
And so about the end of September, I heard from my mother.
She said, "She'd like to take a trip back to Iowa."
I said, "Oh, nothing to it."
I phoned in during.
I said, "I want leave of absence."
I said, "I'm going to take my mother down on a trip to Iowa."
And, well, he said, I won't guarantee you a job.
Oh, I said, I'm not worried about a job, you know.
Independent as they come, I'm not worried about a job.
So I took my mother to Iowa.
We were gone about six weeks.
I had a rip-snorting time.
I had been down there before and knew all my cousins.
And all the contacts.
Oh, yeah, we had a lot of fun.
And then when I come back, I get in the town of Midale, where my
My brother married a girl from Midale, and there they wanted to put a little power system in Midale.
So instead of going back to the... A private one?
Yes, the village itself.
So instead of going back to the Power Commission and looking for a job, I took on the contract of putting this thing in for them, which I did.
And then I stayed in all winter in wired homes and kept busy that way.
And the next spring, now we're into '32,
I come back up to China.
Oh, hiring.
Power Commission said, we're still laying off men.
We're not hiring.
No way.
So I went back home on the farm and just coasted.
Had money in the bank.
Independent, you know.
Good home to stay in.
And I could always work enough to earn my keep.
And make a few dollars on the side here and there.
Until when?
All of '32...
And into '33, then the power company in Estevan phoned me up one day and asked me to come down for about a month.
They need a man for about a month.
So I went down and I worked in Estevan for about a month.
And then they had another project starting up there, building another town out by Gull Lake.
The boss said, I'm going to send you out there to put in that distribution system.
So essentially after that you had a job, all the rest of the depression.
Yeah, then I went out there and I had a job.
Well, we're still in depression.
Things are really tough, you know, but not for me.
I'm always...
But were you the only person you knew like that?
I mean, a lot of people say that there was a depression, but I can't... Well...
It was more for married people then, right?
I'll put it this way, I guess.
I had the ability to do the work for about one and a half men all the time.
And that's the way I worked.
So that if there was work around, I was the guy that could...
They didn't give any preferential treatment then to people who were married?
Pardon?
They didn't give any...
They didn't give the married men more of a chance?
Well, I could have bumped a married man in Estevan, but...
I said, "No way."
I said, "He's got two kids to keep, and I can look after myself."
And you didn't have to contribute to the farm or to your mother to stop keeping or anything like that?
Oh, no.
No, I struck down, no.
But when I worked then, I worked the summer of 30.
That was the fall of '33.
Yeah, then in '34, then the same company said they'd put me into Gull Lake rewiring buildings, business places that were
hay wired in, you know, went and straightened up all the metering and everything.

And I did that, and then I worked all summer by myself as a lineman by myself in towns where there was a plant, a diesel plant operating, and they could always give me a man maybe a half a day at a time, and then I worked by myself.
Saved my money.
On the 12th of October, they said, "Shut her down."
I was in East End.
They said, shut her down.
We've got no more money.

Well, again, those days you looked after yourself, and I had money in the bank, and I went, I wintered myself in a hotel where you could get a room for about $25 a month at that time, eat in a cafe for a dollar a day.
So you spent accordingly.
You spent 50 cents a day for pocket money, and you spent a dollar, the next day you didn't spend any.
That's the way it worked.
Do you remember there being
Who did the people blame for the depression?
Did people have any idea?
Did they say it was the government's fault?
Well, it's funny.
With myself, you know, there really was no depression as far as I was concerned, to get back to your other interviews.
I was never out of a dollar, never broke, you know.
I always had money in the bank.
Save it as you go.
The first that I noticed of the Depression was in the fall of '30, we were working in Biggar.
And every train that came through there, that was on the main line, if there's a hundred cars, box cars, there'd be men on every one of them.
Yeah, this is what the lady said.
And they had little sort of hobo jungles or something in each town.
Oh, yeah.
And these fellas would come out and go through the towns and
and go to the door and ask for sandwiches.
It was out of necessity.
If you get hungry, you'll do almost anything.
That was the first that I noticed.
But it still didn't bother me.
I had a job, you know.
It amazes me that they were putting electricity into the places when this was going on.
Oh, I was rebuilding.
No, but you said you wired a lot of homes in the same thing.
People were putting electricity into their house.
In Midale, I wired homes all winter in Midale in 19...
Who was paying for the wiring?
The people themselves.
But you wire a house, they'd put in an entrance, which cost $18 in those days.
Now, to get that plug put in now, it cost you $25.
But I'd put in the main entrance coming into a house for $18, and for $3, I'd put in an outlet or a light.
So they'd put in the very minimum.
You know, if they had five rooms, they'd put in five lights.
That was $15.
An 18, that's only $33.
And did most people have it put in?
Pretty well, I'd say 75 or 80 percent of the people, yes.
Well, there were some of the ones living in the small shacks, you know, and whatnot that didn't happen.
Did you know anybody that had to go on relief?
Oh, yes.
There were, while this is, later on, this carried on into
When I got back into Estevan again in 1937, all kinds of people were living off relief, right up until the war.
Did people treat them any differently than, you know, did they look down on them because they were taking relief?
Not necessarily.
It was a thing you accepted.
That's the way it was.
And people living in
you know, crowd into, like, you didn't live in a house like this, two people, you rented out every room to somebody if you only got $10 a month for it.
But to interject there for a minute, you said that people looked down on you if you were on relief.
No, because the lawyers, the only ones that weren't on relief were the bankers and the doctors.
The lawyers had the heels on their shoes, wore off, the same as everybody else.
My father objected to, he had to go down the city hall to get his relief, but they delivered it to the lawyers' house.
Oh, is that right?
I mean, there was... Yeah, a difference.
A difference.
But...
Everybody rented.
Like, again, I have to talk about Estevan and Bienfait, the coal mines where I knew every home.
I had 1,200 customers and knew them all, and they all knew me in the years that I was there.
And people were poor.
You were in sympathy with them.
I'd have to go and collect the light bills when they didn't pay, and I
The most heartbreaking thing, you'd go to a door and pound on the door, and here's a woman come to the door.
She had three, four little kids trailing her there.
And you were supposed to collect money because she was $5 behind on her light bill.
The government paid them $1.50.
On relief?
For lights, that was it, and anything over that they had to pay.
And that was one of the heartbreaking things, when you had to go out on collections.
And then, sure, we'd get the list, and here was the,

some big shot in town with 70 or 80 dollars and he he wasn't on the list to go human nature being what it is. But the relief came from the federal government did it federal yeah through the municipality yeah to get back to to uh how did it affect you the one winter the winter of 32 i had nothing to do and my brother was home on the farm
He said, "Well, I'll pay you this relief wage, which is $5 a month," is what the government used to pay, $5 a month for a man on the farm.
And he paid me $8 a month.
He gave me a bonus.
But I worked 12 hours a day.
We had a lot of livestock on the place.
I was busy.
Did you grow grain as well on the farm?
Oh, yeah, grain and livestock.
And did you always have good crops?
No, a lot of failures through the years.
So you had the drought down there as well?
Well, that came after.
The drought only came really after in the 30s when things started getting bad in that respect.
But by that time I was away from farming.
It didn't affect you then?
No.
Do you remember what the price of wheat might be?
Yeah, I had some.
One year I farmed, I had a few hundred bushels.
Well, I guess I could have sold it that fall for maybe a dollar or something, a bushel, and I thought, I'll hold it, it'll go up, and I wound up selling it for about 60 cents a bushel.
The wheat went as low as 30 cents a bushel.
But that area obviously didn't get completely dried out because you said you had gardens, farm gardens.
Yeah, that's right.
You could always grow a garden, but I don't know how.
The pastures were dried out.
You had to sell off your livestock.
The government wouldn't give you money if you couldn't grow hay.
The government wouldn't buy your hay, excepting for two cattle, two cows, that's all.
Things were tough in that respect.
And still, in our local area, there was not bad.
I was out west between Shaunavon and Assiniboia in 1935 and 1936, and there it was terrible.
And I might add,
when I was at Rosetown in 1937, that's on the main road north from Swift Current, the people were migrating up there.
It was an endless stream.
Migrating out of that area.
Out of the dry country down south of Swift Current going north.

You could go out on the highway and get up on a high area somewhere, and as far as the eye could see, there'd be one of these hay racks with a cow behind it, maybe a Bennett buggy like you see pictures of there.
Yeah, yeah.
And about every half mile, there'd be one of these, and the whole family, and up there... ...mile, there'd be one of these, and the whole family, and up there...
Taken a trek up, they went up into the Meadow Lake.
And a lot of them never came back, did they?
Oh, they did well up there.
In 1957, we built power lines up there, and these were the people that had migrated up there into the woods, had it all cleared out, and had beautiful farms.
Now, going back to the... Who did people blame for the Depression?
Government.
But it wasn't.
See, Saskatchewan was not only hit with the Depression, we were hit with the drought, which made it doubly bad.
Right.
Did people, were people politically involved, do you think?
Well, where else do you go?
You blame the government, you know.
Where are you going to go for help?
You start with the municipal level, and then the provincial level, and it finally winds up in Ottawa.
No, no, but I meant, did people, were people...
Did they have political opinions?
The other lady I talked to said that people weren't politically aware.
They tended to be small town.
Because we didn't have this.
Well, yeah, we had radios, but I'd say only half of the family.
I wouldn't say they blamed the government, Lindy. It was the drought.
Well, that's what they said.
I mean, you can't blame the government because you can't grow anything.
Yeah, but all this bunch you see pictures of train loads when they come through Ejani are going down to Ottawa to raise hell.
blame the government because they weren't, well, they didn't blame them for the drought, but they weren't getting anything for the farm.
But for the depression they were, for the crisis or something.
It was really, people were not as informed at that time as they are now.
And I say, God help us if it ever gets as bad now as it was then.
I'm afraid for what will happen.
Did they have any idea of how long, did people think it would last very long?
You never look ahead that far.
And I suppose because everybody was pretty well in the same predicament.
Everybody was in the same boat.
Farmers, you know, people were hard up.
I'll tell you where I noticed it most during the years, like I went to Iowa in 1931 and then I took my mother down again in 1938.
And when you got down there, there was no depression.
Well, financial matters, but people had everything.
When you went to church on Sunday, everybody was dressed so nice down there.
And here, when you went to church on Sunday, people were really dressed shabby in old coats and old clothes and everything.
It showed up.
But it never really did affect you as far as clothing and that kind of thing did?
One fellow told me, he said, "I always remember
you as a young man," he was about my age, and then he went out back and he said, "The only thing I could ever remember about you, you were always kind of a sporty dresser."
Well, I didn't think I was.
You had just yourself to keep.
That's why you didn't have a winter overcoat for three years.
You were married in 1938?
What did you say?
So you were married then after the Depression.
Yeah, that was one of the things that I saw.
These fellows getting married during the Depression, they didn't have anything in their homes.
They'd get in an old house someplace and have apple boxes with curtains on and so forth.
Well, I was brought up in a good home, and I said, "I'd never want to marry a girl and take her into a deal like that."
And I didn't have the wherewith to start a home.
So you intentionally didn't marry then?
Intentionally didn't get married until I... Good job the depression didn't go on a long, long time.
A couple more years.
I had one brother who never did marry.
He's a schoolteacher.
For the same reason, I suppose.
Yeah, he was a schoolteacher.
He never had enough money to get married.
Well, this other lady I was interviewing said that a lot of people were engaged for a long, long time just because they couldn't afford to get married.
I had a brother who was engaged for eight years.
Is that right?
Just couldn't afford to get married.
The other thing I wanted to know was about
what they did with their spare time, what you did socially.
Well, it's funny, even before you've gone here, when you get older, you think about things that come back to me.
When I was home on the farm, again, in the wintertime you're tied up, about four of us neighbor young gang would get together and we'd,
one house after the other would, this was stag, put on a party, stag party.
A couple of them were bachelors, the people were away on holidays, and we'd go there and play poker.
Used matches, a penny a match.
You didn't have that many pennies.
Sure, the Orsted boys.
The Orsted, a homemade beer?
No, we didn't drink at that time.
That was before beer came into being, before liquor came.
Those parties were dry.
Ice cream party and make cake and ice cream and all.
That's what we young fellas used to do.
And nobody made any, any, what do you call it?
Homebrew?
Yeah, homebrew.
No, not then.
Well, my dad always made wine.
My dad always made a barrel of wine every year.
And beer.
Well, then I started making beer, but that was in the 30s when we started making beer.
Yeah, in the years, when I was home on the farm, we made our own beer.
two cents a bottle for costs, and cost made beer just as good as you buy today.
Is that right?
Yeah.
So, and what did other people do for entertainment?
You know, there must have been a lot of men who were on it.
Now, you're talking about the Depression years, not before.
Yeah, I just want the Depression years.
Yeah, the Depression years.
You had schoolhouse dances.
As you know, every six miles through the country, there's a schoolhouse,

something like that. And a schoolhouse dance, and it would cost you 35 cents, and somebody put it on and paid the guy with the violin or the organ, whatever it was, and you had a lunch and everything for 35 cents.
That's more darn less than you could shake a stick at.
And I suppose everybody went.
Well, all the young people, oh, sure, they were always plugged, no matter how cold it was or how much snow it was.
You'd get there in the wintertime.
And then the small towns have a dance.
Maybe once a month you'd have a dance in your small town.
That was your entertainment.
And you had radio?
Yeah, we got a radio about 1923, I think, the first out.
I remember one dad, his dad came home one day with a radio with just headsets, and we used to put it in a bowl, and then we could all hear it.
In a bowl?
Open and amplify it, I suppose.
Yeah, like a big bowl.
What do you have those big bowls for?
Making bread.
Big ceramic bowl and put it in there and it echoed out.
That sounds like sitting under the towel to watch the TV.
I remember when they first came out the TVs were so snowy and you couldn't see them.
You had to sit with a towel over.
No, I've never experienced that.
Well, we're in the city here.
We always have pretty good reception.
Yeah, radios come out, and you'd listen to David McKee and Molly.
You read, you did a lot of reading.
I've seen at home, when I was home on the farm that winter, everything was read in the house.
You didn't have a library like this, you know.
You'd get a book from the neighbor, and you'd read it.
Pass it along.
Did the men who were unemployed, did they have a problem with their spare time?
They went out, and at that time we probably had 80 or 90,000 farms in Saskatchewan.
And the farmer was paid $5 a month to keep this fellow out there.
So the farmer, if he had a room, he'd get one of these men out there.
And did they pay you?
And that fellow, he was lucky if he got the $5, you know.
But he got room and board.
The farmer got paid.
He got room and board, and he kept busy, you know, something to do.
And everybody else was doing the same thing, you know, so it wasn't that bad.
Do you know anything about what they did for medical care?
Was there any medical aid or things for these people?
I never knew of anyone dying excepting one woman, a neighbor woman.
I had about probably her.
10th or 12th baby, and she died from childbirth. Just a neighbor a couple miles away.
I never knew of anybody dying from want of medicine.
Did they have a doctor and a nurse?
We had a doctor at Griffin, which was 10 miles away, but that wasn't our town.
Mydale doctor was the one that would come out.
But for a doctor to come to the farm in the wintertime is almost unheard of.
So you did most of the doctoring yourself?
Oh, yeah.
I remember sticking a fork into my foot one time.
A few hours I had blood poison.
But you had some home remedy and you cured it.
So one of the more people didn't die, didn't you?
I remember that one was hot coals in a pail and throw some wool rags and put a blanket over and put your foot in.
I don't know whether it's the heat or the smoke that...
I remember doing it.
I stuck a nail in my foot and I can remember they had to soak it.
So it wasn't supposed to be the same thing.
Well, in fact, you didn't.
Yeah, well, they'd be contaminated.
I remember this was in the summertime, too.
I had an attack to appendicitis.
That was that summer when I was home on the farm about 33.
I was out pitching bundles folded over.
I had such a pain in here.
Later on, for a couple of days, one whole day later,
in bed putting cold irons on there.
That was supposed to be good for it.
Oh, it went away.
I've never had an operation of this game.
I never heard of anyone dying for want of medicine.
Did you have soup kitchens and that kind of thing in your town?
No.
It was too small.
No.
See, small communities had a big advantage.
Everybody sort of looked after everybody.
What, shared?
Food and that sort of thing?
Well, I don't know.
If you saw somebody that needed help, you helped them.
That was...
So you don't think the government did much, really, then, to alleviate any of the problems?
They didn't... Well, the municipalities handled it.
You could get help, financial help, from the municipalities.
Well, they sent in salt fish and apples.
Oh, yeah, the government sent... From the east.
From the east.
Did everybody get that?
Oh, yeah.
The mother said, I don't want any.
The storekeeper said, you get in there and get your box of apples.
You're entitled to it.
It's for everybody.
Is that right?
The mother very reluctantly took a box of apples.
If you were self-sufficient,
You lived on a farm.
You could basically survive.
You had a cow.
You didn't have much money, but you could sell your eggs, you could sell your odd chicken, you had your vegetables, you had your meat.
So you bought a little sugar and a little salt.
But if you lived in town, you were on relief, period.
But it goes back again.
The guy took 100 pounds of flour home and a half of pork for a couple of bucks and a
They got through the winter somehow.
But they didn't allow, I mean, anybody who owned a store didn't allow much credit, did they?
Oh, yeah.
They did up to a point where it would hurt them.
And then they cut them off.
Then they cut it off.
Yeah.
Small towns, small communities.
It was the same with neighbors.
We had neighbors, well, this was even before the Depression, that didn't have feed or anything for their animals, and we were -
The guy wanted to know if he could get a load of straw.
Well, he came up there with a team of horses that could hardly pull the empty sleigh.
And they were so hard up for feed.
So dad said to us, boys, well, load them up a load of straw.
Brought it in.
Then he said to another one, he said, "Hitch a team of horses on and pull them home."
He was two miles away.
But you help people like that.
And maybe throw some oats in, too, so that he could feed his horses.
It must have been awfully desperate for people, though, who had big families.
No job.
Yeah, of course, there again, as I said, our home never suffered that way.
We're one of the...
But you did help other people, didn't you?
Oh, yeah, you're sure.
I always helped other people.
So there was a sense of community and a sense of closeness more than there is now.
Oh, very definitely.
It was so different.
I can remember this neighbor lady that I got when mother was sick.
And this after dad was dead.
They were going to sell out, go back to Illinois.
And her son took sick.
She just had the one son who was about my age.
So she asked if I'd come up and get them ready for an auction sale, all their livestock and machinery and everything.
And I went up there and I worked like a slave.
Besides doing the chores at home, then I'd go up and work there.
And I got all our horses trimmed up and dressed up for the sale and everything arranged.
Got neighbors in and we dug out the machinery.
And all the while, she kept saying, oh, Linda and I don't know what I'd do without you.
I will pay you a bell, she'd always say, I will pay you a bell.
When I was all over, she'd give me a $5 bill.
I'd worked up there about 10 days.
Well, she did that.
But you felt good.
Well, it didn't hurt me.
No.
Another neighbor, almost in the same direction, he was a bachelor, and he got me to come and take his horses in off of the prairie and trim them up and everything.
He always used that term.
He's a Scandinavian.
I pay you a bell, I pay you a bell.
He gave me a mahogany table about that big.
That was the pay I got for it.
So do you think people did a lot of trading then?
Sort of like barter, rather than paying cash for things, they'd bring barter or whatever?
No, I never saw any of that go on in the small towns, really.
Well, the barber in Mydale, he's still living.
He's over 100 years old.
He would do that.
He would trim hair for butter, or cream, or potatoes, or eggs, what have you.
But you're out on the road, and you had to pull in someplace for two or three hours.
You're afraid to be out on the road.
You couldn't see the length of a car in front of you.
And that would go on for days, I suppose, wouldn't it?
Day and night sometimes, yeah.
Did that affect people?
No, you got accustomed to being dirty all the time, you know.
I can remember working in Shaunavon in 1936.
And we were overhauling the power line that runs from Shaunavon to Assiniboia.
And we would go out every morning, a nice bright morning.
And by the time the sun got up good and it got hot, the wind would start up and by 11 o'clock in the morning you had a hit for cover.
It just seemed to create a draft, you know.
It'd come all the way from Lethbridge down.
The whole country was like a desert.
And they said, "Well, it's finished.
It'll never grow anything again.
The whole country is just..." Gophers starved to death.
It was that bad.
Birds starved to death.
Yeah, because nothing grew, I guess.
Nothing grew except in Russian pistols, and they'd get about so high.
But it did rain sometimes.
Well, and it started raining, and they grew 20 crops in a row after that.
Well, of course all it did was just shift.
They had missed just about 20 crops, too.
They kept trying to grow crops, but couldn't make it.

Did you find that there was any prejudice around in your area, like people who had immigrated into, you know, people from Europe or different places, that people feel that there was enough to go around or not enough, and they weren't willing to care?
I don't know what you refer to when you say prejudices, people coming in from outside communities.
That was a normal thing, people moving around from areas, you know.
We were almost a strictly American Eastern Canadian settlement there.
And the first that we saw of anyone that had any, what we call foreign blood in them,
They would be from German extraction, I guess.
They came in from Maple Creek.
They had gone out in that area, and they totally dried out.
But they were some of the ones that came through with horses and wagons.
They'd come into our district, and if there's a vacant farm, they got on it.
And people, you know, you come there one year, there's one, and three years, there's a half a dozen more.
So they finally come in, and now basically that's what's in the community.
Did people resent them coming in?
No.
No?
They treated them just the same as...
It was one of the things you accepted.
You felt sorry for the guy, you know.
It sounds to me like there was a real sense of neighborliness then.
Well, there had to be a case of survival.
That's how you did it.
Do you remember the church being very involved in any of the volunteers or anything?
No.
The church paid no attention to it whatever.
They had no, now I couldn't speak for, well I can speak for Estevan too.
I never saw anything organized there really, or Weyburn.
We're in a small community in Halbrite.
We're the only church between the two places.
But there's never anything done for the poor or anything, not like we're doing today.

in any of the churches there of course i suppose the churches is you know the people in the church didn't have any money either so i suppose that would be well the church is the people put it that way. Yeah do you remember when it started to get better well yes about 1942,
let's see.
So after the war started, eh?
When the war started, yeah.
Then you could see things starting to pick up a little bit.
Did a lot of the young men go into war because they hadn't had a job, do you think?
Oh, yeah, that was a...
just like that, into the Army.
Because it was a guaranteed wage.
Then relief was cut off.
It was harder to get relief.
Well, they cut off relief.
Married men and everybody into the Army.
I never joined the Army on the account of that.
I was a tradesman.
I was a journeyman electrician.
And I could have gone into the Navy with a ranking.
And then I was just married two years.
And Phyllis and I talked it over.
And I was tied to my job.
But you said they cut the relief off as soon as the war started?
Well, not immediately, but it soon followed.
In other words, as a means of getting people into the army.
As fast as they could mobilize.
Is that right?
Do you remember them having conscription?
Oh, yeah.
Well, I was up for conscription.
What did people feel about that?
That's the way it should be.
Is that right, eh?
Oh, yeah.
There weren't people, they didn't oppose to the idea of conscription at all?
No, I never heard anyone.
Huh?
Because it was so different than that in the East.
A lot of people were really, really opposed to conscription.
Well, of course, well, we had a war just before that.
The World War was only 20 years old when this one - had ended, only 20 years when this one, like the one that started.
You know, a lot of patriotism.
Down here.
More so than there is today, I think.
Although we don't know.
If a war started in ... you'd probably see it.
Yeah, but I think they had more links to Britain then.
People felt more, because Britain was at war, that Canada should go at war, too, because it was that tie.
I don't know if they would now.
Canada's much more independent.
Well, a lot of them probably.
We haven't got unemployment yet.
It's not bad yet.
If you talk about unemployment, I'll get back to that when I was in Saskatoon in the spring of '31.
I saw lines of traveling men, travelers, taken off of the road.
I don't know if they still have them or not.
The guys that used to go from town to town and take orders for things and sell things.
Like traveling salesmen.
From the wholesale houses.
I've seen lineups, two abreast, three city blocks long
waiting to get lined up down to the employment office, or else the relief, where they got relief.
That was the first guy that suffered.
It was a fellow that was not out producing anything, you know.
He went first.
Now, I wouldn't be surprised to see that come again right now, you know.
They talk about unemployment now.
I said, "No, that's..."
I don't call that unemployment what we've got now, not what I see.

it's conditions are so bad for it now. When I went into this small town of Tompkins to build a power line in the fall of '33 in that small town there were about 300 maybe 275 300 people living there, and I went in there by myself.
And now I have to get a crew of eight, ten men.
I could have picked up 30 young men just like that in that small town.
Good young men.
I had to almost fight them off to keep them from working.
Did the government, when did they start putting the power into these towns?
When did the government take over the Power Commission, did you say?
Well, the Power Commission started in 1928.
Do you think that they persisted with this and went through and took over all these corporations as a means of employment?
No, it was sort of a natural evolution.
It's funny you should ask that question.
I just finished reading a book by Clinton White.
He's a university professor.
Do you know him?
I want to phone him and congratulate him on that book.
I read it.
I knew most of the people involved right from the time it was formed.
But I wasn't involved, like he explained, all the things that went on between the private companies and the Power Commission, and how it all came about.
But it all happened.
Whether in Alberta they didn't have a power commission, the same thing happened in Alberta.
It's happened here.
Sort of a natural evolution.
Invention, economic conditions, and everything got right for it.
And it needed to be done, so they went ahead and did it.
It needed to be done, and they got new methods.
were evolved through the years, and it all came about.
Tommy Douglas always said, you know, we give all the foreigners power.
Well, we'd have got power no matter if they were liberal or conservative.
What government was in Regina?
When they took over,
The CCF had just come in.
They came in in 1942, I think, when they came in.
No, but who was in power over the Depression?
Oh, the Anderson government came in.
That was a Conservative, or what did they call them, a united government.
Progressive.
Progressive.
They lasted four years.
They came in when things were just starting to get bad, and then they just
gotten worse and when they went out they never got a seat in 4 years.
Now that tells you that people are blaming the government.
You remember hearing about Bennett just before the election he promised a whole lot of things like employment insurance and all these kind of things?
Well as I say we were not as informed then but I can remember that he was supposed to when he came in he was going to turn the whole world around you know.
And in four years, nothing happened.
So people just gave up?
No, just like that.
But nothing happened after the Liberal government came in either, did there?
No, it was the same thing.
It stalled, stalled.
They couldn't find answers until the war came.
I always say, give Hitler credit.
He put a stop to the Depression in North America, starting that war, and that made work.
And from there we come back and we build up and now we've hit another dead end.
We'll probably have another war.
We could see it.
You could see this dead end coming.
I could see it for the last five years.
I've said there's too much of everything.
We've out manufactured ourselves.
It's all right to obviously, well, people should buy more cars.
Yeah, you can only use so many cars.
But I could see it in my own uneducated way in Regina here.
The use car lots, plug full, plug full, wherever you go, every small town, Weyburn, Estevan.
The towns in between, all plugged full of used, good-used cars, not junk cars, good-used cars, so they reach the saturation points.
Go through the stores.
The stores are plugged so full of merchandise, and they're still packing it in there more and more all the time.
Our own home here, the clothes closets, everything plugged right full.
You couldn't put any more in.
That's different than during the Depression, too, isn't it, because people tended to value things more?
You don't keep them longer?
Well, everybody does as the Joneses do.
You wore the same suit.
This fellow said her dad probably wore the same overcoat for 10, 15 years.
Today, if a guy did that, you'd say, "What's wrong with that guy?
He can't buy a new overcoat."
But nobody else is doing the same.
Sure, everybody else is doing the same.
Except you.
You were living very well.
Well, I didn't live extravagantly.
I say now, though, in those years when I was a migrant lineman, I had a suitcase, a club bag, and I could put the rest of the stuff in a carton that I owned.
You kept yourself down to the basics.
You always had a good suit, an extra pair of pants.
You didn't have two jackets.
When you wore the pants, you wore a sweater with it.
That's easy to pack, you know.
And a good pair of dress shoes, and that was, that's all.
Yeah, nobody had any frills or anything, they just had the bare necessities.
That's right, and that's the way you lived.
Do you think that living during the Depression has affected anything that you do now?
Everything.
How?
Save.
You save.
I'm still frugal on everything I do.
And that was just because you were, that was the way you did.
And you saved during the depression.
Sure, I saved during the depression when things were tough, I saved.
You always save.
So that was just sort of a form of, your form of security, your way of handling that. In case you got laid off or something like that, was it?
Yeah, that's right.
Same when I started my own business, when I started contracting.
I always never went ahead until I
until I saw the finances on my own.
I didn't go to the banker and say, here, I've got to have $10,000 to do this or that.
I'd wait until I had the $10,000, and then I'd make my move.
In other words, you never knew when it was going to stop all through the 20 years that I contracted.
We were contracting for the government here, and they were building up and building up and building up.
And they almost had us out of business, building up their own forces.
and until the government changed.

We were contracting for the government here, and they were building up and building up and building up, and they almost had us out of business, building up their own forces until the government changed.
And then I was burnt out by that time, and I quit just from...
patcher.
But in other words, you got so that you didn't depend on the government for anything to provide any of the security.
So it didn't matter to you whether you had unemployment insurance or old age pension or all these kind of things then because you figured you would save and look after yourself.
I did that for myself because I'd always looked after myself, yes.
And I think we'd be better off if we had more of that now.
I agree.
I think the government's taken over too much of that.
When a person comes to a job and the first thing he asks is, what's your pension system here?
he's looking for something to ride on right there.
Although a lot of the jobs now you can't, you're not able to save, I don't think.
Well, then I'll use an old corny saying, it's not the, today the problem is not the cost of living, it's the cost of the way we live.
It's the big thing.
We all have our expectations so high that, and you can get that way.
And the same person that is that way, if they had to, they could go back to the
The house with apple boxes and cans and pans just enough to get along with a human is a funny creature, you know.
You can do with luxury and you can do with the bare necessity.
I saw some of that during the Depression.
I saw a farmer just out of our hometown that always rode high and mighty.
When things got tough, he really got out and drove a team of horses in the field, wore overalls when he was a man 70 years old.
This was out of necessity.
He did it very humbly and he was a better citizen in a small town then than when he was when he was riding high and mighty.
