Were there very many people in a town who, like were there opportunists who took over other people's farms because they had the money, bought up property and businesses or anything like that?
Really never saw it in those days, not when I was...
So everybody in town was in the same fix then?
The only people, farmers, they would be mortgaged with a mortgage company, and I guess they still are today in the same way.
Maybe much worse today because it's so easy to get money today from the government and what not to keep agriculture alive.
Agriculture, as I see it, having lived here all my life, is a struggle from day one, and it's still a struggle.
There's a few that get on top and really make it.
All this high-priced land and everything, it's not that the land is worth that much.
It's people with so much money, they don't know what to do with it.
So they say, I'm going to go and buy a couple sections of land.
How big would the town be that you were in?
Oh, when we came to Halbrite in 1907, it was about 700 people.
No, but in the 30s, when you were, where were you in the 30s?
Yeah, well, Midale was about 250 people.
That was my headquarters for a small town.
No, I didn't have general stores.
I had one general store.
You could buy a suit of clothes there, maybe.
So you did all your dealing right there in town.
Basically, you went to Weyburn and Estevan for those things.
Did they use the candles?
Did they use Eaton's candles?
Oh, yeah.
I saw Dad one time, about 19 or 20, had made up a big order for our winter clothes.
He went to town and came home, and here he had the order.
Everything, except one or two things.
And how could you get it so fast?
He said, "J.P. Shum took the order."
He said, I can sell that at that price.
He had a pretty good general store, including a fur coat in it.
Is that right?
Not a mink coat or anything, but a fur coat.
One of my older brothers, he wanted a good warm overcoat.
He said, "I want this warm overcoat, maybe $39 or something."
What did all your family do?
Now, you said there were 13 of you, and you and your younger brother ran the farm.
Now, did the girls all marry off?
Well, I have to go back now to you say, why did Dad leave Iowa and come up here?
They had land promoters coming down there and saying the opportunity up in Canada.
They used to put on excursions, give them a free ride up into Canada, and I suppose lots of food and everything.
and drive them out in the country and say, here's the kind of land you can buy.
And it was virtually all prairie at that time.
You know, there were some farms starting up.
And Dad had eight sons.
What do I want with this $80 an acre land in Iowa?
That's what farmland was worth.
Up here, he bought it for $20.
And I'll have a quarter section for each one of the sons.
Well, none of the sons stayed on the farm.
Okay, so now, the older boys, they left before the Depression.
Yeah, the oldest boy, he left the first fall.
He became 21 in November, and he left and went back to the States, and he never came back up.
The second one, he was a year younger or something like that.
He left, he went, he homesteaded out by Okama, and then he went grain buying.
He spent his life as a grain buyer.
And the next one is John.
He tried farming.
And it didn't work out, so at that time, the big trek to California, so he goes to California.
Didn't know what he was going to do.
Charles, the next one, he follows up, he goes to California.
Joe, he left and he went to California.
And Sylvester, he got to be a schoolteacher.
He stayed in Saskatchewan.
And he is younger or older than you?
They're all older.
All older.
I'm the next one.
And I spent my life in Saskatchewan, you might say outside of two years in the States.
And then Milton, the eighth one, he was on the farm.
Well, he farmed but lived in town, but always, never farmed big, just enough to make a living and so on.
And the girls all married off, and they lived in Saskatchewan?
Two became nuns.
Just one married.
Second, one just younger than me, she married.
She died young.
She was only in her early 30s when she died.
California, she's in California.
California must have had a real attraction, eh?
It did.
I mean, John must have sent back some glowing stories.
I - no it wasn't.
Yeah, I guess it was that good when he first went down.
And that's from the other Achen family.
There were nine girls and four boys in that family.
They lived just a mile away.
Now, what other Achen family is that?
That was my dad's brother.
Oh.
So they came up from Iowa together?
No, they came up five years after us.
After.
And so they migrated to California.
All of them?
Well, first the oldest girl, and then the mother, and the father,
And a pair of twins about 15 years old and two more girls went down.
They all migrated and lots of them, they still sell them.
Is that right?
Three girls living.
One girl's in Arizona and one in Victoria.
But it's funny, they all scattered.
It didn't turn out the way Dad had it planned at all.
The other thing I was just thinking about, you said that single men never had a problem getting a job as opposed to married men.
No, because the company looked at it, here's a guy, you know, that he can be a migrant.
We don't want him, well, we can get rid of him.
If he's married, you have a responsibility.
I know this other lady was saying that she was a schoolteacher, and when she married, she had to quit.
They wouldn't have people who were married being schoolteachers.
Yeah.
Well, I run a business for 20 years, and I was the same.
I always hired single men.
Yeah, I suppose.
Do you remember the big trek that came through Regina?
All those young men who were unemployed, and they had them in the labor camps.
And they came here, and they had the riot.
Do you remember that?
Oh, yeah, I remember that there again.
But that was an accumulation of men right from Victoria through, you see.
They weren't from the district here or anything like that.
What did people think about it?
They didn't pay much attention to it.
The same, I drove into Estevan after they had the big strike, the coal miner strike down there and shot two or three guys.
I drove into town and they had martial law on and I didn't know what was wrong.
It was so quiet, about five in the afternoon.
I stopped at a service station and the guy said, "I said, what's happened, Jack?"
There's nobody around.
He said,
"Got martial law here, he said."
It's just a big riot here this afternoon.
I hadn't heard a thing about it.
I didn't pay much attention to it either.
It was one of those things that happened.
So people really weren't in sympathy much with these people who went on strike then, were they?
Excepting the miners, because everybody else didn't pay too much attention to it.
Were there very many of the unions developed?
That's, yeah, they were developing unions
at that time.
Were people enthusiastic?
Pardon?
Were people glad that this was happening, the unions?
Did they think they were a good idea?
No, they didn't pay much attention to it, but the miners.
Anybody who didn't have anything much, they were the ones who were the moral sort of ones.
Yeah.
Well, the miners were getting a bad deal, there's no doubt about it.
But things were tough, I mean, for the mine company to make it go too.
Were there many people who were communist?

Well, they said the guy that came in there, this Annie Buller, she's a woman from Winnipeg, and what was the other fellow's name, the ringleader, they said "He's a communist, but he was making a living organizing."
That was the same as all these McDermott we have down in Ottawa now.
He's got a job to do.
He's got to holler and castigate all the big companies and everything for treating his men so bad.
How does he earn $100,000 a year if you don't?
So it sounds like that anybody who was sort of making go of it, they didn't bother politically or they weren't involved in the unions.
They were just glad to be left alone.
It was only the people who were really, really hurting or on relief that sort of got involved in those kind of things, were they?
Well, I don't know.
See, just when I left the company to go contracting, they were trying to organize a union in our company, the power company in Estevan.
And the organizer who sat down there, I couldn't agree with him at all.
All he was getting was so much for signing up.
And if you want $5 an hour, we'll get it for you.
That was his song.
He made lots of promises.
I said, "How can you give us $5 an hour?"
This company's having a hard time staying alive and paying us $1 an hour.
When would this be?
What year?
It was in 45, '44.
And so it was after the war.
Yeah.
And with the result of it, they never did get organized.
The company was taken over by the government.
About '46, I think, companies taken over.
Well, I can't think of anything else I have to ask you.
Unless you can think of anything else I should know.
It doesn't sound like the depression was that bad.
Listen, you can't go by Lindy.

i'm going to interview you too because he was one of the affluent type he doesn't tell you his mother sold the chickens for five cents a chicken all that kind of stuff that they went without food or they went without clothes and he doesn't tell you any of that stuff because he lived on a farm. He doesn't know what the depression was. But I think it was very individual type of thing the depression was -
This is June the 14th, 1988, and I just decided I'm going to put my obituary on tape, and it will save those left behind the trouble of figuring out all my brothers and sisters.
And they can use this as a guide, and it will probably be about what they'd want to put in the obituary.
So I'll start out with Achen, Linden Hilary, 86 years of age. Born, Le Mars, Iowa immigrated to his - with his family to Canada in 1907. Pre-deceased by his parents
one son - brothers and three sisters. Leaves to mourn his loving wife Phyllis, two sons Norman - Norman and Daniel - Norman
and Linda in brackets and Daniel. Three daughters: Linda and John in brackets. Verlin Feist and Blaze in brackets and Jane
Waugh, Joe in brackets, and eight grandchildren.

Burial of...
A funeral mass at 7:30 on such in such an evening.
Cremation and private burial.
Arrangements by Spears.
Or leave funeral home, whichever.
And I'll play this back and I'll see how it sounds.
I forgot to add, after naming the children, eight grandchildren.
Yes, I found I had room enough to put it in after Jane and Joe Waugh were mentioned.
So that's taken care of.
The end.
Now I'm going to add a little more about my history, which possibly many of my family don't even know.
My business life.
After working at home on the farm until I was
24 years old, when I left the farm and started working in the electrical field in Iowa.
I worked for, in two years, I worked for the Iowa Electric,
The Northern Light and Power in South Dakota,
The Montana Dakota Power Company in North Dakota,
and then went to the Dominion Electric Power Company in Estevan, Saskatchewan,
and the next electric company I worked for was the Tompkins Electric in Saskatchewan where I operated the power plant
and took care of the business for two and a half years before it was sold out to the Dominion Electric Power.
And then I will add that while being a foreman from the time, from two years after the time I started, I was always in charge of men.
And my first venture into business was to become the president and general manager of Tompkins Electric, which was just a small electric plant, diesel plant.
And from there, I went back to work for the Dominion Electric Power.
And started contracting on my own in 1945.
And I might mention that I was the president of Tompkins Electric, the president of Achen Minerals, the founding president of ... Minerals Limited,
the founding president of the Central Line Contractors Association, a charter member of the Kinsmen of Canada in the Estevan branch,
a lifetime member of the BPOE Elks Lodge of Canada,
A life member of the Knights of Columbus, third degree.
And a 33-year member of the fourth degree Knights, Father Huguenard Assembly.
A member of the Regina Chamber of Commerce for 22 years.
I became a journeyman electrician in 1934 and still carry a journeyman certificate at the age of 86, though I never use it.
I still have the valid certificate.
I've been on the payroll of
Achen Construction 1965 Limited since the inception of the company 21 years ago.
They still pay me a standby pittance on account of my electrical journeyman's license, which sometimes is necessary
that they have a journeyman on certain types of work.
And that's just a quick resume of some of the things that I did in my lifetime.
I just got thinking about that the other day, and I thought, hey, I've been, not many guys have been president of so many companies.
They weren't very big ones, but they still needed looking after.

I might add, and I forgot to add, that I was the founding president of Achen Construction Company in 1953 and remained as president until the company was sold in 1965 to Achen Construction, 1965 Limited, at which time I went into semi-retirement. End.
Which was purchased by four of my employees.
And it is still operating under my name, although since I made this tape, the company has been sold, sold all shares of the company,
and the name stayed with it.
The men that are running it now still use the name Achen Construction.
1965 Limited.
I also, in replaying this now, noticed that I hadn't mentioned Survived by Sister Hilary.

But that's not for sure.
I just outlined all this.
I doubt if any of my children know
of all the different things that I was involved in in my lifetime.
None of them very big, none of them very important, but they all served a purpose.

This is July the 1st, 1989.
Beautiful day.
I'm up by myself at 9 o'clock.
Pete and I.
And I've been going over the tapes I received from Andy Hemmer.
And I happened to run across this one.
So I wanted to hear Sister Hilary sing that song again.
And I said...
I just run it over so I'll close off for now.
Over and under -
Over and out, they say!
I go right back, and it starts in where I taped ...
I'll roll this back now and see what's happening.
Yes, it was erasing and taping over, so I'll just keep on talking now
and go back and start relating the story from where I was born at Remsen, Iowa, October the 7th, 1902.
My folks were living on the farm, as far as I know.
They were farming at that time.
My dad had been a drayman and a policeman in a town of 1,200 with 11 barrooms in it.
Those were the hectic days,
the pioneer days for western Iowa.
However, he farmed for some years.
He had three or four teenage boys at that time, so he thought it best to get out on the farm.
And for some reason or other, some land agent got a hold of him
and suggested that with a gang of boys like he had, he should be up in Canada
where land was cheap and a lot of room for expansion.
At that particular time, land in Iowa was selling,
well, roughly $80 an acre, where you could buy it up in Canada from $20 to $25 an acre.
But it was still the raw prairie.
It had to be broke up and put into cultivation before you could get anything off it.
And I guess they did a good job selling because dad had a lot of courage to move up in that godforsaken country
when he was in a good corn growing,
well-established community down there.
Well, we went up to Canada and they had about three boxcars of, I guess, mostly animals and some machinery.
They didn't have much machinery that you could use up in Canada.
But along with what they took along, they had a boxcar load of it.
And the rest was packed up in the passenger train and headed out.
I don't know the history of whether we—I'm sure we didn't have sleepers.
And we would have had to stop possibly overnight in Minneapolis when we got there.
I don't remember any of that.
I do faintly remember some of the train ride.
And there were—the two boys went with the boxcars of animals.
And they tell of all the fun they had riding in those side door pullmans,
if they call them, along with the animals,
all the way up from Remsen, Iowa, up to Sioux Falls, Sioux City,
and then up what they call the Sioux Line, up to Halbrite.
And landed there on the 17th of March,
after a winter of the greatest snowfall the prairie had ever seen.
There was an estimated 36 inches of snow in Malapa that winter.
However, we landed at Halbrite.
And now this is where my memory starts to get in gear.
We stayed in the hotel.
And I have a faint recollection of us being in this hotel.
This is Mother and all the children.
And then the boys and Dad arrived up there.
Well, I guess Dad was on the train with us.
And the boys came up with the livestock, and they had to unload.
And take it out to the farm.
Now, when they got out there, everything was under snow.
They tell that they had to dig into a snowbank to find the barn, a stable.
And I'd like to see, you know they - just what went on.
These guys coming from Iowa were, if they had six inches of snow there,
it was a big snowfall.
And here they come out with three foot of snow on the level.
We landed in a farm.
Six miles north of Halbrite.
As I remember it, the house stood there for years and years after.
It was a two-bedroom house with a dining room, kitchen combined.
Or a dining room, living room combined, and quite a large kitchen.
And that house stood there for 20, 30 years after we lived in it for the first summer.
And I don't have any recollection of the sleeping arrangements,
where we all slept or anything.
But there were 12 of us.
But the youngest one wasn't born yet.
But there were 12 of us and mother and dad lived in that house.
And I can't figure out, we must have had solid mattresses on the floors
in order to find a place to sleep.
But first I remember the snow was all gone when I remembered that.
I can remember the ride out there with all the deep snow.
All you could see was snow.
As far as the eye could see, everything was white.
And then everything blanks out to me.
The next thing I can remember is that I would have been four and a half years old at that time.
And Liona was just past three, about 14 months younger than I was.
And the two of us walked across the mile where they were building the house on our regular farm
where we were going to live.
And I can remember just a faint recollection.
I guess we sneaked away without authority or anything.
We followed the trail, just a trail over there.
We followed the trail over there.
And the people in the house, they gave us cookies.
And I remember prunes. And Liona ate so many prunes that she had that nickname for years after Prunes Achen.
And then I have no recollection of us moving into that new house.
And that's funny.
And I don't remember, we moved over there that fall before winter set in.
That house was finished.
They put on, the original house was 14 feet by 20 feet with just a bedroom.
The whole upstairs was a bedroom and had a sort of a ladder to get upstairs.
Well, then they put on an extension with three bedrooms on it and a big dining room and a big living room
and a new stairway going up.
So we are well off because we had one of the bigger houses in the community.
And now we're talking into 1905.
I don't think I started school.
I must have been eight or nine years old when I started school.
We used to, we had two schools.
We started at Crow Lake School and that was two miles across the field.
But we had to go around the road.
And it was three miles to Crow Lake.
I can remember starting school there with the Orsted boys in ...
And Joe Barlow, Manning Body.
We were all in grade one.
And we were learning to - the ABCs.
And I think we went there maybe two years.
And for some reason that school was closed down.
And then we went to DeVille School.
That was in the other direction.
Two miles again across
country, no roads.
We went there for, as I remember it, only one year.
And then Crow Lake School opened up again.
And we used to go around the road.
We drove horses the three miles.
And in the summer when they needed the horses in the field,
we walked the three miles.
And then at certain times of the year when it was dry enough,
we'd cut straight across the fields.
And that was just two miles going across that way.
And we used to go a walk, you know the
mosquitoes would be so bad that we'd run to get away from them.
You'd run for a ways and then you'd slow down.
And then when they'd congregate again around you,
you'd run for another stretch -
and that's the way you got your education,
on foot to school and back.
Went there.
And when I was about ten years or twelve years old -
No, I wasn't twelve then,
maybe eleven.
And we were driving.
A single horse on a buggy.
And four or five of us would pile in that one-seated buggy.
Some would stand in the back, stand on the axle.
And an old great big horse that - that drove back and forth.
And then, as I said, in the summertime when they needed them in the fields,
well, we would walk.
Well, I kept up.
That was my education, going to school.
But I will say this much,
when you're in grade, by the time you're in grade four,
you knew the times table.
Not just the four times.
You knew them right up to twelve times.
You knew spelling.
You knew geography.
You've got a good grounding in education.
There's no...
And writing.
They're very particular about your writing.
How you learn to write.
Make the letters, letter perfect, so to speak.
And then I...
Remember when I was about ten years old,
mother and dad took a trip back to Iowa via train.
For some reason, I was picked to go back with them.
I don't know if I was cute or so mean
they couldn't leave me at home or what it was,
but I went to Iowa with them.
I remember quite a bit about that trip,
about being at the various uncles' places.
And there were cousins there my age,
and we used to have a lot of fun playing.
It was in the fall of the year.
And then dad and I came back.
And mother stayed.
For some reason, she stayed.
And dad and I came back,
and I still remember us being in Minneapolis.
And he went down to a bar room
and took me down with him.
And there were all kinds of food.
You could have all the food you wanted for free.
A glass of whiskey at that time,
I guess, was about 25 cents.
And then as we went home that night,
I remember we went
down the street,
and he said,
"Take a look down this window."
We looked down a basement window,
and here there were newspaper machines down there,
running all automated newspaper machines.
And the papers were coming out of there
just like nobody's business.
Then I remember going home from Minneapolis,
and dad had me singing.
I could sing pretty good.
Some of the old songs of the day.
And of course I'd sing,
and then everybody would hand me a dime - a dime!
I had dimes,
and I had porters on the train
that were selling candies, oranges, and apples,
and all sorts of good things.
No soft drinks in those days.
But I ate so much of this stuff I got sick,
and I started puking.
And that was the end of the singing.
This train ride to Minneapolis home was about,
oh, I would imagine,
about a 24-hour train ride
to Minneapolis, yes.
It would be, oh, 24 hours
to get from our place up to Minneapolis.
I don't remember anything else then until 19...
Oh, no, not any detail,
except milking cows and doing chores,
and you start doing that
when you're eight years old on the farm.
And at 11 years old,
I rigged up a team of horses on a hay rack,
and I was a straw monkey in the thrashing house.
At 12 years old,
I was out disking the land
with horses while the boys,
all the older ones,
were busy with the thrashing.
I couldn't remember that very well.
And you really had to work
when you were on the farm.
As soon as you were old enough
to pull weeds,
at five and six years old,
you're out pulling weeds in the garden.
And when I was ... 11,
that would have been 1913,
that's right,
then Sister Aloysia was already
a novice in the convent,
at 14.
And Sister Hilary and I
both went to the convent that winter.
Mary got an education, too.
I was 11 years old.
Well, I...
Yeah, 11 years old,
and I had to do my own laundry.
You were on Saturdays,
you did your own laundry,
you washed your clothes,
got a change of clothes,
took a bath once a week,
I don't remember what bathtubs are.
I think we just took a bath.
The boys had their own room
down in the washroom,
in the laundry room.
We'd take a bath
out of an ordinary wash tub.
The food was plain and simple
and you had to eat
what was put on your plate,
or have a darn good reason
why you didn't eat it.
because you didn't like it,
that you were sick or something
was the only reason
you got away from eating
the food that was put before you.
I remember I was in grade five,
and at Easter time,
we went home,
and I didn't go back.
I don't know why I didn't go back.
I don't remember if Sister Hilary
went back then or not.
But I started school at home,
and I wrote my grade five exam
at home.
Then I must have missed a year,
or I must have failed a grade,
because I went...
I passed my grade six,
I would have been about...
I must have been fourteen
or at least fourteen
or into my fifteenth year.
And then I started school
in grade seven.
Now, I would have been
about fifteen years old
that fall, I guess.
And the teacher and I
couldn't see eye to eye at all.
I guess I was as stubborn
as a fourteen or fifteen-year-old
can be.
And she and I were jangling
all the time as the saying
go:
I was lipping her back
and whatnot.
And I was dissatisfied then,
and I said to Mother one day,
I want to quit school.
What are you going to do?
I said, "I'll go trapping."
You could trap coyotes
and weasels
and what have you.
So she said,
"Well, if you don't like school,
well, you might as well quit."
And I quit school.
That was my education.
And many, many times
after I was grown up,
and away from home,
I wish I'd have had more education.
However, I got by, as it was.
I went on the trap line then.
Well, the trap line,
I was doing chores at home.
And, now Joe - I think Joe was in the Army at that time.
And now, Zip,
I don't remember if he went.
Maybe he was going to school
in Weyburn.
He went back to school
after he had quit for a while.
He went to Weyburn
and got his high school
up,
he got,
I would think he got his grade
nine and ten.
During that time,
well, I was at home
and we had a lot of livestock.
There's always so much work
to do around there.
It would take you
all forenoon
to get the chores done up.
You had to clean the barns
every day at our place.
There was no other way around it.
Every animal had nice bedding,
nice straw place to lay down.
And then in the afternoon,
I'd go out and I'd set trap
and I'd try to catch the odd weasel,
a weasel skin is worth
maybe a dollar,
a dollar and a quarter.
And there were skunks around
and that skunk was worth
two and a half or three dollars.
And I caught the odd coyote
and they were worth
up to fifteen,
twenty dollars.
And so,
I was doing this trapping
on horseback.
I'd get on the old black mare,
old lady,
and I'd ride,
you know,
two or three miles away
from fifteen,
twenty blows zero.
You didn't stop for that.
Go and check your traps
if you caught something
and you brought them home
and you skinned them.
That was,
you kept busy all the time.
There was never a dull moment.
If you had any reading to do,
you did it at night
by coal or a lamp.
And reading wasn't as common
as it is today.
We had no radio at that time.
So you didn't get any news.
You were sort of blanked out,
as they say.
The news you got was
when Dad'd come home from town,
he'd go to town
two or three times a week
and he'd always bring home
all the gossip
from the neighborhood,
you know,
what he had heard.
But we had telephone installed
when I was about twelve,
eleven or twelve years old
when we had the telephone installed.
So you had some communication
with the neighbors,
which was good for the women
in particular.
The boys,
we never talked back and forth.
We had neighbor boys,
but we wanted to talk to them.
We got on a horse
and rode over there,
visited in that way.
And life was simple, very simple.
You worked hard and you ate well, and you slept well.
And the next day, you got up
and it was the same thing over and over.
And to get back to trapping,
after I was trapping for a while,
I found out there was a place in St. Louis, Missouri
where you'd ship your hides.
There was no one duty or anything to get them across the line.
And I found out that you got about half again as much for a coyote skin down there.
So I started shipping them down there -
and skunks the same.
And then I found out
that I'd go out
and I'd buy them
from the neighbors
with the,
they would price them
at what they could get
in the Canadian market
and then I'd ship
to the States
and make myself
a nice profit
maybe a lot of times
50% profit.
I know in one case
I went to a fellow's place,
he had wolfhounds
and he went over there
and he had
10 or 12 coyote skins.
They were in bad shape
they'd been tore up and they were bloody and everything.
And he wanted
$10 a piece for them
and I had another neighbor boy with me
and we went out of there, we had,
I think we had six or seven hides.
Some of them were so bad
I wouldn't buy them.
And they had averaged me $3 a piece.
When I showed them
a few greenbacks
well,
he started
coming around.
I took them down in the dark basement
with the cold oil lamp - lantern
and then I redressed those hides and I put them on a proper stretcher
and got them
the lead washed off them and cleaned up.
The first one I sent away I got
more for it than I'd paid for the whole lot.
And,
well,
that only lasted
so long
and then
this all
happened in about
one winter
that I was dealing
in furs
besides doing
the chores at home.
And that was over with.
Well, now I'm into 16 and 17 years old.
The war was on, and it was work, work, work.
Joe and Zip, Joe went in the Army.
Zip was away from home,
he was getting an education.
And I was the main one.
Mac was, what, two and a half years younger than me.
And we always had to do all the chores.
So you worked, worked, and worked.
You would work eight, ten hours a day, every day, in the wintertime as well as in the summer.
Summertime, you worked from
five in the morning until eight at night, every day, six days a week, and then do the chores on Sunday.
We always milked a half dozen cows.
Besides, it was all horse farming in those days.
They had up to 50, 60 horses around the farm that you had to look after.
In the summer when I was, what, 1915, I was 13 years old that fall.
Yeah, this is hard to believe.
And Joe and Zip were home that summer.
And the three of us hauled rock and gravel all summer.
Had three wagons and horses.
And we'd used to go 13 miles down to the river to get three loads of sand, load them up, and take them home.
By the time we got home, we'd leave at, say, 7 or 8 in the morning.
By the time we got home, it was 4 in the afternoon.
And you had to unload your load,
and then do the chores, and then be ready to make the trip the next day.
And this went on, I guess, for two months that we were hauling material to build this big barn.
It was a huge barn.
The foundation was 56 feet one way and 60 feet the other way.
And then the contractor came in and he built the barn.

he put down the foundation everything then we had to haul all those rock in to fill that foundation in and then filled it up with sand so they could put a four inch coat of cement all over the barn it was an up-to-date barn everything was first class. That's when I was 13 years old that fall, the fall of 1915. You want to know about work, well that was work and that same
The year before that, yeah, we didn't have a decent well on the farm.
My brother Mac and I, he would have been about ten, I guess.
We were hauling water with a team of horses and a big wooden tank.

We used to have to go two and a half miles up to where there was a pond and get this water and then haul it down and dump it in the big tank in the pasture where there were about, oh, there would have been three, four hundred
head of cattle in that 1,500-acre pasture.
And that went on until we finally got a well at home that wouldn't go dry, and then we didn't have to haul water anymore.
We didn't have to pump it.
They'd pump it into the tank and haul it down.
Somebody else finally took that job over.
We probably had to go to school when the school term started.
I don't remember the detail.
But that gives you an idea of how hard we worked when we were young.
I often said, I don't know what all the help -
I was the seventh son, and Mac was the eighth one.
And as soon as we were able to work, I can remember when they built that pasture in 1914, I guess it was, that they used to haul us a mile and a half away, and we'd dig post holes all day
with a postal order, and they'd come and pick us up at night.
We'd have our lunch with us out there, but the bald prairie, no trees or no shade or anything, just out there working.
And you'd get so tired that you'd stop and snare golfers once in a while, just for getting a little bit of a rest.
Well, that's
through the 1914, '15, '16, it was the same thing.
It was a repetitious thing.
And short on help.
I wasn't going to school then.
Work, work, work.
1917, bad, kind of a bad year.
1916, yeah.
Then, '17 is a short crop, '18 was a short crop.
Now by this time, I'm 15 years old, I'm taking a man's place hauling bundles.
And I've been out hauling bundles, and I'd be so tired when night came that I couldn't throw on the load anymore.
I'd just throw on a rack full and come in, and my brother would unload them at the machine.
And that went on, 1917.

1918 short crop. 1919 less crop again but we always fared somehow we had a lot of always raised 50 60 hogs for sale and we always had hens, 125 hens around so we had our own eggs our own pork had our own beef we always had 50 60 head of cattle and about that many horses so you're never short on work
As 1919, we dried out.
1920, the hoppers came in and just about cleaned us out.
In 1921, then, we got a crop.
And Dad bought a... Oh, in the meantime, 1918, he had bought a little Titan tractor.
It was about equivalent to four horses.
Or maybe, no, do more than that.
Do the work of about six horses.
But it was slow.
It would only travel about less than three miles an hour.
And in 1921, we had a crop.
Not a good crop.
It went about 20 bushels per acre, but a low grade of wheat.
Dad bought a small separator for that thrashing outfit.
And for some reason or other, while we were getting it rigged up, he and Joe - Joe and Zip were home that fall.
They got in a jangle of some kind, and I was 19 years old that fall.
And so he got mad at him.
He said, "Linden's going to run this outfit, and you guys can haul bundles."
So I was the chief cook and bottle washer around the thrashing machine.
I was running the thrashing outfit, and I run that same outfit then in 1921.
'22, we had a bumper crop.
'23, we had a bumper crop.
'24, the spring of '24, my dad died.
And that was a short crop that year.
We had only maybe 18, 15 or 18 bushels to the acre.
It was a short crop.
We were through harvesting real early.
And, oh yes, in '24, in '24, yeah, we had finished.
And then I went up to Griffin.
I got a job running a thrashing outfit up there.
I just, what was I, in '24?
I was 22 years old.
And after that, I went thrashing for another fellow.
Oh, I could tell you the incidental experiences that I had on those thrashing outfits when I got around and saw how those guys run a thrasher after I had run one enough.
I was always so sure when I started that machine in the morning that it wouldn't stop until night and nothing would happen.
It had eight or ten belts on it.
You had to have those all laced up properly and everything.
And you had to have everything set just right that they couldn't plug the machine by throwing in too much at one time.
And all that.
Unless you thrashed, it would be very difficult for you to understand what I'm talking about.
That was in 1924.
But I was managing the farm when Dad died.
Bud was old enough then.
Bud was, what, 13 years old at that time.
And so between, he was going to school, but when he wasn't to school, he was helping, you know, taking a man's place.
He went to school and got his grade eight education.
Mac dropped out of school about grade seven, I'd say.
Now, that's the farthest that he went to school.
And there wasn't much emphasis put on education in those days.
You know, it wasn't important that you had an education.
I don't know how Zip started back to school.
He became a teacher.
And Leona got her grade nine, I think, and grade eight in the convent.
And then she went to high school in Halbrite and got her grade nine.
I think that's as far as she went.
And then she went to California.
And Sister Hilary, of course, went through high school and became a teacher.
And Sister Aloysia, the same.
Charles and all those guys.
I don't think any of them had a grade 8 education.
You could read and write and know your arithmetic, and that's as far as it went.
I've been talking steady for a half hour.
I'll take a rest now.
This is the morning of July the 23rd.
I have a few minutes to go before I go for coffee, so I'll just continue about the years before 1924.
You may think from what I said that we never had any fun.
It was all work, work, work all the time.
Life was simple.
We made our own fun.
When I was growing up and got to be a teenager,
you'd go to a schoolhouse dance two or three times a year.
There'd be dances in town that you'd attend.
No drinking or anything at dancing in those days.
It was all very primitive.
You never saw your neighbors during the week.
Only once a week that people would gather together.
The rest of the time
you just did your routine daily work.
On the weekend, in the summertime, you'd get together and work up a ball team between a bunch of the neighbors and play, work up baseball, and that's where you whiled away the day.
Every other Sunday, we went to Mass in Halbrite.
That took two or three hours out of the center of the day.
Life was very, very ordinary.
In the summer, there would be picnics here and there that you'd attend.
But you just went along.
And I don't know whether you enjoyed life or not, but everybody did the same thing, and that's the way it was.
This is now on July the 20th, or August the 22nd.
So I'll try to continue.
I'm getting the story a bit mixed up, but I'll go back to 1924.
There I mentioned that our crop was fair and that Dad had died that year.
And I'll go back and tell the story of when Dad died.
The fall of '23, Mother had gone to California.
Yes, she went by herself.
And Leona stayed home to keep house.
It was Mac and Leona and I.
Bud was away to the convent.
Dad was there with us.
And Dad and I were hauling grain.
We had a big crop that year, and we were hauling grain.
There was no snow.
We were hauling with wagons all winter.
And he always complained that he wasn't feeling well.
I would go and load the grain after the chores was done in the morning.
I would load the grain, and then he and I would haul it to town.
Slow process.
A team of horses moving at about three miles an hour.
So it took you about three hours to make the trip, or more than that, four hours to make the trip to town and back.
And then it was chores time again.
We kept that up.
Finally, he said one day, "I'm going up to Griffin to see the doctor."
When he came home, I said, "What did the doctor say?"
"Oh, the doctor said, just a broken down old piece of machinery."
So he accepted that and kept on going.
And a couple of weeks later, he still wasn't feeling well, so he went to Kenmare, North Dakota, where there were some renowned doctors.
And he was gone.
a week, and we hadn't heard anything from him, or of him, I should say.
And finally we got a card that he had been admitted to the hospital as a patient.
And following that, the next day we got a phone call that somebody had better come down.
He's a pretty sick man.
So I caught the train that night.
I didn't have any money or anything.
I went to the banker, and the banker was a good friend of Dad's.
So he gave me money to make the trip.
When I got to Kenmare about two o'clock in the morning, well, I phoned the hospital and they said he was resting fine.
And the next morning when I got up and I went to the hospital and I got there, they said, "Your dad just passed away."
So there I was with a dead father and didn't know which way to turn.
So I phoned back up to Halbrite and talked to my Aunt Mary.
And she sort of directed me to let the relatives in Iowa know about her mother in California.
And by that time, she had worked her way up to Oregon, was staying with my sister Emma.
So we got a hold of her there, and I went back home.
We had the body shipped back to Halbrite for burial, and

mother,  mother got there by train it's funny how you could move around in those days. She'd come all the way from portland and we only held the funeral two days, I think. She was there for the funeral. And then I was in charge of the farm starting right then and there so that was in 1924 and uh
Mac and I run the farm.
Bud was there.
Leona was there, but she went to California that winter again, so we were alone with Mother.
'25, good crop.
'26, good crop.
'26, some of the boys came up from California, and they thought Mother was paying me too much money.
I had got $1,000 for the year, and the next year I was to get $1,200.
And I got on my high horse, and I said if they thought that was too much, well, let somebody else run the farm and I'd go my own way.
And I headed for Iowa with my cousin Norbert Hemmer, who was up for the harvest.
Brother Mac took over the farm.
That was the fall of '26.
We had really had a bumper crop that year.
But I went back down to Iowa, and then from there on, I started doing anything.
I didn't know where I was going or what I was going to do.
I had a few hundred dollars in my pocket.
I got down to Iowa to where I had a lot of relatives.
I'd been visiting down there two years before that, so I knew all my relatives.
And I visited around for a while.
Finally, one day, they had the rain, and it got all icy.
And I knew the blacksmith in town.
He needed some help.
He said, "Anybody know how to nail
shoe on a horse?"
I said, "No, I don't know how to nail a shoe on a horse, but I know how to trim their feet."
So he started me trimming up their feet, and he would put on the shoes.
And we got along.
He was paying me $4 a day.
I don't know.
I worked there about a week, I guess.
That was the end of that.
Ice melted, and everything went back to normal again.
And then I hung around until right after the first of the year, and a line gang came in town.
They were revamping or rebuilding power lines.
Restubbing is what it was, a restubbing job, putting a new stub in beside the old pole to keep it from breaking off.
And through a tip, one of the girls in the cafe said, if you're looking for a job, I heard the boss say he's going to fire a guy.
So I went and asked the boss for a job.
And I, yeah, he says come to work tomorrow morning.
So away I went.
And I knew nothing about what I was doing.
And I would have liked to about wore my hands out the first couple of days digging holes through frost.
The fourth day, I - all of a sudden I got the knack of it, and I got a hold of a set of better tools.
I dug as many holes as anybody the fourth day, and the fifth day I dug more holes than anybody, and I never looked back from there on.
I was getting thirty-five cents an hour, working nine hours a day, paying my own room and board, which you could get anyplace for a dollar a day.
So I was making about what, uh 3 times 20 -
making a little over $3 a day and paying $1 a day for room and board.
I had - In the meantime, I had bought a car, a Model T Ford, for about $350.
Good, reliable car.
And then away I go, and I followed the outfit.
They moved away from Worthington, where we had started, and one day the boss come along and said, we're moving across the state, down near Sioux City, Iowa.
He said, "Do you want to
go along with us?"
Well, I said, "I will."
Oh, in the meantime, he had come along one day.
He said, "Look", he said, "I put in for a raise for you for 40 cents an hour.
And they turned it down at the head office.
I'm going to plug enough time that you'll get 40 cents an hour."
He said, "I'll plug your time."
He said, "I like the way you work."
When to get back, well, I said, "Yeah, I'll go across to Iowa, but I don't think I should have to dig any more holes."
I said, "I can dig more holes than anybody you've got around here.
It's time I learned something else about the business."
Well, he said, "You won't have to."
He said, "Well, I'll start you out on other work that you get to familiarize yourself with the building of lines."
So when we got over to, it was Homer, Nebraska,
just south of Sioux City, Iowa, where we were working.
And there I started doing other things, framing cross arms and helping set poles and tamping poles and lining poles and putting on cross arms.
And pretty soon we started stringing wire, and I knew a little bit about climbing poles because I'd fix telephones at home.
So I started climbing poles, and that was the start of my career as a lineman.
This takes us into about the first of March, 1927.
And it was spring down there.
Spring broke up early, and there was no snow there by the first of March.
It was real mild weather.
And then one Sunday, I took a notion to drive up to see my brother Lorie up at Sioux Falls, South Dakota, which is about 150 miles away.
And when I get up there, I found him in a terrible shape.
He had sciatica, and he had lost his wife the year before, and he had eight kids.
The oldest girl was about 17.
And the oldest boy was about 13.
So, oh, he was in a terrible way.
He could hardly walk.
Didn't know how he was going to get his crop in or anything.
So I said, "I'll quit my job, and I'll come up and I'll put your crop in for you," I said.
When I went back, told the foreman I was going to quit, and I said, "I like this work, but I don't like the life that's with it."
I said, "You're sort of a,
a transient wherever you go.", I said, "You're never in one place very long."
And I said, "I think I better leave it right now and start doing something else."
He said, "You'll leave it now, but you'll be back."
He said, "I know you.
You're the type.
You'll be back."
So I went back up to Sioux Falls and
took over - took charge of my brother's farm.
So I'll just rest for a moment now.
Well, after a pause and taking a bit of a rest, I'll start in at my brother's farm.
Now remember, I'm 25 years old.
I'd run the home place for three years.
So I was pretty well prepared to take charge of almost anything that come along.
I had never worked on a corn farm, and this was strictly corn, cattle, and hogs.
So instead of driving a six or eight horse outfit here, you were driving a two or a four.
And with his direction, I got everything done.
I planted the corn and checked the corn when you planted
you have a wire that you string along and it trips every 36 inches it trips the corn into the ground but you have to keep that lined up that you can cultivate it both ways.
Everything went fine we got along good and had his crop all in the corn cultivated and everything going fine, and then July all of a sudden about the middle of July here comes a hail storm.
And it just knocked everything to pieces.
Well, he didn't know what to do.
It knocked the old crop down.
He had 40 acres of oats almost ready for the binder.
Well, I said, "We'll call in the neighbors and mow her down, stack her up."
So I called in the neighbors.
We mowed down the 40 acres in a hurry and had it all stacked up and everything, and now I'm finished.
In the meantime, I'd been - while I was in Iowa, my cousin from Nebraska, David City, Nebraska, said, when you're down in our part of the country, come and see us.
So I had been in touch with them, and they said harvest was coming on there.
Why didn't I come down and help them harvest?
So I did that.
I went down, and they were right in the throes of cutting the grain.
In those days, they still cut it with the binders, with horses and binders.
And we thrashed.
I was a stooker, setting up stooks.
And when that was finished, well, we started then thrashing.
The neighbors had an outfit, and I hauled bundles on that thrashing outfit.
And we got all the crops thrashed.
Well, there were some experiences on that that would make you smile if you knew what kind of a - an ornery bugger I was.
The guy -
he wouldn't move the machine, and I was pitching in the dust all day long.
So I got mad in the afternoon, and I filled that machine so damn full of straw.
I took him an hour to clean it out.
And then he got mad at me.
He gave me hell.
I said, "If you knew anything about an outfit, I couldn't plug the machine."
Well, he said, "If you know so much about it, let's see you set it that you can't plug it."
So I said, "Get me a pliers and a wrench, and I'll set her."
So I set it.
that you couldn't plug it no how. The feeder would stop if it got coming in too fast.
Well, he never said anything until after I left it came back to me that he said, "I guess that guy did know something about a thrashing outfit."
Well, we finished there and then I headed north I didn't know where I was going I stopped in at my brother's place in Sioux Falls and I had an uncle up near Aberdeen South Dakota so I
I knew the oldest boy.
I'd met him in Iowa.
He was just my age.
So I went up there, and when I got up there, well, it was just haying time.
They were not in -
well, they had been doing some harvesting.
Anyway, I said I was looking for a job.
I'd like to run the thrashing outfit.
This is what I was after.
So they turned me on to an outfit.
It was the best thrasher in the country.
So I drove up there, brave as hell, 25 years old, applied for the job.
Oh, he said, I'm sorry, I just engaged a fellow this afternoon.
But on Saturday, we're running through some oats to have horse feed for the thrashers.
And come on up anyway.
Well, I went up, and as it was, the other guy didn't turn up.

So we started thrashing, and we hadn't gone ten minutes, I don't think, and all of a sudden, biffity-bang, and pieces started flying, and part of the concave, that's the part that does the thrashing in the front of the machine, had broke loose and gone into the blower, smashed up the blower and everything.
He was a Scandinavian fellow.
He said, "Okay, he said, you fix up the front end, I fix up the back end."

Well, I found out that they had overhauled the separator, and they hadn't set the concaves right in them, and one of them dropped out, had went through into the blower and bent things up badly back there.
He got that all straightened out, and in an hour and a half, we were thrashing again.
He come to me.
He said, "I think we'll get along okay."
He says, "You, you come back when we start thrashing.
You've got the job."
Well, the wages were ten dollars a day.
Ten, room and board, which was big money at that time.
Well, his brother ran the engine, and I ran the separator.
And we thrashed twelve hours a day, thrashing belt time.
He said, "I don't want this machine to stop, ever.
For twelve hours a day, I want it to be turning.
As long as the belts are turning, I get paid."
We thrashed
- what was it - 29 out of 31 days.
We thrashed right through Sundays and all.
We got laid out twice for showers of rain.
And in that time, I only had an hour and a half lost time with the separator, and the engineer, he had about two hours lost time with his engine.
That was all the lost time we had, thrashing 12 hours a day.
You ate
lunch on the run.
In the mornings, you always had lunch.
At noon, you stopped for half an hour for dinner.
In the afternoon, you had donuts and coffee again.
And then you'd thrash right through until about 6.30 or 7 o'clock at night.
Then you had to do all your belt lacing and everything like that.
I hardly ever got to bed before midnight.
And at 4.30 in the morning, he was there calling again.
His call was, oh, I must

relate that we slept in the barns and the bedding you were furnished, was a horse blanket and you can make your own bed out of hay or however you want it and my way of sleeping was right on the hard floor with a little straw under my head for a pillow. I'd take off all my clothes at night shake them out I wore high top boots,
take them off, put everything back on, excepting my high-top boots, and lay down.
And by the time you had slept, say, four and a half hours, and he was, his call was, "Daylight in the swamp, boys." and you were glad to get up after laying on the hard floor for four hours.
Well, that was the way I slept for a month, right on the hard floor.
No, just the horse blanket,
laying on it and used half of it to cover me.
However, that season wound up.
I picked up my $300 or whatever it was.
And then I headed north -
yeah, then I was home since - now it's fall.
I wanted to come to Canada and see the family.
So I come up to Canada to see the family, driving my Model T Ford.
And let me get ahead of myself now.
I have to stop right now and think.
Yes, I headed north for Canada.
I spent a couple of weeks up home, and then
driving back to South Dakota, down to where my uncle lived at Frederick, out of Aberdeen, South Dakota.
I rolled my car, went to sleep at the wheel.
I was driving straight through in the middle of the night.
About daylight in the morning, I rolled my car.
The luckiest guy in the world.
Smashed all the glass in it, and I never even got a bruise or a cut.
Well, I went into Valley City, and I patched it up enough that I could drive another hundred miles down to my uncle's place.
So while I was there, my cousin said, hey, there's a power gang working down here at Leola, about 25 miles from there.
He said, "They're building a power line in there."
Maybe we could get a job.
So I -
He and I go on down there one afternoon, and by God, I go in the hotel and talk to the foreman.
He was half drunk.
Told him what experience I had.
He said, "You come to work Monday morning."
And I went to work.
Frank Wenner was his name.
A Finlander, about five foot two, and weighed about 180 pounds.
He reminded me of Uncle Wally for build.
Anyway, he wasn't a guy that went out on the job.
He stayed in the hotel and stayed drunk and let the crew run their own.
He had a foreman that would run the crew.

Well, when we got from Leola, we went up to Frederick, and I was acquainted with Frederick, and he knew that, so he used to keep me in about every second day to go out and buy him a bottle of whiskey.
Everything was home brew.
The country was dry, so you had to go out and buy bootleg liquor.
And he took a liking to me somehow, and he always favored me.
From there we moved over into Dawson, North Dakota.
This was the middle of the winter, oh, maybe in about February.
And in Dawson, North Dakota, we were there building lines.
He took pneumonia and died.
So they brought in another foreman, a fellow by the name of Bill Oberts.
And Bill was a quiet type of a guy.
He didn't go out to the job much either.
He relied on his foreman bringing in reports to him.
By this time, I'm pretty well recognized in the outfit now.
I'm in with the linemen, the guys that are four or five linemen, and I was semi with them.
And we kept on going and going, and -
Then we split up the crew and they get another foreman.
And this fellow, he took a liking to me.
He's a younger man.
He taught me an awful lot about line work and gave me a lot of breaks.
Then they put two crews together and we built a heavy line from
Thunder Hawk, South Dakota, up to Elgin, North Dakota, about 40 miles across.
And I mean no man's land.
There wasn't a damn thing in there but hills up and down, up and down, and up and down.
And in the meantime, they had bought a boring machine to bore the holes instead of digging them by hand.
So they saw they were behind schedule, so they decided to run the outfit
the boring machine 24 hours a day.
And my friend, Elmer Jones, who was a lineman also, he got the job running the boring machine.
So the foreman didn't know who he could get for a second man, and the timekeeper was a good friend of mine and knew me pretty well.
He said, "You put Achen on that machine, he'll run it."
So I was put on the boring machine, which was, well,
one of the top-matched jobs on the outfit, and had a lot of experience with that machine.
It wasn't what machines are today, but it had its abilities and it had its disabilities.
It required a lot of maintenance, and I was good at that.
I had the experience
on machines from the farm, so I had no trouble getting along with them.
However, in the meantime, harvest comes along.
My old thrasher up in South Dakota, he gets word to me, he wants me to come back up there to run his separator, and he'll give me $12 a day.
So I leave the outfit, and I go up back to Frederick to -
to thrash.
Well, we had a bad fall.
It rained a lot, and a lot of lost time and whatnot.
I wasn't doing too well financially.
Well, I was making my $12 a day on the thrash and nothing, room and board only when we didn't thrash.
So we finished that job, and then my uncle had a machine just like it.
It was a sort of a crop machine. Four farmers owned it.

<backwards it erases everything and I'm still finding out> - papers well we finished thrashing there we only thrashed about 10 or 12 days and then I, I was finished and I didn't know where I was going or what I was going to do so I jumped in the car and I started heading for Canada.
I got up as far as Minot, North Dakota, and I stopped in the city there to see if I could get a job.
"Oh, yeah, sure, no problem.
Come and get a job here, working in the city."
That was on a Saturday, I guess, or a Friday.
And ,"report to work for Monday morning."
So Monday morning, I
Well, in the meantime, on Saturday and Sunday, I went around, and I looked, and, oh, boy, I thought, I'm getting ahead of myself here.
This is pretty heavy stuff, pretty hot stuff.
I'll be working in here.
So come Monday morning, I never reported for work.
I got in the car, and I headed up for Canada.
I hadn't got up the road 50 miles, and here I come across a small line gang working right along the highway, so I stopped and,
and I got talking to them, and I got talking to the foreman, and I told him who I was and what I'd been doing, and he said, "You looking for a job?"
Well, I said, I'm out of a job right now.
Well, he said, my head man is leaving here, and he said, "I haven't got a good experienced man on the crew."
He only had about -
waiting for you.
So I go up to Kenmare and I go into the office and tell him that I'd been talking to Leo O'Grady, a foreman.
He said that he would take me on.
So they said report to work on Monday morning.
My wages were 87 cents an hour and I paid my own room and board.
Well, again, it was something else to go in an outfit and right away you're made the head man.
I didn't have that much experience under my belt, but I had a lot of confidence and a lot of ability.
I had been taught something about working hotline work, had done some hotline work.
So, and - the foreman and I got along very good.
He was an Irishman and full of humor.
Right away, I was the top man on the job.
That was in November, early November, yeah.
I started working then.
I worked all winter.
We were what they called the flying squad.
We had 150 miles of line from Mohall, North Dakota, over into the last town going out of North Dakota on the other side, Mohall -
Mohall was on the east
and somewhere past Crosby, about 30 miles, was the end of our last territory.
We were what they called the flying squad.
If there was trouble anyplace, they blew the whistle, and we went there and took care of it.
And I had some real good experience on that job in a matter of a few months.
And everything was going fine until while we were working in Crosby, North Dakota, and one night they pound at the door and it was the immigration -
he said, "We've got a lot of unemployment in this country", and he said, "You can't work down here unless you get a permit to work here."
He said, "You'd better go to into Portal and report to
the chief immigration officer in there and see what he's got to say about it."
So he took me into a Portal and that night and the next morning I went down to see the immigration chief and told him the story.
"Ah, " he said, "You can't work down here."
He said, "We've got to deport you."
He said, "We'll allow you to make a willful deportation."
You just sign this card here that you're willfully deporting yourself from the United States, and that's the end of your working down here.
Well, to get ahead of the story, in the fall when I had gone back up to visit, Mother wanted to get off the farm.
Bud was tired of the farm.
So Brother Mac and I were going to rent the farm.
We drew up a
a deal or an arrangement whereby we would take over -
- so. That was all cut and dried, so when they said, "You better skidoo back up to Canada," I had no qualms.
I went up there and immediately got a job -
or had a job, I should say, on the farm.
We were going farming.
And that was all there was to that.
Now I've got to stop and revamp it again for a while.
Now we're up to September the 11th, 1986.
This is about three weeks ago when I last spoke into this thing.
Well, anyway, to get back to the story,
Mac and I farmed that year.
We put in the crop.
We didn't have a very big crop.
It was kind of a dry year.
That was the summer of 1924, my first year of running the farm.
Mother was paying me $600 for the year and of course I had the use of the car as I needed it.
Everything went fine.
We got our crop in and we were ahead of everything and we had an early harvest.

When the harvest was finished, a buddy of mine and myself got in the car one Saturday, and we were going out for a little fun, and went down to Estevan, and the country was dry at that time, and so we landed in on the bootleggers, and having a drink in there when another friend of ours from our hometown came in and got talking. I asked what he's doing.
He said he's working with the power gang.
Well, I said, "My God, I said..."
get me a job," I said.
This was about, well, I'd say about the middle of September.
And he said, "Sure."
The boss was saying the other day that he could stand another man.
He's going away to university.
So that was on a weekend, and Monday night I had a phone call from him saying, come on down, go to work. So,
I had packed up my bag, which was the climbing tools and a few wrenches and a hammer, and I head for Estevan.
And go down there.
The wages were set, I think it was 83 cents an hour.
Paid my own room and board.
We were working 10 hours a day, six days a week.
However, I got on this job, and they were a very inexperienced crew.
None of them knew very much about building a line and
the foreman immediately recognized that I knew more than anybody around there, and he come to me and said, I should make you foreman of this crew.
I'm going away.
But he said, "I've already promised it to Jack, and I hate to go back on my word."
Well, I said, "I'll tell you, John, I just come down here to run a few dollars."
I said, "I'm farming, and I'll just be working here for a few months probably and back to the farm again.
So you just go ahead and leave things the way they are, and I'll continue to do my best.
Whenever I get ready to leave, I'll just leave, that's all."
Well, as things worked out then, it wasn't very long, and the superintendent from downtown, and he picked me out right away.
He said, "I'm going to let the district serviceman go and
put you in on service work."
I said, "My God, I don't even know how to read a meter.
All I know is how to build a line."
He said, "We'll teach you that." He said.
"Well," I said, "I might be leaving here in the spring.
And then you'll have to get somebody else."
"Well," he said, "that's okay.
We'll deal with that when it comes."
Well, anyway, I
I go on to service work, changing meters and servicing customers and reading meters and all the things that go along with service work.
I worked there all winter.
Yeah, I work all winter, and then when spring comes, I go back home -
that's right -
about the end of March, I go back home on the farm.
By that time, Mother and Bud had their fill of living in the city, living in Weyburn.
And Mother wanted to get back on the farm, so we just canceled our contract, and Mother went back on the farm.
In the meantime, I head back to Estevan to see if I can get a job.
And they had brought another crew in there, their building.
It had quite a lot of construction going on.
They're building a new power plant there, and they had brought another crew in.
So they put me on.
Oh, I go down and - that's right -
I go down, and I talk to the foreman.
I said, "What are my wages going to be?"
He said, "55 cents an hour."
I said, "Hell, I'm not working for 55 cents an hour."
I got 83 last fall when I left here.
"Well," he said, "I didn't set those wages.
That was the superintendent that set that."
"Well," I said, "I'll tell you.

I'll work here until he comes down and then I'll talk to him." "Well," he said, "When I saw your work and saw your work he said I right away told the superintendent you were worth more money than that and he finally said I'll give you 75 cents an hour. "Well,", I said, "From now on I'll just do 75 cents an hour worth of work every hour that you'll have to
put up with that until he gets down here."
Well, it was about a month later when he comes down.
In the meantime, this foreman had left and gone to one of the mines, and another foreman came in and took over.
And when the superintendent come down, I'm really up in the air.
I'm going to eat him out good.
So I heard he was in town.
He was looking for me, and I was looking for him.
He said, "Hey, Linden, I want to see you."
He said, "I want you to run the crew."
"Well," I said, "What's happened?"
He said, "Bill Wilson broke his leg this afternoon, and we've got to have somebody to run the crew, and I'm picking you."
He said, "Do you think you can run the crew?"
I said, "You think I can?"
He said, "yes, I do."
I said, "okay."
And he said, "Your wages will be 87 cents an hour."
Well, that's settled the wage argument right there.
I put that crew through the paces all summer.
I had eight men, and they were good men but
we really went through a lot of work.
We finished the job at Estevan and then moved up to - down near the Assiniboia area and did some work, then up to Craik and Davidson, worked there for a while, and then up to Biggar.
Now, Biggar is a town about 3,000.
We were totally rebuilding the distribution system in there.
And,
I had no problem at all.
Everything was going good.
When this man Wilson, who had broke his leg in the spring, was ready to come back to work, and Doboy's superintendent had come up to Biggar.
He said, "I want you to come back to Estevan and put Bill back in charge of the crew, and I want you to come back to Estevan.
I've got a job for you down there."
He said, "We're cutting the whole town over from 2,300 volts to 4,000 volts, and I want you to do the job."
Well, I went down there, and I had one man helping me, and I cut the whole town over, which had to be done in sections.
You cut one section after the other and kept crowding the higher voltage behind you and cutting off the lower voltage sections and cutting over all the transformers and everything as you went along.
It was a good experience.
I finished that job, and then he had to do an odd job around until January when an ad came in the paper that the Power Commission were hiring men.
And so I went to the superintendent.
I said, "Look, I'm about finished here."
I said, "Maybe I should apply for a job there."
He said - And I said, "What would it mean if I called them?"
"Well," he said, "They'll be here long after we're gone." he said,
"The government starts taking over, they'll take over."
So I came to Regina -
Oh, I wrote in, that's right.
I wrote in and asked for a job and forgot all about it.
I go out - out of town.
I had run into a pretty nice schoolmarmie out in the country, and I was out visiting her.
And I didn't know where she lived, and I got into this small town to phone and see where she was at.
While I was there, the telephone operator said to me, are you Lindy Achen?
I said, "Yes."
But she said, "They've been trying to get a hold of you from Estevan."
Well, I hadn't told a soul that I was going out that night.
But the telephone operators in those days, it wasn't automatic like it is today.
Telephone operators kept very close track of what was going on when a long distance come for me from Regina.
They had heard me phone this girl that I was coming out, and so they phoned the operator there and said, if Lindy Achen is in the district there in any place, trace him down.
And they had swum out to where this girl was teaching, and I wasn't there.
However, I get on the phone.
I phoned back to Regina to this number, and here's the superintendent up in Regina here.
He wants me to come in in two days.
They're hiring five men, and I was one of the five to pick them up.
And they put us through a fast two-day course of the book work and everything, and then put our...

service work and I was posted to Saskatoon which was a Class B district my wages were $155 a month. I had my own car and I used my car on the job and I got 10 cents a mile for driving for using my own vehicle.
Well, I was up there, many things happened.
The superintendent was a young green superintendent.
He didn't know anything about power lines, just out of university.
So he relied a lot on me for information when there was trouble and whatnot.
And I worked there about four months.
He was pretty impressed with the job that I was doing.
And the man that they had sent to the
class one district at Rosetown with a university graduate.
But he got down there and he really got things mixed up good.
So my superintendent said to me one day, how'd you like to be transferred to Rosetown?
I said, "If I get his wages, yes, I will."
So I go down there and I knew the fellow.
He had got into the bottle quite a bit and wasn't paying attention to his job and he had everything mixed up.
Well, I took over Rosetown and more or less took charge of things.
Well, I had my district there.
I had eight towns to look after.
And they'd all been built in the wintertime and everything was hanging crooked and leaning crooked.
So as I had spare time, I'd hire a kid and help me and get this all straightened out.
And then one day I got a letter from the superintendent eating me out as the superintendent in Regina, eating me out for
hiring help without authority.
I was paying these kids 30 cents an hour.
And - doing a good job.
We got a lot of work done.
Then when he saw what a job I had been doing in the towns, then he turned around and made all the other district men do what I had been doing.
Well, along with that and a few other things that happened, I was young and, what would you say, young and free and wild.
I had money in the bank.
I had bought a brand new car while I was there.
So I said I wanted a leave of absence. That was about the end of September.
Wanted leave of absence for three months.
"Oh, we don't have such a thing in the books."
"Well," I said, "I'll take it off and take my own chances of getting back to work when I get back."
So, okay, I check out,
when I came to Regina, I stopped in the head off at the head superintendent.
He said, "Boy, you've done an ace-high job for us."
He says, "We have nothing but praise for the work that you've done.
But we can't guarantee you that you'll have a job when you come back in the spring, because things are getting tougher all the time."
This is in the spring of 1931.
No, in the fall of 1931.
And anyway...
The way I go, I go down to Halbrite - Midale.
Mac was living in Midale.
Brother, Mac was living in Midale.
Midale wanted to put in a power plant.
And they said, "You're just the guy to come down here and put in our distribution system for us."
I said, "I want to take my mother down to Iowa for a month first."
Well, that would be plenty of time.
You get back here in November, that would be plenty of time to do so.
Mother and I went to Iowa, and I had a whooping time down in Iowa with all the relatives.
And I had been down there on a visit twice before that, so I knew a lot of people.
When I come back, I want to work in Iowa, or in Midale and put in the distribution system.
Got that done, done.
All the houses and stores had to be wired, so my brother Mac and I spent the winter there, wiring up these houses and stores and making money all the time, you know.
Well then, the job is finished, and it's spring.
So about in May, I wander up to Regina here,
going to the power commission.
See if I can get a job.
"Get a job?
We're still laying off men. Things are that tough that we're still laying off men. We're not hiring men."
So - and the superintendent got nasty with me then.
The guy that had praised me so much when I checked out, he said, "Oh, that's the kind of a guy you are.
You go and pick an inside job for the winter, and now you come back in the summer and want a job."
Well, he'll never know how close I come to climbing over his desk and punching him in the nose, but I held myself and walked out of his office.
And I went back to the farm and loafed around all summer, having a good time, helping on the farm, of course.
But that fall, we didn't have much harvest.
And we got our own work done up early with our own help, just Bud and my cousin Tad and a few of us.
We put our little thrashing outfit to work, and we got the harvests off of them.
There were others around the country that wanted to get thrashing done, so Bud hired me and the separator outfit.
These guys would have a tractor, and I'd run the outfits.
And for that,
He was getting $4 an hour or $40 a day for this separator and myself and the farmer who furnished the tractor, and I would run both ends.
I don't know what he was paying me, a couple bucks a day.
Oh, he must have gotten more than $4 a day, because he was paying me $2 or $3 a day, and he probably got $7 or $8 for the outfit.
And I worked there, and maybe three weeks doing that, and fall was in, and I got in the car.
In the meantime, in June, Dominion Electric knew I wasn't working.
They phoned me.
I went down there and worked for two weeks.
They had the job they wanted done, and I went to work for two weeks.
So I went back in the fall, and Dubois says, I can put you to work.
He says, we've got some work here.
So I worked in Estevan for about the month of October with the gang that was there.
And then they were putting in a power plant out of Hopkins, which was 18 miles from the nearest power plant, so they put in another isolated plant, a little diesel plant.
And he said, "I want you to go out there and build a distribution system."
And I knew the fellow was going to go out there and put the plant in.
They were setting up a small company, and he was leaving them.
He was a steam engineer from Estevan, and he was leaving them and going out to set up this small plant to get on his own.
I go out there, and I put in the distribution system.
Oh, it was a beautiful fall.
We went out there in November.
There was snow on the ground, but it all went away, and it was like summer until the middle of December.
And they got the plant going about the 17th of December.
Well, in that winter, I wired houses and

stores in that town all winter. So I was busy making a living and that was about all. Then the next spring my old company Dominion Electric Dubois hired me to go into Gull Lake. There was some bad wiring jobs and they couldn't meter it properly in three or four places involving business places. So I went in and I straightened all that out. And
when I got that finished, he took me up to Biggar, Saskatchewan.
It's up by Saskatoon.
There I did the hospital and three, four stores that had messes in the wiring and got it all straightened out.
And then I come back to Gull Lake and I started overhauling the distribution system.
And I worked there about three months in the summer.
All by myself.
I would have a helper for a half a day and that was all.
The rest of the time I was by myself,
and doing jobs that normally you'd put two or three men at.
When I finished that, I went down to the town of Eastend, which had another small decent plot, and down by Shaunavon.
And I worked there for a month, and all of a sudden, New York, who owned the Dominion Electric Power, said, "Cut all spending.
That's the end of it.
We can't spend any more money this year."
So I get a phone call to shut her down right then.
I was one-man crew again.
I could shut her down pretty easy.
But while I was there, I lined up a lot of wiring jobs, so I wired for three months - for a month in Eastend.
I had enough work to carry me on for a month, and then I went back up to Tompkins, where this friend of mine was running the power plant, and...
I tucked in there for the winter.
You'd get room and board for about, oh, a dollar and a half a day is what it would cost you to live.
And played cards and did some wiring around there that winter, odd jobs.
And then in the spring,
Oh yeah, then the next year I went out and I ran a crew for the Dominion Electric.
He hired me, Dominion Electric hired me back here and I went down.
I overhauled the power line from Shaunavon to Assiniboia for 120 miles.
We climbed every pole on that line and repaired everything that needed repairing.
I had three men and myself.
And we worked hard.
It was a hot, dry summer, but we kept on plugging away, working six and seven days a week.
And what was I getting then?
I was getting, I think, 50 cents an hour.
We worked a nine-hour day, so I'd make it $4.50 a day, and you could get room and board for a couple dollars a day.
I worked there until fall -
Yeah, we haven't finished the job when they shut her down again.
They got out of money.
So back to Tompkins I go again.
The pal was running a power plant.
But this time he was getting pretty dissatisfied.
Sitting in there watching that little 40-horse diesel chug, chug, chug, chug away.
Turning over 300 revolutions a minute.
And he was having trouble with it.
But he was not a good operator.
He...
He never fixed anything before it broke, put it that way.
So I saw he was dissatisfied and I asked him one day if he wanted to sell out.
Yeah, he said I'd be glad to get the hell out of here.
So I phoned Estevan and told him about it and they were glad to get rid of him.
They weren't happy with him as an operator.

So they said, "You go ahead and make the deal and tell us what you've worked up," and I told them and they gave me the money to swing the deal. I traded my car in. I borrowed 600 from my mother and here I was president of Tompkins Electric. Well, I was without transportation so in Dominion Electric my old superintendent saw that he said, "We've got to have some trucks standing down in the yard there.
It's not in good running order, but you'll make it work."
So they sent that up, and I had that half-ton truck when I was running the power plant.
Well, then my brother Mac was out of work, and I said, "I'm sorry."
He'd come up, he and his wife, they moved up.
And then right away, he learned how to run the power plant.
Well, then I was free to go out and do wiring and
I did odd jobs for Dominion Electric around the country. They whistled me out to do a job.
I went to different towns, would work a month at a time.
It wasn't making any money.
My salary when I run the power plant was $75 a month.
And that's all the plant could afford.
It just barely paid its way, paid interest and fuel and wages at $75 a month.
