Well, I operated that for about a year and a half and then the company saw fit to build a line from Gull Lake to Tompkins and shut that down. Now we're into 1930 we're into 1930 - yeah I operated the plant '35, '36 into the spring of '37 and uh -

The next time he come back a couple weeks later he said, "I can't give you that job." He said, "The diesel superintendent's son-in-law is a contractor and he's been out of work for three years. He wants to give it to him, but he said, 'You can work on the job; you'll be his foreman while he's here." Well, I went out -
I didn't do a hell of a lot of work.
He had a crew of about 12 men, bossing around.
He wasn't much of an operator, I found that out.
When we got that -
Oh yeah, in the meantime, this fellow that I took the plant over from had gone up to Dauphin, Manitoba.
He got a job running a steam plant up there.
While he was there, he found out there was a little town, Winnipegosis Manitoba that needed a power plant.
And he right away makes a deal with them to put in power, and he didn't have any money.
So he comes hollering to me.
He said, "Can you help me out?"
He said, "I'm in trouble.
I have a franchise here, that can't fulfill it."
Well, hell, I didn't have any money.
So I phoned down to the super - not the super - the general manager of the Dominion Electric, told him the story, and "Oh," he said, "Well, we'll pull him out."
So they go up and take a look at it.
They take over the franchise.
And then they said, "We're going to put you up there.
There's no wiring in the town.
There isn't a power of any kind in the town.
There's about 120 places to wire up and put you up there to wire up the town."
Not to build the power lines.
This son-in-law deal, he was going to build the power lines.
But I was going to wire the buildings.
So I went up there in June.
And my brother Mac followed me up in about two weeks.
Everything was closed down in Tompkins.
And they were moving that plant up to Winnipegosis.
Well, I had nothing to do with the plant.
All I did was wire the stores and houses.
My brother Mac and I worked up there. In the month of September,
they needed me down at Estevan for a month.
My brother, Mac had a helper and kept wiring.
I went down to Estevan for distribution, the mines and everything, for a month.
While that guy went out and looked over another job, he was going into a different field, and the guy that's the operator down there.
So then I come back up to Winnipegosis, and I wound up there on Armistice Day.
And I walked out of the - out of there with everything cut and dried.
I had a little box full of material left, and I sent it back to the wholesaler at Winnipeg, and they paid me back 75% on the dollar, what it was worth.
I walked out with $1,200 in my pocket after living all summer and buying a second-handed V8 Ford, which turned out to be a kind of a lemon after a while.
Well, when I went back to Estevan, I was back to Estevan by the end of, well, the middle of November.
Then I took over the distribution, operating, servicing all the mines and the town of Estevan.
That was in the fall of '37.
In the spring of '38, I went out and relieved out at Gravelbourg in Lafleche while they changed operators out there.
And in the fall of '38, I was sent out to Shaunavon and Gravelbourg to calibrate meters.
Ohy, I got about two weeks on that and then come back to Estevan again.
And then I kept on working in Estevan until
I was married.
Oh, yeah, the war broke out in the fall of '39.
And they were building an airport in Estevan, around '42, and ... one hell of a place to work.
And I worked there '38, '39.
Fall of '40, we were married.
And there's '41, '42, '43, '44, on call, 24 hours a day, 30 days a month.
But when I got holidays - I don't think I took holidays off.
You know, maybe take off the out day to go hunting.
And that was all.
And then in - to get back to the story again, by this time, Dominion Electric were getting pretty big up in the Peace River country.
The superintendent was spending a lot of time up there.
So I pretty well had charge of everything in Estevan - distribution.
And they blew up a big generator down at the power plant.
And they tore it all apart.
Got it opened up so you could see what had burned out on the wiring end of it.
And Schlosser, the general manager, he didn't know how to put that into words to send a telegram to New York to tell them what had happened and what was necessary.
So he asked me to go down and take a look at it.
And then we went back up to the office and drafted a telegram to New York to tell them what was involved in that.
It was going to be a lengthy lay - shutdown.

And then after we had sent the telegram, this was about 9 o'clock at night, we were sitting in the office, and I said, "I see where the Power Corporation, or the Power Commission at that time, are advertising for a bunch of line they're going to build.
In fact, they're building
tearing down airport lines at Midale and Halbrite, all in through there, and building up into power lines in those small towns."
I said, "I got a damn good notion to bid on that."
I said, "I know how to build power lines if anybody does."
He said, "Why don't you?"
He said, "I'll give you a leave of absence.
If you don't get any work", he said, "you can come back to work with me."
Well, I went and looked the jobs over, came into Regina, ...
Contracts.
And, two days later they were opened.
There were four bidders, and they were all older men that had been contracting power lines when the Depression came. It was at
And now, 15 years later, they're still sitting there with all their tools and equipment, and nothing had happened for 15 years, you might say.
And these guys were all in there, bidding, and I'm in their bidding.
I'm the kid bidder.
These fellas are all 15, 20 years older than I am.
Well, they opened up the tenders, and it was one of the most amazing things that you ever saw.

The tenders ranged - now this is in 1945 dollars. You can multiply that by five for today. The first tender opened up, $11,000; the next one, $11,500; the next one, $11,075, and then mine was the last one, $10,050. Well here I was just
right in the ballpark.
Those guys, I'll bet you any money that they went to that graves thinking that I had an inside tip-off.
But the amazing part of it was that we all bid it in such close range, and it was really a complicated job.
You had to take up thousands and thousands of feet of underground cable that was buried two feet under the ground, and you were supposed to take it up without damaging it,

and, tearing down all these old power lines, and there's telephones and power and everything mixed up. And then, using that same material or what was usable, build a power line - well in one case, from Macoun to Halbrite. And we were all bidding that close. It was almost impossible to think that four men could sit down individually and come up within a thousand dollars from top to bottom. Anyway,
away I go.
Then a little later, they bid on a line from Stoughton to Estevan.
This was the first high voltage, suspension-type line in the province.
None of us had ever bid on it.
And only the one guy, that was this son-in-law, again, as I referred to him to,
and myself was the only two bidders.
And he bid $600 a mile, and I bid $550.
Today, you pay about $3,000 a mile for that same kind of a line.
Anyway, I got it.
Now I'm in business.
That line wasn't to be started until the next year.
Well, I worked -
I tore down the airports, and I got the line going from Halbrite to Midale,
and then part of it to Macoun.
Well, then again, I started hiring.
I was paying another guy to wire,
and I kept him going all winter in Midale.
I went to Estevan and really didn't do much of anything.
Sort of laid around all winter,
and getting ready for the next spring.
And then the next spring is when I had to wrestle some equipment and get going on my own.
That fall, I hired -
No, I went and I bought a half-ton truck. Thats ... Bought an old
half-ton six, seven years old, and had been on a cream route. Pretty well done in, but it served my purpose.
When I need the heavier truck, I rent it. Rented a man with a truck.
So then I - oh yeah, that fall, that's right, there's some army trucks came for sale up in Regina.
And I come up and I bought a,
what they call a 1500 weight, that's about two tons, and a gun carrier.
That was a useful thing.
Four-wheel drive.
You could go anyplace with it.
I put a boom on it for setting poles.
And then the next spring I bought another truck.
In fact, I bought two trucks the next spring.
But then I started and got going.
And, uh...
Oh, yeah, I forgot.
The Fall of '45, when I finished the airport, I came into Regina here and did some work.
They were starting up a new building development, the first one in Regina for 20 years, I guess, and this was for returned soldiers.
And after I put the distribution system in there, that was in December and January, and then in February I went up to Saskatoon, they had the same thing up there.
And I went up and did that one, and then I was getting into it towards spring, and
Then the old Dominion Electric had some work to do at Craik and Davidson, so I went down there and I worked there until the spring break up.
And then I went back to Estevan.
I was still living in Estevan at this time.
Went back to Estevan and started on my power line job, on my contract.
And really never looked back from that day on.
It was work.
There was always work ahead.
I was never out of work.
Well, you'd lay off in the wintertime.
Lay off Christmas until maybe March or April and then start up small by the time the snow was all gone and the fields were dry.
You would build up your full crew and get going.
So it was an interesting

part of the contracting with that start start up period. I started out with men - I didn't have a man that knew anything about line work, and I finally got a hold of a alignment a little fella he went about five foot six, but a hell of a good lineman. Hard worker, but he could hardly write his own name. He could read enough to get by. And then I - the next thing I -
In about a month's time, I picked up Art Miller.
Art came, he was sent to me by - he had come to the power here to get work, and they didn't need men, they sent him to me.
And he had climbed some telephone poles in his day, but he wasn't much, you couldn't rate him as a lineman, but he was real good with trucks.
He had been a driver in the Army, so I had a good man to move our trucks and to look after them and see that they were running.
And then from there on, you hire nothing but kids.
You know, young guys, 18 years old.
They knew nothing from nothing, and you train them.
But I saw that these young guys, at that time you could
give them a kick in the rear end if they need and pat them on the shoulder the next half hour and tell them what a good job they were doing.
And they worked for you, I'll tell you that.
I've turned out a lot of good men and they come back to me after, "Oh you were tough, but you sure taught us a lot while we worked for you." And,
it's one of those things that you just keep going.
That was one of the biggest thrills, was going out with a bunch of young guys and training them.
And as your crew got bigger and you couldn't be out with them all the time, I always missed that.
I always loved working with a bunch of men.
And I knew how to get work out of them.
Once in a while you'd have a guy that's a failure and wouldn't come up to standard.
You'd have to let him go.
There was an awful lot of shovel work in those days.
You'd break a guy in and get him hardened in.
You hire a guy, come out, never done any work in his life, and give him a shovel and start telling him to dig an anchor hole, and he thinks it's an impossibility.
And you spend an hour with him and show him how to use the tools, and in a couple of days you've got a good man.
He hardens in fast, he gets tired but if he sits down for five minutes, he's up and going again.
And that's just about...
a lost art today, there is no more shovel work.
You hire a guy today and you've got to put him on a big machine.
You're almost afraid to him maneuver
in case he smashes it up or there's something wrong.
We're talking now the end of 1945 and 1946, the '46 -
I built that big line to Stoughton in '46.
Besides doing many odd jobs, I built the lines out of Regina down to the new radio stations six, eight miles south.
And I did some work for the city at that time.
And I finished that line on the 9th of December, that big line.
That was an experience.
And I had to do everything on my own.
You had no place to go to ask for information.
You had to rig up all your own equipment and everything.
But we'd come out of it, made some money on it.
All my diggers, they went on the strike when they were about a third of the way up that line.
They kept asking for more money.
They wanted peace work.
They kept asking for more money.
One day I heard there was a boring machine in Winnipeg and I jumped on the train and I went in and I bought a brand new boring machine.
It was the second one to come into Saskatchewan.
And then from there on I had the diggers behind the ball and we could dig all the holes we needed with that one machine.
And we finished that job, yeah, on the 9th of December.
I remember that so well.
And then I laid  up - for the winter.
Didn't do anything all winter
until the next spring.
And then we had to tender about early April we tentered.
I got some work building a line from Weyburn to Radville.
That's about 30 miles.
We start on that - that was early spring
we start on that.
When we finish that, we did some farms around Weyburn.
That was in fourty - '46...
No, that was '47, that's right, '47.
And after that, I got a job up at, a big job up at the Rosetown area.
I got a hundred and some miles with eight or ten towns to build out.
That was the summer's work.
And I built up a crew and got them going, and then the power come along, they want me to go up to Yorkton area.
There were six towns up there and about 50 miles of line to build.
And that was really stretching myself a bit when I took that on.
I had to leave my crew at Rosetown and go over and start up another crew out of the blue at Yorkton.
And that was going through bush country.
That was a new experience for us.
I had to cut the swath through the bush where you're going to build the line.
You had to cut a 25-foot swath through these trees.
And they were trees up to 40 feet high.
Well, you could get a lot of labor up in that country.
Put guys in there with axes.
Get a good ax man.
It was amazing what he could do in a day.
And not only chop them down, but you had to pile them up neatly and everything.
One of the biggest things that
And that job was bidding, I don't know how to bid, how do you estimate what it's going to cost to ...
However, I let Art Miller and the crew back at Rosetown, and I stuck with this job up there.
By Christmas time, I had her finished.
On the 23rd of December, I came home. When I finally got out of ...
where we were headquartered,
it was about 11 o'clock at night, and I drove home.
I got home about 3 o'clock in the morning.
It was wintertime, and the heater in my car quit.
I thought I'd freeze my feet before I got home.
And right now, I'm going to pause for a moment.
I had to go get something to wet my throat, sitting here talking for a half hour without any let-up.
And as the funny says - Fools Hesitate, Funny McGee. Well, that was the end of 1947.
The spring of 1948, I could see then things were going to move ahead, and I had to make some definite moves.
So I decided to move to Regina.
Phyllis, and I come up to Regina, and we looked around for a couple of days and found a house

and moved up here. It was still spring there was a lot of snow I think we come up about - maybe about the end of march or the middle of april sometime in there we moved to Regina. Now, I didn't have any work for '48 we had to tender. Well I still had a little work to finish up at Rosetown and a little
to finish up at Yorkton from the fall of '47.
Odds and ends to do. So,
I was able to keep my - as soon as spring broke, I got my key men up there and finished that.
And then of course the old tendering went in again, about the end of April.
And I have to really think hard now to think of where I worked in '48. In '48...
I did a job from Vanguard to Aneroid, rebuilt a substation in there.
Now I'll have to pause.
Yes, now it comes to me.
I did an awful lot of work south of Swift Current in the Mennonite areas.
There's all Mennonite farms around there.
And we did, we must have worked in there until well after - oh yeah, it was well after harvest.
It was in the fall by the time we finished up there.
Then I come to the city.
By this time the city is expanding and I set up a whole big area of poles in the city here.
That was the start of our work in the city which, by the way, carried on for 10 years that I had men working in the city here the year-round.

Not a big crew. A crew about six men. mostly. Five, six men, boring machine, pole truck, and another two-ton truck. Everything was paid for by the hour. It was sort of a meal ticket as far as I was concerned because
It was right in the city here, and I could - well, they supervised it.
I didn't have to supervise it.
All I had to do was send in the bill every two weeks.
And I had steady rental for my equipment.
I didn't make much on payroll.
I made 10% on payroll.
We had a set rate for equipment.
And, incidentally, you talk about inflation.
In the 10 years
I never raised the rental on my equipment.
I was happy with what I was getting, and I wasn't greedy.
And naturally, the city was happy, and it was - everybody was happy with the arrangement.
And it was all made over a telephone call between the city Light and Power Superintendent, whom I had known for some years, and myself.
And he asked me what I wanted, and I told him, he said, "Send me a letter outlining what you said just now."
And I did that, and about eight years later, he said, "Hey, Lindy," he said, "What kind of a deal did you and I make over the phone that day?"
And I told him, he said, "Have you got a copy of the letter you owed me?"
He said, "I can't find anything in here."
I said, "Yes, I have, and I'll dig it up."
He said, "Send me a copy."
He said, "There's been some inquiries around here about that."
And he said, "I...
I want to have the answers." But that's the kind of working relationship we had.
And I want to go back now to 1947, when I was running the two crews.
I had about 60 men working for me on the two areas.
There was one time that I didn't get home for six weeks.
Phyllis was in Regina here
with the kids.
We had, at that time, four, maybe five, maybe Janie was a baby by that time.
And I had, she said, "Can't you come?"
I said, "I can't leave!
I've got so darn much out here, so much to look after, I just can't leave."
I was working seven days a week.
And about, well, from six in the morning until about ten at night, I was on the go every day.
The only time you got a little spell off is if it rained, that
you had to stay inside.
And then I'd try to catch up on my book work.
And I was making, writing out payrolls out in the country.
I had a bookkeeper in town here, but it was unbelievable.
And then I to invoice.
I had to invoice.
I'd send that in to the bookkeeper and tell him to invoice it.
To keep the cash flow right, you had to keep on going.
It was a hectic time, I want to tell you.
But it was one of those things that went with the job.
I enjoyed it.
It was hard on Phyllis, but she got by some way or other.
Well, that was '48.
Now I got back, I covered '48.
Yeah, we finished up in the city here that fall.
We started out in the... in the...
near Yorkton, up in the northeast again.
We got an early job there, and I more or less left Art Miller in charge of it.
Only a small job, about 30 miles - 25 miles of line, I guess.
And after it was built, the head superintendent came out there to look at it, and he and I drove over.
Never said a word.
Drove the full length of the line, and when we got over there to the end of it, I said, "Well, what did you see wrong?"
He said, "This one thing."
He named one thing that he saw wrong.
I said, "If that's all you saw wrong.
I'm glad."
I said, "That line looks like a rank amateur built it."
Well, he said, "What did you see wrong?"
"Oh, cripes," I said, "I could name a dozen things that I spotted driving across there that wasn't right the way I - it would have been if I was there."
"Well," he said, "I didn't see it."
"Well, it wasn't serious," I said, "but it wasn't right."
Well, anyway, that was the start of '49. Now I have -
Oh, yeah, then we got the job building another chunk of the high-tension line from Estevan
that would extend right through to Yorkton.
We got the piece from south of Melville up to Yorkton.
That was another about 50 miles of high-tension line.
Well, you didn't build that so fast, you know.
I worked - I worked on that right through until we got snowbound in December.
It stormed so much that we just couldn't move anymore.
We plowed out -
rigged up a snow plow, and the plow road opened one day, and the next day it was filled in again.
So finally I talked to the superintendent.
"Well," he said, "Basically, we're not ready for that line until next spring."
He said, "You better shut her down."
So I shut her down for the winter.
That was the winter of, yeah, '49.
And in '50, we started out again -
- the last time. This machine may not erase as you go along, as my old one did. I could just play over the tape and it would erase what was on previously -
...outside of Yorkton. Then moved down to Shaunavon -

...Alberta about the end of harvest and we worked up there. We did some highline move where they're building a high - it was up at the Vermilion and Lloydminster area, They were moving a highway - or widening a highway, and we moved some line for that. And then we put in a - a section of farm land - farm line there, about 50 miles of farm line.
And we were on that until about Christmas time when we wound up and then moved back into town here.
Then while we were here, the city had a bunch of line they wanted built and we worked on it through the winter with a - oh, a sizable force and set all several hundred poles for them. That was the -
Yeah, the windup of '49 and into the spring of '50.
Then in '50, as I started on that, I said - yes -
We built the piece out of Yorkton and then down to Shaunavon and then back up where?
I have to pause.
Oh, yes, we'd come back up and build a line from Montmartre to Kipling, which was about 50 or 60 miles, and built two or three towns along with that.
That pretty well wound up our season.
We had a real good year.
And then one of the other contractors was behind, and the superintendent come along and he said, "I want you to go and take some of his line."
And I said, "I don't want to.
I'm finished.
I've had a good year."
I said, "I don't want to go taking something away from him and create an enemy."
And at that, he took me down with him, showed me, oh, it was about 100 miles out of Regina, showed me what he wanted done.

And he went into the, this guy was running a camp, a small camp, and he went into his caboose where he was working, and he stayed there for three hours, trying to sell him that he couldn't handle that work and it had to be done before Christmas.
And he wanted to give it over to me, and I sat out in the car all that while.
I didn't know if they had killed each other in there or what.
I stayed in the car, and he finally came out.
And he said, "I couldn't convince the old man that you were going to do it anyway." he said.
I said, "I don't want to do it."
He said, "I'm commissioning you to do it."
He said, "and that's all there is to it.
You're going to go and do it."
So I sort of had to get back into gear again and go out and do this work.
And the weather stayed good.
Then on top of that, the -
another project came up just outside of Regina, and a bunch of farms just south of Regina here, and we went out and did those.
And that wound her up to '50 - '51. We went up north in the Rosetown area and we had a big job up there, a lot of three-phase transmission line and about probably
60 or 70 farms to do.

And then down in the Ogema - Shaunavon area, yeah, Fife Lake, and in that area we did a bunch of towns there in '50, '51, we were in the Ogema–Bengough, and in through there we did 60 or 70 miles of line in a bunch of towns.
Again, working about 30, 40 men all the time.
And then just about then, yeah.
Then in '52, the farms started coming in.
Then we really swung into the farm building in '52, '53.
And, we geared up.
We were building five miles of line every day.
That was the pace we were hitting in '53, '54, '55
kept up that pace,
'56, we did more than ever.
I think we did about 600 farms in '56.
Then that was pretty well getting near the end of it, of the farm phase.
But all this while, don't forget, I still had a crew working in the city.
And sometimes, you know, four or five men sometimes we'd have to boost that up.
We'd get - They'd have some extra work and we'd have to boost up and get
eight or ten men working. So -
Oh, I didn't relate in '53.
Yes, I incorporated in 1953.
Before that, I was just L. H. Achen.
In 1953, I incorporated.
I'd been going eight years then, and my insurance man said I should be incorporated in case something happens - something disastrous happens,
you better be incorporated.
So I incorporated, and that year I bought property on Ottawa Street and built a shop.
And from there on, it seemed most of my work was sitting behind a desk, and that more or less took all the joy out of it.
We were out building all these lines in from - starting about '52 is when the farm program really started.
And we were punching through, I think, at '52, about 350 farms, and then we jumped up to 500 or better every year for '53, '54, '55, '56.
Then in '57, it started tapering down.
In '58, tapered down some more.
More guys were getting into it, and the government were encouraging more to get into it because it cheapened the price.
Guys were going in, bidding, and one year and they'd be out of it.
They'd be on their fanny.
And the head superintendent seemed to think that that's the only way he could get it done cheap.
We never raised our prices.
We got so much a pole, so much an anchor, so much a transformer, from 1952 through to 1957.
And then they opened 'er wide up.
They said, "We're going to start tendering everything."
The bigger contractors, those that could build, there were just two of us, could build 500 farms a year.
We are set on that price of $13 a pole, $10 an anchor, and $30 a transformer.
Get out and go to it.
Do all you could.
When they opened their wide up in '58,
Well, then these little one-horse guys, they come in there, and they started bidding $8, $10 a pole.
'58, I never got any work.
Just sat there; was outbid.
I said, "No, I won't bid below what it cost me to do it with a profit."
And so when the work was all let out, I was sitting there, I didn't have any work.
I said, "Well, we'll see how many fellows know Lindy Achen." I said.
In the meantime, I forgot to mention, this is when we started doing
foundation piling in the city here.
And that was just a sort of a sideline that we were doing.
When we'd get short on work, we'd go out and bid on -
there's a plane coming over -
We'd bid on foundation jobs, enough -
You never made much on them.
It was very tough bidding, but you kept your crew working, kept everybody busy.
That was a big thing.
Well, in the meantime, I hadn't gone through all the mechanics of this thing.
I'd gone from one boring machine up to five boring machines.
When we were building 500 farms a year, we had to have
two machines going steady in the country and sometimes three.
And we also always had to have a machine in town here to take care of the city work.
And then we developed a machine that could go down 30 feet for foundation boring.
So by this time, I pretty well had to keep one man in the shop all the time.
Well, after '57, I kept Art Miller in the shop to more or less
look after the guys out in the country to keep their equipment going and everything.
And he didn't go out and run any crews or anything.
He just stayed in the shop and kept things rolling.
And this was all very interesting, you know, to see the thing grow.
In '55, when I had my heart attack, you know, I was right out of it.
I could go down and sign checks, and that was about it.
Thank goodness I had Art trained well enough at that time that he could keep everything under control.
And while he'd always come to me to ask me what should I do, what should I do, I'd have a hard time convincing him, well, do what you think, or else give him an answer of what he should do.
That was all part of the business that I went through.
And I wasn't - In '55 and '56
I was sort of hanging on the ropes, and those were big years.
That's when we turned out some of our best production.
Well, anyway, to get back to '58, when I was bid right out, didn't have any work.
See how many guys know Linda Achen?
Well, as it was, I kept all my key men working.
One job would come, and another little job would come, and a lot of...
work would come where i'd have to go and do the layout work. Plants starting up and everything you had to go out lay out a substation for them, or a small distribution system.

The potash out at Kalium between here and Moose Jaw start up and we did all their distribution layout for - not only did the engineering as well as building the lines. And uh that lasted for I guess it's still going today. That
started about 1956 or 7, about '57.
And I think our outfit still does all their expansion work, which is an ongoing thing.
It never stops.
They keep expanding all the time.
And I'm still talking back in '58.
Yeah, when the dust was settled in '58, well, I had my men, most of them were in Regina here.
We didn't have any country work, but
the jobs kept showing up and showing up here and there.
Keep busy all the time.
They were overhauling equipment.
We had everything in first-class shape.
And that fall, when the dust settled, I was taking my full salary, and everything else was paid up.
We didn't skimp on anything.
And when the season was finished, what did we have?
$8,000 profits.
And I gave Art $3,000, and I gave Bill, second in command $2,000, and I kept $3,000 for the company.
And I said, "Now we'll see what happens next year."
And the next year, in '59, things started picking up again.
And we kept going, '59 -
I'd have to think hard now to go back to - Oh, by that time, yeah, potash mines were coming in, and we were doing the distributions for them.
That was - those were big jobs, and we had to do the design and supply the material and everything.
So you got a chance to roll over some dollars and make a few cents on every dollar you rolled over.
And that went on.
Then in '60, '61,
'62, work started showing up again.
Bidding was tough.
You had to "pee in there sharp" to get a job.
But we got some good jobs and made some money.
And when was my last year?
'65, yeah.
'65, I got a real good job at Swift Current.
One of the biggest jobs I ever bid.
in one particular project, and should have really made some money on it.
And by God, it rained and rained and rained that summer down in Swift Current, where it never rains.
And we kept on losing time, falling behind. Go out -
Well, when you get a rain, you lose a day.
We had to keep the men, a crew of 30 men,
costing us $18 a day just for the camping outfit that we had in there, and we supplied the camp.
They fed them for $18 a day.
And, we had a supply a minimum of 18 men a day.
Well, when the dust was all settled, and that winter I said to Art, I said, "Okay, go over the books now, charge up that job, and tell me exactly what we made on it."
When he charged out everything, overhead and everything that goes against the job, we had made 6%, which was about as low as you can get.
I mean, that's kind of scraping the bottom of the barrel.
And that was my last year.
Then that was the end of the year.
Well, previous to that, I told them that I was getting out, that they had to take over
at the end of the year, or I'd put the outfit on the block and sell it.
We'll go into that now.
I'm going to take a rest.
I'm into my... Well, not so bad.
I've only talked about an hour and fifteen minutes so far, and I've gotten fairly condensed.
I had this stretched out originally to about three hours, but there was a lot of...
shall we say, incidentals in there that didn't mean anything.
So I'll cut off for now, and it's five o'clock right now.
Here we are again.
This is September the 16th.
I'll try and wind this - fill this tape out by reminiscing over some of the things that happened throughout the years of my contracting,
and maybe even back up to the years when I was working for a power company.
So every once in a while it may be a bit repetitious, but accept it for what it is.
I set up - I set up the -
the terms under which I wanted to sell the business to the men.
And I was not for selling to a partnership.
It had to be a company, and Art Miller was to be at the head of it, which meant he had to own fifty-one percent of the shares.
And there were Bill Zaharachuk, Von Kennedy, and George Jenny were coming in as minority partners.
And we all agreed to that.
They were all willing to just take a chunk.
They all knew Art and knew that he knew how to run the business, etc.
So they went ahead on that basis, and everything was going fine.
Things never looked any better for contracting.
The government had changed from the NDP to Liberals, and Liberals were for contracting out work rather than
doing it with government crews.
So when I sold out, I said to the boys, "It never looks so good," but I said, "I'm finished.
I'm burned out.
I'm getting out of here right now.
After 20 years," I said, "I've had it."
And on the basis of that, they took over.
I gave them a good deal.
I didn't hijack them, hold them up for it or anything.
I forget the exact terms of what they paid me for the iron and whatnot, but it was under $100,000.
And they were to keep me on a salary of $1,000 a month for two years.
That was sort of an unwinding period for me, and I was to work a few hours a day as -
as was necessary, and as he called me in.
I might mention that along with selling the business, I kept the property, the shop and the land, and they were renting it from me.
And again, I gave them a good deal of $500 a month.
And throughout the years, as inflation went along, well, I sort of pulled it up.
And I might add that at no time have we ever had a contract.
It was all just word of mouth.
And as taxes would go up, I'd come along, and I don't think I raised the rent on them for six, seven years, and inflation was setting in.
So I come along one day, and I said, "Art, we've got to have a little more money."
And, well, they said, "Whatever you think."
So
I raised it to $600 a month, and then another couple of years, I raised it to $700 a month, and then inflation really come in.
And throughout the 20 years, no, less than that, in 16 years, I had it up to $1,325 a month.
And now for the last two years, I've kept it at that on the account of it

uh I didn't want to lose them as tenants and there were a lot of empty buildings showing up around the city and I thought, "Well, if I jacked them up and they move out then I've got a vacant building." The taxes there are over six thousand dollars a year so i'm quite happy to have them as tenants and uh Art sold out as -
When Art, Bill, and Vaughn and George took over, everything was going fine.
And the first setback they had was when Vaughn Kennedy was electrocuted doing some work at the Moose Jaw Airport.
Well, Art picked up his shares.
He left his family well off.
He was well insured.
He had four children, and his family had never suffered.
And I'm sure he didn't, because when electricity hits you, you don't know what hits you.
Then the next thing, George Jenney, he had saved his money, and he bought a section of land south of the Assiniboia.
And he decided he had worked 20 years, and he had his land paid for.
So he said, "This has been a wonderful experience, I'm getting out."
So he sold his shares.
And the next thing happened in another year, Bill Zaharitzuk took a heart attack and died in the middle of the - well in the middle of winter, out shoveling snow one day, and he dropped over dead.
So Art was sitting there with the whole business back in his hands, and he sold out a share to Doug McGillivray, who had been working for him for about eight, ten years.
And I don't know how
how big a chunk Doug McGillivray took.

And then a couple of years later, Con Bandy, who had worked for us for some years and had gone with the Power Corporation, got tired working for the Power Corporation, he come back and he took - bought in, or was going to buy in and buy out shares as he saw fit, but he was going to buy in to the company. And he only stayed about a year and a half, and I don't know -
I've never seen him or I've never asked why he left, but anyway, he sold his shares back into the company.
So Art is back there now with Doug, and the two of them are running the outfit.
Art's been out in the country pushing a crew, stringing in high-voltage underground wire.
They plow it in with the big caterpillars and a plow.
And he's been out, and he said, "It's like a holiday to get out in the country where there are no telephones or anything, and all you have to do is have your mind on one particular job."
And Doug McGillivray takes care of the office end of it, and works as well, the same as Art did.
Art never just sat behind a desk.
In the 20 years he was there, he would do the most menial work there was to do around there.
He was always busy.
He never just sat behind the desk and watched the world roll by.
So they're going on that basis now, and they seem to be busy.
A lot of work.
What is it now?
I sold out in '65, '66, '67.
This is in '66 - yeah that's -
22 years they've been going.
And the company still goes.
There's still some of the old-timers around there.
Ernie Smith is still there as mechanic and machine operator.
The two Cubians are both retired, but they come in once in a while when they're short-handed and help out.
And there's always new men coming in, and right now they have no problem getting help.
There's so darn much help around, I guess it's a nuicance,
guys pounding at the door.
And I don't get down there very often to see them anymore.
It just seems every - every year less and less.
And they pay their rent every month on the dot, and they still -
Oh, yes, after my two years was up, they put me on a retainer because I had my electrician's journeyman license.
And there were jobs that they were doing that they had to have a journeyman electrician on the job.
And while I very seldom went out on the job, they always said, well, I've got an electrician.
And everybody knew the conditions and that was
if it was necessary, I would be out there helping.
And if it wasn't, I wouldn't be.
And there's less and less of that where they call me in less and less all the time.
I don't think I've
been back there more than once in a year.
If there's anything they have to see the inspection department about, I always go up and handle that for them.
So, that's the way it wound up.
Oh yes, I started to tell you, when the two years was up, they wanted me on the retainer, so they put me on. Art said, "What do you want to come
and, you know, that we can use you if we want you."
I said, "Oh, I don't know."
Well, he said, "I'll have a couple thousand dollars a year sound to you."
I said, "That's good."
Well, I've been on that couple thousand dollars a year for 20 years now, and I don't do anything for it anymore.
It's just sort of a bonus to me.
But they still have a journeyman electrician on their payroll, which I guess it's worth a couple thousand to them to have me
just for a backup in case it's necessary.
So that's the way it all wound up.

And when I think back through the years from about '31 when I started working for the Power Commission, and I mentioned that earlier, and then I only worked for them eight months, and I took a leave of absence and I never went back.

Well, there's so many stories I could tell, things that happened through those years, even after I started contracting, what you run into, different individuals, and you were dealing with them throughout the years.
And I saw all the, when they were hiring young engineers, that all of a sudden these guys on my project.

Leave them there for two or three months and then pull them off and send another guy out. And I told the construction superintendent one time I said, "Why do you do that? I get a guy out there and he becomes some good to me and then you pull him off and send out a greenhorn." Well, he said - Ted said, "I sent him out there to learn something and I expect you'll teach them." "Well," I said, "That's what I've been doing but," I said, "I feel
pretty good about that you sent them to my outfit, but at the same time it doesn't help me out as much as it could if I had one man steady all the time."
Ted Durnan was the man that I dealt with from 1945 until about 1960.
That would be 15 years.
And he and I have a very good relationship.
He trusted me and I trusted him.
I'd be out in the country and there'd be something to do that the blueprints didn't fit and he'd give me authority.
You go ahead and put it the way you think.
The way you think it should be and then I'll back you up on it.
We never had any arguments over anything that
that I ever did, or I'd go and do a job.
He said, "To do the job, he'd send me a bill for it."
Well, knowing him, I knew that he would thoroughly analyze the bill, and he didn't put anything in there that wasn't yours, that was for sure.
And we went through that for the 15 years that he was there.
Then, after he left, another younger engineer took over.
By that time, things had changed an awful lot

in the power business. Oh yes - and just about then the new General Manager came in and he said, "We're going to contract more. So there was a sort of a period of, shall we say, when contractors weren't looked on by the general employees of the corporation as a necessity.

The union got in awfully strong. And they were all union men and we were non-union and of course they didn't like that because we could always show them up on production. So you had a - sort of always had an under feeling when you went to get material from their warehouse, you could sort of notice guys dragging their feet a bit and and you'd say, "Well that guy isn't very happy with
giving us this material.
It isn't his.
It belongs to the government.
But he's dragging his feet wherever he can."
Or if you got on the phone, you could tell when you phoned for something whether the guy was for you or against you.
Basically, we had the main engineering staff were all for us.
They liked the contractors.
And they finally got to a point where they had to tell some of the staff to either -
"Get in there and do your job.
If I hear one more bad report on you, you might be demoted."
And that sort of straightens things out.
This is after Art took over.
In the 20 years that I was contracting, it was interesting to see the young fellows starting up and couldn't make it.
They'd run a year or two, and then they would be belly up.
And then the corporation would nurse along some more upstarts.
And this, they watched an outfit like ours operate, and said, oh, that's pretty simple.
But they didn't realize all the things that you had to look after when you run an outfit single-handed.
You've got to do the hiring, the firing,
the dickering for wages, the buying the equipment, keeping the equipment running, family troubles you had to deal with.
There were so many things turned up that you wouldn't believe what would happen in the course of a year.
And having my background and experience is what carried me through. It was -
I knew a lot about machinery.
I'd been brought up on the farm.
We had all kinds of machinery of every description.
I knew all about men.
I hired and fired men when I was 21 years old.
And from there on, I was always in charge of people.
When I was on the farm, I was always in charge.
After Dad died, when I was 21 years old, three years I spent there.
Then when I started in the power business inside of a year and a half in there, I was always in charge of men.
Always in charge, no matter whether it was one man, three men, five men, or ten men, or twenty men.
I was always the foreman on the crew.
That way you got to know men and you could deal with men.
You always had problems when you had men.
They were always guys giving, you know, agitators, one thing or another that you had to deal with.
However, no unions.
We never had any union problems.
Even to this day, the union was never able to work its way into Achen Construction.
They tried and tried and tried, but we had a sort of a family arrangement there where the employees got a lot of benefits that you couldn't give if you were unionized.
In other words, it's like George Jenny said.
He was here about a month ago.
He said,
"What made your outfit go is you kept a good bunch of men around you."
I said, "And for sure."
I said, "If a guy didn't have it,
he didn't stay around.
You guys would run him off."
I said, "I didn't have to run him off.
They'd just fade away."
I had a good basic crew of about 10 men, and you could build that up to 40 or 50 men and really have production because you had good leaders.
And that's what enough it needed.
In the course of the years,
that I - when I was contracting, I saw over 30 fellows start up and go down.
And there's only, in those years, only a couple of other outfits that started up and stayed in the business, and they never got very big.
And I go back to 1958 when the -
the corporation had a meeting.
They said, "Look, a guy that's contracting is going to have an office.
He's going to have a phone.
And somebody on that phone that knows what's going on, that if we phone out there and want information, we want to be able to talk to somebody that knows what's going on."
And I was the only one that had an office set up where there was somebody in that office that - always there that could answer

anything that come up no matter what it was. And they let the other guys get by. Some of them set up little one-horse offices but didn't have anybody in there to man it, so they were no better off but that was one of the things they asked for and never got. But I don't know to relate back - Oh, we had a contractors' union. We started that way back in about 1950.

And it was to get the contractors sort of pulling together because the Power Corporation used to push the individuals around. It was something unbelievable and we could - together we could sort of spell out, "Look, we don't like this. You can't do this to us. We want this in our contracts, we want that in our contracts." And, while it never got to be a strong
organization, it still served a purpose.
It got us together with the engineers that were in charge, and they sort of got our viewpoints.
We'd always get a little out of them, but never what we really wanted.
They took the attitude, we're the guys with the work, and you guys have to come and get it.
There wasn't much you could do about it excepting, as I say, get them to
reword the contract a little bit so that it didn't hammer you to pieces if something went wrong.
And that went on.
I was the first president.
I was in there for three years as president, and I said, "Better put somebody else in here to run this organization."
I said, "I've been three years - in here three years and
And I've done mine."
So then they started up that they'd put a new president in every other year.
And we were only eight or nine members. That's all we were.
And that's still going today.
That same Central Line Contractors Association is still operating today.
They get together once a year and get together with the power, talk things over to see how things are going.
And it looks as though it can go on forever.
Basically, the union have got so tough and so organized that it hampers the Power Corporation in anything they want to do.
They've got to see the union first.
I've had them call me out at Christmas time to do jobs.
When we all closed down and they were closed down, I said, "You've got 150 men out there.
What are you calling on me for?
A little one-horse outfit."
"Oh, God, if we were going to do that job, we'd have to advertise it first and then let them come in the seniority until we got the amount of men we want to do it.
By that time, you can have the job done."
Well, I did the job for them.
I said, "Look, you know what this is going to be working over Christmas?
There's about three days' work, and there's going to be about three days' holiday I'm going to have to pay.
Christmas Day, Boxing Day."

"Oh, that's all right you go ahead and do it send us the bill for it." When I sent in the bill the guy looked at it, he said, "I can't pay that!" He said. "I -" "Well," I said, "You're the guy told me that you would." "Oh no. No way can I stand that much overtime. If I send that in, God, my job might be on trial." Well, he's got me over the barrel so he sort of dictated the the terms. We'll take this off, we'll take that off and -
But what else could you do?
You were looking for work, and this was your bread and butter was the Power Corporation.
So you had to accept that, and they would take advantage of you occasionally.
They never gave you the long end of the stick.
They always took the long end of the stick because they were in the driver's seat, and I suppose maybe I would have been the same had I been in there.
And incidentally, I might mention that in 1948,
Ted Durnan, I was in Ted Durnan's office as the head construction superintendent.
He said, "How much would you take a year to come in and run our construction department?"
And I thought for a while.
I said, "$10,000 a year."
But there'll be no contracting.
If I come in, contracting's finished.
As long as it's contracting, I want to be a competitor.
I'll be out there and be in it.
"Hell!" he said, "I can't pay you $10,000 a year.
I only get $8,000."
I said, "I don't care what you get.
I figure I'm worth $10,000 a year to run your construction crew."
"Well, no," he said, "We can't do that."
So he took one of their other fellows, and nobody will ever know what that cost them to.
But I could have saved them
with my knowledge.
They had nobody that had the knowledge that I had in construction, or had the wide variety of experience.
He brought it up to me then. "Well," he said, "You've got a history of jumping around a lot."
"Well," I said, "I never left a job that I didn't better myself going to the next one."
And,I said, "I picked up an awful lot of experience in that time.
I worked for the Iowa Electric to start with.
That was only about
four months.
And from there I went, that was in Iowa, and then I went to South Dakota, and I worked for the Northern Light Power for about a year and a half.
And then I went into North Dakota, and I worked for the Montana-Dakota Power Company.
I was there about six months.
Then I went up into Canada, and I worked for the Dominion Electric Power for about
That's about 15, 16 years.
Not steady.
I was on and off.

But I worked in every possible capacity that you could work on on a job, including running diesel plants and calibrating meters and building switchboards and shooting trouble of every description, rewinding transformers.
I've got a hell of an experience, and all this time in charge of men, which was a great thing to be in charge."

So I pointed out to him, I said, "Look, with my experience," I said, "you haven't got anybody around here that can even come up with an itinerary that worked any place excepting for the Power Commission.
And you think that I wouldn't be worth that much money to run it all?"
"Oh," he said, "I can't - I couldn't consider that."
Well, the result was I was happy.
I kept on contracting, and -
and run out my 20 years when I said "That's it".
After I'd shut down one day and calculated that I had enough money to keep me for the rest of my life at the style of living that I was accustomed to.
Had I known what inflation was going to do, I'd have been afraid to retire.
However, the inflation sort of took care of itself because when I retired,

six percent was the interest and it never stopped going up and so my income kept on doubling as I went along and never - never suffered for want of money. Every year after I retired I always had more money than I took in so that kept on pyramiding and cost of living kept on pyramiding of course. So that all took care of itself it was like a

"index pension" is what it was like as far as I was concerned. And uh what'll we go back to now. Yes some of the years that I was contracting. I have to take a look and see if this machine is still going. Yes it's still going but I must be getting near the end of this side of the tape when that runs out
I may just wind up, and I don't know what else I would talk on on the other side.
There's so much detail, incidents that I could tell that happened throughout those years.
I had labor trouble, sure.
I had guys - they'd go on strike right out on the job, and I'd go out and settle it right there.
"Okay, what's your problem?
Let's get this thing - okay.
Get back to work."
"Well, we want five cents an hour or more salary."
"Okay, I'll give you five cents an hour or more salary.
Get going.
Don't stand around here."
I said, "This is costing money."
You could talk to them in that tone
,in those days, I'm talking back in 1946, up to 1950, I had a bunch go on strike up - way up north of Swift Current.
It was the first time

I ever had all my men staying in one place, and we got into a small village there, and we rented a big hall, and we got bunk beds and put them up in there, about 18 or 20 men, and that's when trouble started.

There were some young fellows in there that were students and, you know, didn't believe in drinking beer and having a lot of fun and raising hell at night, and it wasn't long, and there was trouble in camp, and
Art Miller and I were both in town here, and we got in the car and drove out there.
Didn't take me long to sort it out when I got out there, and I said, "Who's the ringleader here, and what's your problem?"
And he still - Well, I said, "Okay, you'll take charge of this wire stringing crew right now, and you better, bloddy well, do a job of it, or down the road you'll go, and no fooling about it."
And a couple of guys automatically quit -
left the crew and they quit.
And they went back to work, and they did a good summer's work for me.
That whole summer, that was, that happened in June.
And we had a hell of a year, that year; a big year. But everybody went to work and that was the end of it.
Then in about two years later, I - Bill Zaharitzuk was out at Swift Current with a small crew, and he had
four guys climbing poles out there.
One day, they take a notion.
They're climbing creosote poles, and they take a notion.
They want a dollar a day more for climbing black poles.
And Bill said, well, I'll phone the boss tonight.
Oh, no, we're going in to see them.
And they jump in a truck, and they come into my house in Regina here.
And I said, "What in the world are you guys doing here?"
"Oh, we're on strike.
We want more money."
I said, "Why didn't you phone in instead of coming in here?"
"Oh, no, we want to -"
Well, I said, "You're all fired right now.
If that's your attitude."
"Well, what are you going to do?
You've got 200 farms to build.
Who's going to string that wire tied in it?"
"Don't you worry about that." I said.
"Art Miller, Bill Zaharitzuk, and I will take care of that.
You fellas don't have to worry about it."
And down the road they went,
all four of them.
I used to call them the whiz kids.
They were a good bunch of workers, but they thought they were going to take control of things because they were the whiz kids.
Well, as it happened, they all scattered, went their own way.
One of them was my nephew, Don Achen, who was a hell of a good man.
If he'd have stayed with the outfit, I'm sure he would have gone right to the top.
Another one we hired, oh yeah, when they were all gone, Art said, "I want Eddie Vizcerna back."
He said, "He's a hell of a good kid.
He's only 19 years old, but he said he's been my pet ever since he come here."
"Well," I said, "You go and hire him."
So Eddie Vizcerna come back, and unfortunately, that winter, we were stringing wire in the city here, and he was climbing up a pole, and he went too high and walked into the 4,000 with his head
And bang, up was the end of Eddy right there.
So those were some of the problems.
Oh, you had - Had another guy
who had family troubles.
A hell of a good man.
He was a man about my age.
And he brought his kids into the city.
And, cripes, they went wild in the city here.
And he was out in the country.
And he used to tell me to look in on them.
And I tried to follow the one boy.
He was trying to get his grade eight.
He was about sixteen years old.
And, hell, he wasn't going to school, and the schoolteachers would phone me up, and I was a truant officer, amongst other things.
And those were probably, as I recall, the only two real
problems I had with -
Oh, you had guys going down the road.
They're fired -
I fired a guy down near Assiniboia.
He was running a camping crew, and I happened to come into town at one o'clock in the afternoon, and here the outfit was standing still.
When I go up to the hotel, I see the trucks staying there.
I go in, and here they come out of the beer parlor.
He was drunk as hell.
I said, "You're fired right now, Owen.
You're finished right now.
And that's
The first time I told you, I said, you're finished.
Not the second."
"Oh," he said, "Fired?"
I said, "Yeah, fired."
The rest of them went back to work.
And then they pull up their britches when they see a thing like that happen.
And one of my old diggers, George Kukachuk, pulled a boner - not a boner, just a dirty, rotten trick, burying an anchor.
And took some shortcuts to make it look like we'd done a hell of a sweetheart of a job.
And just by chance,
they changed the construction design on that corner, and I had to dig these anchors out.
When I saw what he'd done, I said, "You're finished right now, George.
My God, a man of your age and how much work you've done in your day, and you would pull a trick like that on a guy that's used you the way I used you."
I said, "You're done right now, George.
You're fired."
Those were probably the only two men I ever fired right on the job.
I said -
I had one other fall when I was working for the power company in Estevan.
And as soon as the superintendent was out of town, he'd start hollering.
He wanted his pay slip so that he could go out and get a better job.
And every time the superintendent left, he'd start that and keep on -
And that upset the other men, you know.
So this night, Dubois phoned me from Peace River.
He's up there on a project.
He phoned me
(that's the superintendent).
He phoned me and
had me to send some things up to him.
So I said, "What about Walden?"
I said, every time you leave town, he comes in and says, He wants to quit.
"Where's the superintendent?
I want to quit."
He said, "If he wants to quit, go over to the unemployment insurance and get a slip, and you sign it," he said.
That he can quit, you know.
That was during the war.
You had to get a slip to leave your job.
The boss had to release you.
So the next morning, this guy comes -
comes out of work and here's his quit slip laying on the desk.
Well, you never saw a guy.
He didn't mean it.
He didn't mean it.
I said, "Bob, every time Dubois is out of town, you ask for a quit slip?
There it is, and I have the authority to sign it."
Well - "Three days, you're finished, or tonight if you want to quit."
Well, he quit that night.
That's about the only recollection I have of where I really had to -
Oh, you had guys all the time that you had to - that would -
When I was working for the power company, guys nipping at your heels all the time, trying to make you trouble and whatnot, but you just brush them aside and keep on going.
You didn't pay too much attention to them.
I'm still looking.
I can't see whether this -
Yes, it's still going.
I'm running out of talk.
I think I'll have to just make up a few things as I go along now.
After you've read this tape and analyze it,
you'll probably have a lot of questions to ask me the next time you see me, some of the details that go into such things as looking after machinery and equipment.
That's a long story, looking after our machines.
We never bought but very little equipment new.
Trucks! We bought new trucks, but other boring machines,
and cranes and things like that would buy second-handed and fix them up in our shop.
And if you saw some of the things that they did with the little equipment, you'd say it was impossible, such as making an auger.
We made all our augers for our earth-boring equipment.
We had six earth-boring machines.
And we used to wheel them out in the wintertime.
They'd make them up to, oh, as much as 30- and 40-inch augers.
We designed augers that would dig through frost.
No manufacturer made augers like we did.
When they got the screw-in anchors, instead of paying $15 for one, we could build one for $10.
And a better anchor than the factory had designed.
The big factory in the States was turning them out by the thousands.
And we designed a better auger right in our own shop and built them right there.
Oh, so many things.
We'd buy these old machines that somebody else couldn't make go and make a few improvements on them and go out and dig all year, no problems.
And they'd say, "How come
that machine was always in the shop when we had it."
"Well, you didn't have the right kind of man on it."
You didn't tell them about the little things that you fixed that had been driving them crazy.
We bought one that the city had.
We spent about $600 on it.
We dug more footage with it the first year we had it than what they had dug in 10 years.
And they had it in the shop, and they had bills for $1,000, $2,000 repairs on it.
They couldn't figure it out, you know.
Well, I said, "It's all on the man you put on it."
I said, "You get the right guy to run your machine and train him the proper way, and you won't have any trouble."
We bought a Williams boring machine from Billings, Montana, and it would only go down 18 feet.
And we wanted to go down 30 feet.
So the guy was up from the factory one day, and he told me, "Oh," he said, "That machine,
it won't stand up.
The frame and everything isn't heavy enough."
We extended it ourselves in our own shop that it would go down 30 feet, and never anything went wrong with it, and we had it for 15 years.
The only thing with it was clutch trouble.
We always had clutch trouble, clutch trouble.
We got the trouble book with it when we bought it from Billings, and the guy from the factory had been up there half a dozen times, couldn't figure why they were throwing clutches all the time.
And after we had it a couple of years, this guy was up from the factory, and he got up and crawled in there.
He couldn't figure it out.
The next day, I had never looked in the transmission.
The next day, I went and took a look.
I said, "Hell, you haven't got enough clutch in this machine.
Look, you've got the same kind of clutch out here doing the job that this one is doing, and you've got a three-to-one ratio.
So how do you expect this clutch to stand up?"
Art Miller measured it up. And "God,"
he said, "We can double that -
twin this clutch. You can add on as many segments as you have room for."
He got on the phone and phoned Edmonton and got the repairs and put it in, and we never did have clutch trouble after that.
Previous to that, when I was nosing around to see what the Power Corporation had, one competitor, George Carmine, up in -
in Lloydminster had one, asked him, "What do you do for clutch?"
"Oh, we never have clutch trouble."
"No? never have clutch trouble?
Power Corporation never have clutch trouble?"
So after we've got ours fixed, that noise gets around, the men get talking, you know.
Then they started phoning me up, hey, what do you do to your clutch that you're not having clutch trouble?
Well, I said, "Why do you ask?"
"Well, we're having trouble."
"Well," I said, "When I asked you fellas what to do,
you all said you didn't have, you and George Carmine both said, never had any clutch trouble."
About a year later, George Carmine phones me up.
"Hey," he said, "What did you do with your clutch?"
"Well," I said, made it - doubled the capacity of it.
But I said, "Why do you ask?
You never had clutch trouble."
"Oh, yeah, we've always had some clutch trouble."
"Well," I said, "When I asked you, you said you never had trouble."
And those were some of the things that you run into -
make you laugh, you know, if you apply a little moxy.
The Power Corporation had engineers try to design a pole straightener, a machine that could grab a pole and straighten it up and hold it there while they tamped it in.
And so one day I said to Art, "Let's build a pole straightener."
"Well, what do we make?"
Well, I explained to him what I had in mind.
"Go over to the lumber yard and get a bunch of lap."
He went over and got a bundle of lap, and we come out.
I drew a picture on the cement floor with a piece of chalk of what it should look like.
And then we built one out of - a mock-up out of lap, you know, and see how you could jackknife the thing and move it in different directions.
Art went ahead and built that thing in the shop, put it on a truck,

and never changed one thing. And we used it for five, six years never changed a thing on design or on equipment or anything. It just went out and it did its job. And the Power Corporation said, "God we spent thousands of dollars trying to design one." Then I said to Art, "Go ahead and put one on each boring machine, put one up on the head of the boring machine that you can grab it swing the boom back and forth and straighten the pole out."
He designed one, put it on, never changed a dang thing on it.
Now I'm talking about equipment.
Yesterday I talked into this thing for about 20 minutes and found out I didn't have it on - in gear for taping.
So now I have to go over the whole thing again.
And I'll still stay with the equipment, which is one of the big factors in building a power line is that you're properly equipped at all times.
We'd have 12, 13 trucks out on the road, and most of them had special equipment on them.
And it had to do its job.
And if it didn't, you were losing time on the job.
Your production was down.
I go back to boring machines.
I never bought a new machine.
I may have said this earlier on the tape.
I'd tell about buying the machine down at Billings, Montana.
Driving through there, one day I saw this machine and phoned back after I got home and found out it was for sale, so I sent Art down to buy it.
And it was a machine that was almost in, as Danny would say, in mint condition.
But it would only drill 20 feet deep, so we brought it up and we converted it to go down 32 feet.
And the fellow from the factory is up here in the meantime.
Salesman, I should say.
"Oh," he said, "You can't do that."
He said, "That machine, the frame and everything isn't heavy enough."
Well, we did it.
And we never had any trouble with the frame.
But the machine had a history of bad clutch problems.
While it was in Billings, they had a correspondence with the factory on it.
And they had experts up there two or three times.
And we had all this, got this file with it, when we got the machine.
And we were taking out clutches all the time.
Well, not often, but every so often the clutch would go out.
So I inquired from the Power Corporation.
They had one.
"Oh, no, we never have clutch trouble.
I don't know why you're having clutch trouble."
So then I went to George Carmine.
He had one of the machines.
"No," he said, "Never have any clutch trouble.
I don't know why you're having clutch trouble." So -
Anyway, when they had it apart one day, I looked up in
the mechanism and I said, "You know we haven't got enough clutch in here. I said the one I had about it's the same size and we've got a three to one ratio, so how do you expect this one to stand up?"

Well it was a twin disc clutch and you can add and add on to them. So Art measured up and found out there's room enough to double the capacity of the clutch. Got on the phone, phoned Edmonton, got the unnecessary parts, and took it to a machine shop, had a few alterations made and
put it in, and that was the end of our clutch trouble.
No problems, whatever, after that.
About a year later, the noise got around.
The men get talking, you know.
The noise got around.
I got a phone call from the Power Corporation one day.
"Hey, what did you do with your clutch that you're not having any clutch trouble?"
"Well," I said, "I just doubled the capacity of it.
Why do you ask?"
"Well, we're having clutch trouble, and -
and uh we were wondering what you did." "Well, when I asked you guys about clutch trouble you said you never had any trouble. So now when I get mine fixed now you want to fix yours the same way."

Less than a month later I had a call from George Carmine; same thing. "Hey what did you do to fix your clutch?" "Well," I said, "You're the guy never had any clutch trouble and when I asked you for information
you said, never have any clutch trouble.
Now you're having clutch trouble."
"Well, we always did have some, but it wasn't that bad."
Well, anyway, I told them how to fix their machines, and that was the end of that.
But I got quite a kick out of it when I inquired.
"No, nobody ever had any clutch trouble."
We bought the second-handed the machines.
We would take them in and find their weakness.

the city had one, ten years old and they had it in their machine shop all the time. And they traded and got a new one and I bought the dang thing for $2,000 and it was about - well if you bought a new one it'd be $15,000 so -

We took it and we spent $600 on it. And we dug more footage with that machine in one year than what they had dug in the ten years they had, and we never had any trouble with it. These guys couldn't figure out how
you know, "How did you do that?"
"Well," I said, "Got to have good operators."
I didn't give them any of the secrets, some of the things that they hadn't found fault with, and we recognized it immediately and altered it.
So that was the history of machines.
I bought two used machines down at Devil Lakes - Devils Lake, North Dakota, and I brought them up here.
And I wouldn't have defied anybody to dig a hole with either one of those machines.
And the guy said that just the way they came off of the job and they were standing in the yard, he went out of business.
I bought them and we brought them up here and spent considerable money overhauling them and got them in shape.
Used those machines for 10 years after that.
I think I gave them $2,000 apiece for them.
And again, a $10,000 machine, if you bought a new one, that would do the same kind of a job.
It was interesting to see how we could take this old junk and make it work, and that was
part of our success was to keep the overhead down.
Power Corporation asked me one time how much I spent on boring machines, and I told them. We had four machines at that time.
He said, "We spend more than that on one machine in a year."
"Well," I said, "You haven't got the operators."
You've got to have the right men operating it and, of course, overhauling it as well.
So that's
gives you an idea what you run into for equipment.
I went over the manpower, and now I come back to the finance.
I may have told you something about that in the start, but throughout the years, I've never had any trouble financing.
I think I told you I had $3,000 in the bank when I started, and I went to the bank manager in Estevan and told him I might need a little

extra money and he said, "Well how much you got?" And, I said, "I got three thousand dollars in bonds in the safety deposit box." I said, "You want me to put them up for collateral?" "No," he said, "You go ahead," and he said, "I'll just run you on overdraft until - and charge you interest on it." In those days they allowed you to run on overdraft. With the result that I never had to sign a note of any description. I operated out of Estevan
from '45 and then '48 when I come up here.
I said to the fellow in the bank, "There's one thing I want understood now when we start.
I don't ever want one of my cheques turned down.
If there isn't enough money in the bank to cover it, you cover it until I get there to take care of it."
And I said, "If you don't, I'll move my account to Moose Jaw where my old friend Bill Mullins
who started me up in Estevan, is the manager at Moose Jaw, and he'll take care of me."

Well, I never did have a check turned down on overdraft, no matter - One time I was overdrawn about $10,000, and there was a relief manager in, and he called me, and he said, "Hey, your account's getting pretty badly overdrawn."
He said, "I think we'd better take a look at that."
I said, "What are you worrying about that for?"
I said, "I think I've got to worry about that."
He didn't know that at that particular time I had about $30,000 worth of work done, which I hadn't invoiced, which would wipe out the $10,000 in a hurry.
One time in the spring of the year, I needed quite a bit of money, and I give them a $5,000 government bond for security.
There was another manager come in and he was a little shaky.
So I put that up until I had my overdraft taken care of.
And then I forgot that they had it and that fall when I went in to get it, I thought I'd better cash it in and here I'd run overtime and had to take a penalty for not cashing it in on the due date.
I don't think they have that anymore.
So I always got along well with financing, never had any trouble.
I could complete a job and then invoice the Power Corporation for it.
Evidently, all the other contractors were always, when they'd get a couple weeks' work done, they'd invoice on it, invoice on it.
Ted Durnan, the superintendent, said to me one day, he said, "I think you're making too damn much money."
I said, "Why do you say that?"
"Well," he said, "All the other guys are subbing all the time and you're not."
"Well," I said, "I'm working for the same price they're working."
But I said, "I've got news for you.
My credit is good enough at the bank that I could build lines all summer and invoice you for it in the fall."
And I said, "So it's not that I'm making too much money, but," I said, "I've got a good credit established."
So I went along.
Oh, in 1954, they had a big sleet storm in Manitoba, and they took down all the telephone lines in southern Manitoba.
And they come to Saskatchewan for help, see if we could rig up some small three-man crews on half-ton trucks.
So they came to me, and I don't know how they got a hold of my name, and asked how many outfits I could send out, three-man crews.
They would supervise them once they got out there.
So I phoned all the contractors, and I rounded up 12 outfits.
This was, oh, early in the spring, about early May or the end of April.
Between all the contractors, I lined up 12 outfits.
Two of my own were in that.
Well, then I found out how the guys were operating.
Most of them had me sub them for money to buy a license before they could get going.
I paid them.

Every two weeks I paid them out for their units and I didn't get any money until the whole job was finished. And when it was finished it came to - I think was $38,000 and I invoiced the government telephones in Manitoba for that much money. And that was the biggest check I had ever received up to that date. On that I owned - I didn't know how much I should charge -
take off, you know, for supervising the operation.
So I took ten percent.
That's what I got for it.
It was all over in a, I think our outfits were down there maybe three weeks or something like that.
So I got paid $3,800 for my three weeks, and it was all office work as far as I was concerned.
Telephoned a lot of telephone calls.
And what were some of the other highlights?
I told you about the last job I did, which was the biggest individual job I had ever bid on, which was just under $200,000.
And didn't - I made some money on it, but didn't get wealthy on it; put it that way.
And I don't know.
All in all, it was interesting.
I may have told you before that Ted Durnan tried to hire me one time to run their whole construction outfit.
That was in 1949.
He asked me what I'd take, and I said I'd take $10,000 a year, but there'd be no contracting.
As long as there was contracting, I wanted to be in on the competitive field.
And he said, "Hell, I can't pay you $10,000 a year.
I only get $8,000."
"Well,"
I said, "I figure I'm worth $10,000 to run your construction outfit with my experience."
So that was the end of that.
They set up a different arrangement.
I think Jack Bell was sort of put in charge of the construction.
They never looked back.
They got so big at that time that money was nothing to them.
They just kept on expanding and expanding and expanding,
until the Union got a hold of it,
and then all of a sudden they got in a squeeze where the Union was running the outfit instead of themselves.
And I've had them come to me when they had trouble, or not trouble, but they had a customer just out of Regina here that had to have service just at Christmas time.
They'd come to me and ask if I couldn't rustle up a crew to go out and do this job that would take
maybe four, five, or six days.
And in that came Christmas Day, Boxing Day, and the Sunday and all, and they wanted to go out and work right straight through until it was finished.
And I said, "That's going to cost you a lot of money, a lot of overtime in there for the amount of work we do."
"That don't make any difference."
But when I present them with a bill, "Oh, we can't stand that overtime.
No way."
The guy, the engineer in charge said, "If I presented that, I said, my job could be on the,

on the line." And he said, "You're going to have to cut back and stand half that overtime yourself." Which meant that I made damn little on the job but that's the way - They would take advantage of you occasionally when they got you over the barrel.
All in all, I will have to say they treated me pretty fair I had - I had a lot of leeway with them and with this man Durnan,

we had a lot of trust in each other although, he was a tight one. Boy, he wouldn't - he'd give you authority to do things but don't ever try to to pad the invoice or anything like that or he'd be right on you in a minute.
But it all went on and on. While that was going on, I think I related earlier that, I had an outfit working in the city for 10 years that equipment: a boring machine,
a pole truck - pole trailer, and a two-ton truck.
And they were on so much an hour.
And in those 10 years, I never raised the rate on that.
I only raised wages as they went along.
I took 10% on payroll.
Well, that was sort of a meal ticket for me.
And then when they'd get behind, I could leave the work stack up and bring in my men from the field in the fall when they were finished out there.
And then we'd pick up a lot of extra work in the city.
I set all the poles in all the suburbs that were built from 1951.
Yes, I even did some in 1945 or '46.
And then started again in '50.
Set all the poles and built all the lines in all the suburbs in Regina.
