So, I was a bit dissatisfied the way things were going and I suppose independent as hell. I had money in the bank I'd bought a brand new car in Rosetown - a brand new Ford Coach for nine-hundred and -
no, $819 is what I paid for it, to give you an idea of car prices at that time.
Anyway, then I had taken a trip down home.
I got a few days off and went down home.
And mother was wanting to go to Iowa.
She said, "She wished she could take another trip to Iowa."
I had taken her down there.
No, I hadn't ever taken her down.
That was the first trip.
"So," I said, "Well, I'll sign off my job and take off time and
I'll take you down there."
So I told the superintendent, I said, "I'm going to book off for three, four months."
"Well," he said, "We can't guarantee your job when you come back."
"Well," I said, "I don't care if you guarantee my job or not.
I want to take the time off."
So I left and when I came through Regina
I went into the head office and this
head guy told me, "Boy you've sure done an ace-high job for us out there."
And that's all there was to it. I went down and mother and I took the trip to Iowa which was a wonderful time we're down there about six weeks.
I think I left about the end of September, and come back.
And when I got back to Halbrite - Midale, here Midale we're going to put in the power plant, and a power system.
So right away they heard I was back. "How about coming and building the lines for us?" I said, "Sure, I'll take on that job."
So I went down and supervised - laid out the lines for them and everything. Built - you could get help in those days. No trouble getting help.
And in the course of building the line the Power Corporation found out I was working, see?
So one of the guys, the superintendent, kind of took a dig at me.
After I had built the system, then my brother Mac and I started wiring houses, and we wired all the houses in Midale, which is a total winter job.
So that was all cleaned up in the spring.
I went back to the power to see if I could get a job.
And, oh, the superintendent, he - no, the general manager, oh, he got -
he got so nasty with me that I still don't know today how I didn't just climb over his desk and flatten his face in for him.
And anyway, I didn't have to work.
I had money in the bank
I went back home and stayed on the farm.
I worked around - messed around there for my keep, and I had one hell of a good time that whole summer.
And in the fall, I went down to Estevan, and I went in to see the superintendent there.
So I told him about this general manager giving me such a bad time.
He said, "Linden, they can't do that to you."
He said, "We've got some work coming up.
You go home and get your tools and come on down here and we'll get you to work."
That would have been about in October, I would say.
Maybe the first of October.
because there was snow on the ground shortly after I got there.
And I worked there about a month.
And then they were starting up a power plant out at Tompkins.
One of their men was a fellow that I worked under, while I was in Estevan,
he was superintendent of the steam plant.
And he was dissatisfied there, so he went out to start up this plant at Tompkins.
It was a little town of 350 people out west of Swift Current.
So I packed up and went out to Tompkins and built the distribution system out there, and then again went into wiring for the winter.
After the system was going, I started wiring houses, and I kept relatively busy all winter.
Now we're talking into the winter of 1932 was the year that I was at home on the farm.
And the fall of '32, I went to Tompkins.
Yes.
And then in, no, that's, I mixed up there someplace.
'32, I didn't work.
And it must have been most of '33 I didn't work.
Fall of '33, I landed out at Tompkins
put in that system,
and wired houses all winter.
And then the spring of '34, Dubois, the superintendent, this was still the same company that was in Estevan, he said, "I'm putting you down in Gull Lake to do some wiring there.
There's a bunch of the business places, the wiring is such a hell of a mess."
"And," he said, "we want to spend some money and straighten it out on account of it."
So I went into Gull Lake, and I -
I did these jobs.
I probably worked there a month.
As I remember, three of the bigger businesses there that I worked in put in the metering setup and the whole panel and everything for their electric system had to be changed around.
And then when that was finished, he said, "Now, I'm going to put you out to overhaul in the distri -"
down there, I lined up there - there's a bunch of wiring to be done in that town, so I spent a month in there wiring.
And, uh, as I remember it, I finished and went back up to Gull Lake on Armistice Day.
Well, there wasn't much to do around Gull Lake, so there was some wiring to be done in Tompkins, and I moved back to Tompkins.
There you could get room and board for a dollar a day.
So I went back up.
By this time, Jack Dyer was getting kind of tired of running the power plant.
He - It was too small for him,
and he was in financial problem - difficulties.
He was in the hole all over the place.
I saw this.
He wasn't doing a very big job running the plant.
It was a diesel plant.
He was having a lot of power outages and what have you, So
one day I said to him, "You're dissatisfied here."
I said, "If you'd like to get out of here,"
I said, "I'll take over.
I'll buy you out."
"Well, what kind of a deal were you on for?"
So we sat down at we talked.
- what kind of deal that I would give him.
I would pay all the debts that he owed, and get him cleaned out of that one -
I'd give him my car, and I'd give him some cash.
"By God," he said, "I'll go for that."
So I phoned up Walter Stasher, the general manager in Estevan, and told him about this deal.
"Well, hell," he said, "We'll give you money.
If you want to buy him out, we're not happy with the way he's running the thing anyway."
So they staked me - It wasn't a matter of any amount of money.
I remember I borrowed $600 off of Mother, and they staked me for the rest of it.
There, I had a job running a power plant.
My wages were $120 a month.
Well, that gave me plenty to live on.
I traded in my car, so I didn't have a car, so they wrestled me up an old truck they had sitting down in Estevan.
They put new tires on it.
Drove in to Regina, and I came in here and picked it up, and I had a truck to work with.
Well, my brother Mac was out of work, so he came out.
He and Ellen moved out there.
And he was mechanically inclined, so it took him no time at all to learn how to run the power plant.
So then I went out working for the Dominion Electrics.
I was still managing the power plant.
Paid Mac and Ellen a wage.
They had a house, rented a house, and they had a place to live.
And I went out and worked for them on the - overhauled the power line between Shaunavon and Assiniboia.
Had a four-man crew.
And we worked there all summer, right up until, I would say, about the 1st of October, when again they'd run out of money.
We hadn't finished the job, and they said, "Well, that's it."
So then I went back to Tompkins, and between Mac and I, we had nothing much to do but run the power plant and a little wiring.
I used to go around doing wiring any place in the country.
There were no electricians out in there. And that - now we're into 1934.
It was in 1934 that the electrician - the inspector who used to come through there said to me one day, "Why don't you write your journeyman's paper?" he said, "You're a good electrician."
So, I went up to Moose Jaw and they held a writing in there, and it was the worst headache I ever had in my life. I went in there, they give you a -
oh, what the hell did they call it a -
(code book) - a book of rules and regulations.
The "code book", yeah.
And there they give you a bunch of questions, and you dig the answers out of the code book and put down on paper.
Well, this I found very, very difficult, not having ever had to use a code book and figure out jobs according to the code.
And I, when I went out of that place that night, I had the God-darnest headache that a man ever had in his life.
I didn't figure I made it.
Well, about ten days later, I get word that I had passed my examination.
Well, I was never so happy in my life as when I got that journeyman's paper.
That was in the fall of 1934.
So that - I spent thirty -
nineteen thirty - yeah, '35, running a power plant and wiring.
And then in '36, they decided they were going to run a power line from Gull Lake to Tompkins, and abandoned this little power plant.
Found out they could serve it cheaper in Gull Lake, and they had plenty of capacity there.
And they were going to put the power plant up to Winnipegosis, Manitoba.
So I was going to be out of a job just that fast.
Well, when the dust was settled, I came out of there with $2,000 for my equity in the power plant.
And they said, "You'll go up." -
Oh yeah, I was made a foreman on the line while the line was being built.
Well, that job only lasted about six weeks.
And then I went up, they said, "We're putting the plant up to Winnipegosis, Manitoba, and we want you to go up there and wire up the town.
Not the line, but the inside wiring of the town.
We've got to have somebody up there that will do it at a reasonable price that we get customers."
So when I went up, and Ellen - my brother Mac, and Ellen, they broke up housekeeping there, and she went back to her parents in Estevan.
I don't mean that they parted, they - The work run out, so to speak.
They shipped their stuff back to Estevan, and Mac came up to Winnipegosis, Manitoba with me where we wired houses there.
I want to interject now.
This same man Dyer, when he left Tompkins, he went up to Winnipegosis and organized the starting of a power plant in Winnipegosis.
Well, he got himself into -
He got the franchise but then he bogged down right then and there, and -
He hollered to me for help so - I couldn't see where I could help him any, so I turned the whole issue over to the Dominion Electric, the old power company in Estevan.
And they took over and he headed for British Columbia where he spent the rest of his days.
So we'll go on taping again about Winnipegosis.
- June, July, August, September, October, and by the middle of November we were finished.
We had everything wired up, so we packed up and went back to- oh yes, I was promised a job when they closed the plant down in Tompkins, that I would have a job at Estevan;
back at Estevan again.
They were transferring the guy out of there to some other place.
So while I was wiring up at Winnipegosis, this fella took holidays, and they sent me down to Estevan for one month to take charge of the district there.
And then Mac and I, we "checked out", as I said, about the middle of November, and he went back to Midale.
And I went back to Estevan and went to work.
And I worked in Estevan.
That was the fall of '37.
Then I was in charge of the lines there for the rest of '37, '38, '39, '40.
The war was full swing.
And, on, I was married that fall.
Married on the end of November in '40.
Then the airport came in in 1941, so it was really a busy time around there.
God, I went - there were times there during the war that I worked 10 hours a day, 7 days a week. ...
And be out a night doing refrigeration work. There was nobody to repair refrigerators, so I got a repair manual, and I took that up as a sideline.
And I just worked like a slave all during the war.
I could have gone into the Navy.
I put my application in.
I could have gone into the commission as a first-class warrant officer, which is equivalent to a sergeant.
And Phyllis and I talked it over, and
well, I had seen the guys that were in the Army and talked to them.
And everybody was having one hell of a time and 4,000 miles away from their families.
And we talked it over and said, "Well -" I was "frozen",
my job as DES.
The company had got an order that I didn't - couldn't be drafted.
So I said, "OK, I'll stay here and slug it out.
Let somebody else fight the war while I work harder than anybody
in the Army ever thought of working."
And everything was going well, fine.
Here, every six months they had to apply for a deferment for me.
They forgot to apply for the deferment here.
One morning I get a telegram, "Report for duty in the King's Army."
Well, my God.
Now here, here I turned down an officer's job
in the navy, and now here I'm going to be just a foot soldier in the Army.
So I went in and I said to the general manager, Stasher was still there.
"Well," I said, "What the hell have you guys run me into now?" "Oh God," he said, "We forgot to get your deferment," so he said, "I'll take care of that."
Well, the next day I got a telegram not - He knew some of the big shots up
in Regina here, and they they called it off.
So I spent the war years in Estevan.
And it was after the war that -
Oh yeah, the company had moved up into the Peace River country pretty strong by that time, and the superintendent spending a lot of time up there.
So I was pretty well in charge of everything at Estevan.
And here, one day, they had a big generator blow up.
And the general manager asked me to go down and look at it, give a report to New York of what had to be done.
He didn't know anything about the wiring in the generator.
So I went down, looked it over.
They had the machine all opened up.
I took a note of what was, and we went up into the office
and drafted a telegram to send to New York to tell them what damage had happened.
Well, when this was over, while we were sitting there talking, and I said, "I see by the Leader Post that they're advertising for line construction for the Power Commission."
I said, "I got a damn good notion to take a swing at it."
I said, "I know how to build power lines."
He said, "Why don't you?"
He said, "You're young."
He said, "I'll give you a leave of absence, and if you don't, if you don't get any contracts, well, you can come back to work."
I won't promise you that you'll be back here in Estevan, but there'll be a job for you.
"Well," I said, "Sure."
So I hopped on the bus the next day, or drove up, I forget.
Come up to Regina, got a set of tender forms, and I sat down in a room -
Oh, yeah, he said, "In the meantime, why don't you take Harry Monaghan on for a partner?"
Harry was an old friend of his and had built lines way back in the 20s.
He said, "He's an experienced man.
He'll be a great partner for you."
So we come up here.
Well, Harry was out of touch with things.
I sat down and
I filled out the tenders.
They were to be opened one up there.
And by God, I get - the first job I get is building a line from Halbrite to Midale and the distribution system in - yeah, and Macoun - and the distribution system in Macoun and Halbrite.
And at the same time bid on a big heavy, high voltage line from Estevan to Stoughton, and I get that too.
Well, here I am, I got two contracts.
I've got $2,000, maybe $3,000 in the bank, and I'm a contractor, just like that.
Well, that was an experience that I'm going to take off time,
now, I've been talking here for the best part of an hour.
I'm going to take off time and come back.
Then we'll be out of my years working for a company,
Now I'm a contractor on my own, and the time is the end of September 1945.
So I'll click this off right now, and I don't know when I'll get back at it again.
I'll give you a history of my years of contracting, which was from 1945 to 1965.
Bye right now.
So I don't know
whether I continued on or not -
well, there it is -
September the 14th, 1981.
I can't remember when I run off the last of that tape when I said - I started to talk, "I'm a contractor now, and I don't know when I'll talk into this thing again."
I think it was possibly back in September.
However,
to get into my years of contracting, I would have to get wound up just right - in the right mood to sort of give you an idea of what it was like to start out from scratch as a contractor.
I had, I would say, $3,000 in the bank.
I didn't own a car. I sold my car.
But I had a wealth of knowledge in the construction business.
So the first thing to do was to come to Regina and tender on work here.
Two projects coming up.
One was a power line from Midale to Halbrite and Macoun,
and the other was a high-voltage suspension-type line, 72,000 volts, which was something new in Saskatchewan at that time, from Estevan to Stoughton, which was a distance of 40 miles.

Well, I then set down and figure out how much is that going to cost you to do a job, you could only dig out of the back of your head and say, so many men do so much work, and then bring that out to hours,
and choose the number of cents per hour, or dollars, whatever it was -
cents in those days.
You didn't pay anybody a dollar an hour.
And what your equipment would cost you, and then say, "Well, it's going to cost me so many thousand dollars to do that job and I've got to add on profit,
and this is what my tender price would be."
The first job was really a difficult one. It was dismantling two airports. One at -
That seems to be where everything stops. I can't find any tape beyond what you just heard there. So, I'm going to
call this to a halt for the time being, and as I said there earlier on the tape, in order to give an outline of what contracting was like for the next 18 years.
I'll need to be in the right frame of mind.
So with that I'm going to say "Good night" and the date right now is May the 5th, 1986.
so I've been monkeying around for five years on this thing and I haven't come up with anything very interesting, as I see it in going over it.
However, we'll finish it out and give you a rundown on what it was like to be contracting.
This is May the 8th.
And, I don't know if I feel like talking this morning or not, but I'll make an attempt at it.
First off, I want to say things were pretty well modeled up on my tapes, having spoken into them over a period of five years.
And then the kids would get into them, and some of it would get erased.
All the way along, it may be hard for you to follow this story as it was unfolding.
But I just want to say that it would be awfully hard to sit down and just give the history over 18 years, 20 years virtually.
I started working on power in 1927, and then I worked until just about the end of '45 for various companies.
And through all that I was gaining experience.
The biggest thing was handling men.
After the first two years that I was in the electrical
business, I was always in charge of men.
And that's the big thing when you're contracting, is you depend on your men doing the work.
You don't do it yourself.
And you have to organize your men and have them working for you and not against you.
And during the years that I worked for the power company, you always - oh - you had difficulties.
Guys would get hard to handle, and
they'd buck you a little bit, and you always - just one thing you had to do, and that's plow them under and keep on moving ahead.
You never look back.
If you had any labor problems, you waded through them and kept on going.
So to get back to when I started out from Midale,
That's where I started building.
I didn't have any equipment.
First thing I did, I went to a dealer in Estevan, and he had a half-ton truck there.
And trucks were hard to come by at that time.
This truck had about 60,000 miles on it.
It was in pretty bad shape.
But I had to have a half-ton truck, and it was the only one available.
So I virtually took it from him.
I said, "I'm going to have that truck, and you can't sell it to anybody else."
I happened to know the dealer very well.
That was the start.
I had a half-ton truck.
Then I rented a man with a truck when I started dismantling airports.
And then I got word that there was some military trucks being sold in Regina.
In fact, the Power Corporation tipped us off to that.
So I come up and I bought an old gun carrier, a great big awkward thing that was built for pulling a gun around the country.
And, what they called, another was a "1500 weight".
It was about a two-ton truck, but equipped for battlefield work.
So I took those trucks and revamped them, took them down to Midale and,
my brother ran a little garage there,
he was able to do welding.
And between the two of us, we got the cutting torches going and got them, made something that would start to function.
There was no steel in the country.
There wasn't -
You couldn't buy anything after the war.
And so I made a wooden derrick -
tripod on the back of this Army truck for setting poles.
We had no digging equipment, just dug the holes by hand.
And I was fortunate enough to get ahold of a couple of experienced diggers.
And they would dig all the holes we needed for a small outfit.
Then a little later, I found out they had another two-ton truck in Regina that the military was selling off.
I got these trucks for virtually nothing.
I got the Army trucks at about $350 a piece, and they were in mint condition.
And then I bought this other truck.
I think I paid $1,100 for it, and it was virtually like a new truck.
So I had pretty good equipment to start with,
just the three trucks, and these army trucks were open.
They didn't have cabs on them.
So you had to put up with that.
In their tough weather, you were sitting out in the open.
Well, then I... Let's see, I worked...
I finished that job dismantling those airports.
That was one hell of a job.
We uncovered...
miles and miles of cable that had been buried about two feet underground.
We were supposed to take it up without damaging it.
Well, I tried every method there was of uncovering that cable, from hand digging right up to getting graders and scraping it down until you got it, but
no way could you go and excavate a half a mile of cable without getting some damage in it; scraping it or digging - even when you're digging by hand you'd rub the shovel into it occasionally. So -
They told us take it out as best we could, and we still had to go through the process of making a trench and pulling the cable out and then filling the trench in again.
I could never figure out how three men,
three different contractors, could sit down and figure out how you dismantled those two airports, overhead lines, underground lines, and everything.
And then use all the usable material to build a power line, as I said, from Halbrite to Midale to Macoun.
And in the three - one - there were four bids!
In the four bids, there was only $1,000 difference from high to low.
I happened to be the low one.
It went all the way from my bid was $10,500 and the highest bid was $11,500.
But it - it was almost unbelievable to think that four men could sit down -
This was something that none of them had ever done before, or none of us had ever done before.
But we come in that close; that's within 10%, which - considered pretty close bidding at any time.
Well, worked in - I finished up in Mydale on that project about the end of November.
Then the Power Commission had some work they wanted done up on the north -
north suburb, what they called North Regina, everything north of the CN tracks.
So I come up and I worked on it through December and January and just had a small crew, I think, four men or so.
And then after that we went to Saskatoon and did a similar job.
This was for wartime housing.
And this was an experience now:
we were digging through frost, anywhere up to four or five foot of frost.
But somehow, well, in Saskatoon it was in sandy ground, you couldn't dig, so I had to put fire, burn the frost out.
And it all worked out pretty good.
You just did what you had to do, and that was it.
Then I came back and I did some work in the town of Davidson for the old Dominion Electric Company.
They were still functioning.
And then I went back to Estevan and just sat in quiet until the spring, when the tenders come out.
Then I got a... the first job was from Weyburn to Radville,
which was a nice job, and it was an early spring.
We were working on that early in April.
And from there on, you just kept on moving ahead.
When I got that job finished, they had a bunch of farms around Weyburn, I did those.
And then I went back to Estevan and started building the
big high voltage line, and that put something else too.
There you got - everything was so heavy and big and we had never dealt with cross arms and insulators and poles.
All the poles were 40 feet or more.
The cross arms were four by sixes, ten feet long.
You had to handle all that heavy stuff.
I still don't know how I was able to make a dollar on the job, but I did.
I finished - worked all summer, besides running out and doing other odd jobs.
That came up from time to time, and I finished that job on the
11th day of December.
We had all the wires drawn, and
it was heavy wire.
That was a new experience, too.
And then I folded up for the winter.
Now I'll relate
what I did for help - for men.
I got a hold of a fellow, he was a few years older than myself, that had been a lineman.
And he was uneducated.
He could hardly write his own name, but he was a good worker.
Good, honest -
On the last tape I started telling you what I had for a crew of men to start with.
I started out about Jack Gardner he was the first fellow I hired.
And, I picked up help. Young kids out of school, one thing another. We're just out staking line and getting material out and then later on, I rounded up some
fellows from Regina here that were experienced hole diggers.
Two of them in particular were professionals.
So we started digging the holes, and what have you, and then we - oh yeah, we had to get machinery then to set these poles.
This was the biggest poles that had ever been set in this province.
So I had the old army truck and I had a derrick
made for it, a 25-foot derrick.
And we could handle the poles quite nicely.
Once in a while, we'd get a heavy one that'd give us some trouble.
Well, anyway, in the meantime, I picked up Art Miller.
He was just a young fellow out of the Army looking for a job.
He was handy at driving trucks.
He was really good with trucks.
So that gave me the nucleus of a -
of an outfit.
We started setting poles, dropping the poles in the holes and hand tamping them.
Have five and six men tamping and two men shoveling.
And, oh, we'd set about twenty-five poles a day, maybe thirty on a good day.
And I worked that crew up - by the time I'd finished the Stoughton job,
I had twenty-five, thirty men working for me.
And again, no one with experience.
Until I come to string in the Wire, then I picked up a couple of fellows that had climbed poles in the Army.
That gave me three or four climbers, and with that we were able to get along.
Everything was new.
You had to figure out everything for yourself.
No one had ever
had the experience of doing a job of stringing a line under the cross arms.
Oh, I'll come back to that again.
Everybody wondered, "How can you string wire when the cross arms are on a slant and you can't lay the wire on them?"
Well, I went to Winnipeg.
I figured out if I got some hay pulleys, about four-inch pulleys,
big enough to take a three-quarter inch rope.
I went to Winnipeg, and I went to Ashdown Hardware, and I asked the guy, "Have you got any wooden hay pulleys?
Just the bare pulley I want."
"Yeah."
I said, "How many?"
He looked up his inventory.
He had 250.
I said, "I'll take them all."
The guy looked at me like I was out of my tree.
"What are you going to do with 250 wooden pulleys?"
So I said, "I've got a job for them.
Don't worry.
I'm not buying them just for fun."
However, I - we designed hangers for these pulleys.
They worked out 100%.
Today, they use a pulley to do the same job.
Of course, the conductors are heavier,
but the pulleys they use nowadays cost you probably $100, $150 a piece.
Those cost me about $0.35 a piece.
Well, plus the labor of assembly.
Well, anyway, that wound up the Stoughton job, and I finished that, as I remember, right on December the 9th, and then we folded up for the winter.
I'll start out, go year by year where we worked and what we did, and sort of run over it roughly.
There'd be so much detail
if I went into detail of what happened.
And all the men I went through, and the men I trained and everything. It would take hours and hours to tell it all.
Anyway, you've got to be in the mood for talking when you start telling stories about things that happened 40 years ago.
So we'll just wind her up for now.
I'm going to listen to Brian Mulroney.
I listened to Brian, and he didn't have much to say talking on trade with the United States.
He talked a lot, but he didn't say much, put it that way.
I'm sitting outside right now.
It's 88 degrees out here.
It's on Tuesday afternoon, May the...
17th.
June the 17th, not May.
June the 17th, yeah.
So I don't know what it's going to be like out here.
The wind is blowing or there may be some roaring in the mic on account of the wind.
I'll just take a shot of this and see what's happening.
I'll play it back now and see what this sounds like.
I played it back and it sounded all right.
There's no interference from the wind, so I'll go on talking about 1947 now.
1947, my first job was building a line from Weyburn to Radville and picking up the village of Trossachs on the way.
It was an early break up in the spring and we got an early start.
After we finished that job, we did about twenty farms just north of Weyburn, and from there I got a job building a line from Dinsmore to Eston and up from Elrose to Rosetown.
That was about 110 miles of line, including about ten towns and villages that all needed new distribution.
So we got into that, oh, I would say, about mid-June when we started on that job.
Anyway, in the meantime, there had been a reunion organized with a family to meet down at Em's place in Oregon.
And I didn't know what to do.
I had so damn much work ahead of me.
So I happened to be going through Moose Jaw.
My old bank manager from Estevan, Bill Mullins, was the manager in Moose Jaw.
So I stopped in to say hello to him.
And of course, the conversation, I told him how much work I had ahead of me and that there was this reunion coming up where I was to meet eight of my brothers and three sisters.
I didn't know whether I should leave my job and go down there or what I should do.
He said, "Lindy," he said, "If I had eight brothers to meet, he said, I'd leave any kind of a job to go down there to the reunion."
He said, "The job will go along for two weeks.
They'll never miss you."
He said, "What difference it will make?"
He said, "You'll never miss."
So we went to the reunion, and it turned out to be a very good trip.
Bud and Ethel and Phyllis and I went down in one car.
And I had a new car at that time.
So we had really a nice trip.
We were gone two weeks.
And back to the job up at Elrose.
I no more than got that job underway when the superintendent come along and said they had a job
running from Canora, Saskatchewan, up to Preeceville, and over to Norquay.
That was about 60 or 70 miles of line.
It took in one, two, three, four, five towns to build in.
And God, there's no way that I could see that I could handle it.
And Ted Durnan was after me.
They had to get this done.
It was sort of a political thing.

So finally I made a deal with him that if he'd give me his boring machine, or rented me his boring machine to dig all the holes and set the poles, I figured I could rustle up the help to complete the job by the end of the year.
So I had the job at
Rosetown - Elrose pretty well underway, and Art Miller was able to take over it.
So I went to Canora and started that job, again without any help.
Just got my brother Zip to come out and help stake a line.
That's the first thing you have to do is stake a line.
And then we hired some local guys there to haul out the poles, and I got a couple of guys to start putting on the cross arms.
When we got the thing laid out, ready to go, then the power sent up their boring machine.
And we went ahead, and it was easy to organize a tamping crew.
You could get almost any farm laborer to work on that job.
Well, we got all the poles set by about, oh, I'd say we had the pole setting done
by about the end of October, in the towns and on the high line and everything, there was an awful lot of bush to cut up in that country.
I had to hire men with axes to go in and slash a 25-foot swath through sometimes, a quarter mile of solid trees.
Oh, the trees up to five, six inches in diameter.
And that all had to be piled and put out of the way.
Then I was jockeying back and forth between that job and the one over at Dinsmore, trying to get them both cleaned up before winter.
And time was running out on both ends.
So finally, I went over to Dins - to Rosetown - Elrose, and stayed on that job to push the wire stringing.
And they weren't going as fast as they should.
And we had to get somebody to string the wires in the towns and I
sublet some of that to local guys that could climb poles.
And then I - when I left the Canora job, left Jack Gardner in charge.
Well, Jack was the first guy I ever hired.
I think I mentioned before, a hell of a good man.
But if you gave him more than two helpers,
he became confused, and he would do all the work, and the guys would all stand there and watch him.
Well, I left Jack there. He was to start string and wire.
Now, he knew all about string and wire.
When I came back on the job about the 20th of November, and my God, he only had three miles of wire strung.
Well, I shouldn't say that, about six miles of wire strung, and he had been fiddling away for
ten days.
Well, I got so God darn mad, I went out there - and he had a crew of thirteen men.
I went out there and the first guy I'd see that didn't have something definite to do, I said, "You're finished tonight."
I paired the crew down to eight men and then we started stringing three miles of wire every day.
We got organized and really got going.
Well, I finished that job.
Hooked up the last town the day before Christmas.
Well, oh yeah, it was a weekend came in there, and I wanted my crew to work on Sunday, that we could get everything finished.
But they got into the beer parlour Saturday night, and Sunday morning you couldn't get any of them out.
So I got Jack Gardner and myself and two ground men, both experienced ground men.
We went out and we did more work on the Sunday than the whole damned outfit would do.
And we got the town ...
That was the last town, the - Sturgis.
And Durnan had told me they had an old and broken down plant in Sturgis.
He said, "You've got to get Sturgis going before you come back in for Christmas."
So I worked all Sunday,
and about Sunday night, about 11 o'clock, I got in the car
and headed for Regina.
Well, that was about a four-hour drive.
Anyway, I got home about 3 o'clock in the morning.
The heater on my car failed during the way in, so I had to drive in in a cold car.
I got mad at the crew.
Payday came, and I said, "I'm not paying you guys until payday, and that - we'd be in Regina on that day."
Well after work on that day,
they all laid around the hotel all day Sunday,
caught the train Monday, got in here Monday - yeah, late Monday night.
I went down to the station and met them and gave them their paychecks.
Monday was payday.
I laughed at them.
I said, "You guys were so smart.
You missed a day's work, and now you come in a day late for pay." I said.
Let that be a lesson to you for the future.
Well, I don't know if it taught them anything.
Most of those fellows were come and go.
Well, that wound up our season for 1947.
Oh, yes, the Elrose job, they didn't get it quite completed.
They were still finishing things up there in January, and then we shut down until spring.
Now, in '48, I have to stop and think.
In 1948, where did we start?
I really have to scratch my head to see where we started.
In '48, yeah -
Oh, I think I didn't get in on the early bidding.
That's right.
I lost out on all the jobs.
And finally, they came along and they had a job out north of Swift Current, system to put there.
And I got going on that about in June.
Oh yeah, I went and built a line to Cypress Park.
That's where I started.
From Maple Creek to Cypress Park, and then went north of town to Mendham and Leader up in there, and we were building in a bunch of farms and whatnot.
Then come back down and worked around Swift Current.
Did a bunch of Mennonite settlements there, and then down and put a line from Vanguard to Aneroid.
And after that, we had 200 farms to go down at Oxbow.
That was the first year we got a big group of farms.
And we had that all finished and had a full season, a good season.
Made good money that year.
The weather was good.
Everything went fine
and we finished all our rurals by Christmas time.
Then I come to the city, and the city, the head guy for the city knew me, and he said, "They had some poles to set in the city."
So I started setting poles in the city, working by the hour, just so much an hour for men and equipment.
And the first thing I knew, I had half a dozen men going all winter.
I never stopped.
And that was the start of a 10-year stint with the city of Regina.
They asked me for a price for rental of the equipment and wages.
So I gave them rental rates on the equipment and wages plus 10% onto the payroll.
And that was it.
That went on for 10 years without any -
I just wrote them - submitted them a letter, a proposal that I would work for this, and it just kept going and going as wages went up while I charged them the more wages.
But the equipment rental, I never changed into the ten years.
I was an inflation fighter, and it was a good deal for me because we always left our poorest equipment in town here because there wasn't that much for it to do.
It was standing around mostly.
Boring machines that bore an average of about eight or ten holes a day were out in the country.
They had to bore anywhere 50, 60, 70, or 80 holes a day.
Well, that was 1948.
And in '49, I went to - oh yeah, I got the piece of high voltage line, the 72,000 from Killaly,
that's up near Melville, up to Yorkton.
I moved in on it just about - just before harvest,
I got in on it.
Previous to that, in '49, I can't recollect where we were working.
But nevertheless, we were busy.
We had some project somewhere, and I had a pretty good crew organized.
Then we went into
to do that line up from Yorkton to Melville and down to Killaly, another 18 miles.
And that had to be completed by wintertime.
Well, everything went fine.
We got the poles up, and we got about half of the wires strung when it came into the first part of December.
And it stormed, and the snow kept piling in.
I built a snow pile in front of my big Army truck,
We'd open up a road, and the next day it would blow in again.
We kept that up.
One week, we opened the road three times and never put a truck over it.
Well, we were virtually stalled.
It kept on snowing and storming.
So the Power Corporation said, "Well, we - we're not ready to use that line anyway.
We haven't got the transformers or anything."
So I shut her down until spring,
and then went back up and finished it.
That was the spring of '50.
Finished the wire stringing in some substations in Yorkton and Melville.
Then I went down and I built 18 miles of line from Stockholm to Esterhazy.
After, we're talking, 1949, yeah.
Oh yeah, then we had a bunch of farms to do in '49.
That was another thing that was just coming on stream was farm electrification.
So we got some farm jobs, units of 40 or 50 farms to a unit.
We did probably 100 farms that year, and then shut down for the winter again.
And in '50, I started out - built a line from Yorkton to Theodore.
From there, moved down to - oh, I forgot to tell you, in '49.
Yeah, after we finished up that line, then I got a job 30 miles from Yorkton to Canora.
And that was a good straightforward job.
We did that up in a hurry
and then, out of work by the end of October, so I got a job up in Alberta.
Went up to Alberta, that's right.
Built in a bunch of farms up around Lloydminster and Vegreville.
Maybe did about 70 or 80 farms up in there.
Stayed there until
the end of the season. We wound up by Christmas time, shut her down for the winter.
That was in '49, yeah.
Then in '50, I started out. Built that line from Yorkton to Theodore and from there, Lampman to Bienfait, that was another 18 miles.
From there down to Shaunavon to Eastend, that was 30 miles.
And then after that, came in and went from Montmartre to Kipling.
That was about 60 miles.
So we had - pretty well had a full season and a good season.
I'd made good money and was satisfied with the equipment. Ted Durnan come along and said, "Gus Branders is in trouble with the line
down at Ogema and Assiniboia countries, bit off more than he can chew, and I want you to go down there and help him out."
Well. He said, "Come on down and look at it."
So I got in the car and went down there, and he went in the Branders' trailer,
and he was in there 3 hours arguing with him, and he wanted me to give him a hand on that line, because it had to be done before winter set in.
And I'm sitting out in the car, and Durnan's in there arguing why he was going to take some of it away and give it to me -
me and another contractor, we were going to set the poles jointly, then we'd string the wire.
And, oh, Branders gone mad at me over that one.
He blamed me,
you know, for the whole thing.
And I said, "You better find out the facts."
I said, "How I fought against that."
I said, "I had finished a good season."
And now I, now I - Durnan puts the gun to my head and says, "You got to come out and do that.
And I'm, I'm commissioning you to do it.
You can't say no."
Well, then when we finished that, went outside of Regina here, just south of Regina, and did a bunch of, of farms,
maybe about 15 or 20 farms, just south of Regina.
So we were going till Christmas time, and then shut down for the winter again.
I said that was in '50? 50, 50.
Yeah, then '51, then I got a whole bunch of line.
I had about 300 miles of cross-arm line to build, besides all kinds of farms.
It was in the Sha - Ogema, Bengough,
Viceroy, down to Rockglen, Coronach, all in through that country.
Up north from Kyle to Elrose, and we did about 50 farms up in there, plus 7 or 8 towns.
Big season that was in '51.
'52 was a short season.
There again, we didn't start very early, didn't get any work, but always had this crew in the city, kept my basic crew.
I could always work them in the city here, never out of work.
And then '52 - and then the farms started picking up.
Yeah, we were doing farm projects all over the place.
Maybe did 250 or 300 farms that year.
That's '52.
Now, I'll just slow down for a minute here.
Take a rest while I think this over.
Now we're at '53.
'53 was mostly all rural farms.
We did about 385 or 390 farms that year.
And we had a well-organized operation by this time on farms.
You went out and you did an average of five miles a line every day.
That meant five farms.
It meant that it involved setting about 75 or 80 poles.
And about - that would, 75 or 80 poles would involve about 30 anchors.
And, of course, the transformers and everything on them, we had to complete the operation ready to throw on the switch, which we usually did before we left.
And that was in '53.
'53 is a good year all the way around.
Again, we were still operating in town here all the time.
The city was expanding.
We built all the pole lines in Regina from Campion College south, and every suburb that went in after that.
We did all the pole lines on them.
That was a sort of a bread and butter thing.
That's '53.
I just finished.
More farms.
All the way from, you can say, from Maple Creek to the west to -
Well, we never got as far as the Manitoba border, about Kipling to the east, and everything - always south of the valley, south of the Saskatchewan River and the Qu'Appelle Valley.
It was '54.
We did about 450 farms.
And '55, well, we did some towns and villages in that, too.
'55 was more farms again.
Then we went up to about 475 farms.
And this was all on a set price.
The price had been established back in, when, '50 - '48 or '50, somewhere in there, had been established.
So much a pole, so much an anchor, and so much a transformer.
Just the three items is all you had to bid on and that covered everything.
You had to do your own bushing and hauling your material and everything.
Well, '55, another big run.
We went right up till Christmas time.
Had snow, about six inches of snow, but the weather was beautiful right up till Christmas time.
We wound up, again, '56 more farms.
And it went up to about 450 farms in '56.
And occasionally there would come a chunk of high voltage line in with it.
And,
'57 now the farm operation the big units of 40 and 50 farms are pretty well all done. We're doing a scattered filling -
On tape 6.
And I just finished saying that we were - in '57, we were filling in farms.
I set up three three-man crews, and each one was on their own.
They'd haul their poles - stake their line, haul their poles, set the poles, string the wire, and connect them, hot, ready to go.
And then, well, we did pretty well.
We had a nice turnover that year.
This was a hard job to keep track of, though.
Your men are on the move all the time, so much paperwork.

I forgot to mention, when a crew went out to do a job, you had to supply them with about five sets of blueprints so that everybody working on the job had a blueprint and knew where the line was going, what to do, etc.
And that was '57.
'58, I got totally bit out.
I didn't get any work.
And then I just hung around, and pretty soon they come along, and I had my city crew going, and pretty soon they come along.
They had a little job here and a little job there, and when the dust was all settled, I had a pretty fair season.
Turned over a profit,
in spite of being bid right out.
Everybody by that time - the work was getting more scarce and everybody was bidding down.
I refused to bid below my costs.
I knew what my costs were and I stayed with that.
In '59, what did we do in 59?
I have to think hard now.
Pause.
Yeah, '59 was not a big year.
It was - but a profitable one.
I'm short of profit at the end of the year.
Then it come along '60 and I build - then I got quite a bit of line in '60.
I was building a bunch of farms and 60 miles of high line north of Regina starting up at Bulyea, and running all the way, well 60 miles north and doing all the towns
up through there, rebuilding the towns.
Oh, yeah, and we had a salvage.
The old line, which had been built in 1928, we had a salvage 60 miles a line.
And that was - well, that's as much work as building a line when it comes right down to it.
So that looked like 120 miles a line that took the best part of the summer for a medium-sized crew.
It didn't have a big crew.
And that completed '59.
Again, we had a good year.
'60 another one of those years where it was a little work here, and a little work there.
And much of this work I didn't have to bid on, I'd just submit a price to them and they'd say go out and do that job.
And,we were experienced enough that we could take on almost anything.
We built some H frame line in '60.
Then in '61, Rags Johnson and ourselves jointly bid on a piece of high-voltage line from Shellbrook to Prince Albert, which had a river crossing in it, which was always a big job.
A river crossing was always a big job.
It involved -
It would probably cost you, at that time, $8,000 or $10,000 to cross, just to cross for the river crossing itself.
We worked on that job.
And after it was finished, again, it was going around doing odds and ends, nothing -
The farm electrification by this time was pretty well finished up.
So you didn't - you didn't go out and do these projects with 40 and 50 farms on them.
You had to be mobile and scattered all over the place.
That was '61, and '62 was about the same thing again.
Then '63. Trying to think, in '63 - as the later years come come in it's harder for me to remember what the hell we were doing. And -
However by this time we're finished in the city here. This this was petered out.
But there were other things showing up.
Oh yes, the potash mine out here at Kalium, just out of Regina.

We got all their work. No matter what they had to do they called us in, not only on building it but on on design, you know what they should - they didn't have engineers that were familiar with line work at all, so you got in on the design end of it.
And we - oh yeah, potash mines were coming in.
We did quite a lot of work around the potash mines, running in lines here and there for the potash mines.
That was '63 and '64.
And '65 was my last year.
We got a big project out at Swift Current.
Well, big in those days, in dollars and cents
as I remember it, it was something - the one job, I think maybe the biggest job in dollar and cents that I had ever undertaken,
And then there was, you know, a lot of extras came in.
By the time I wound up, it was about $150,000.
But that turned out to be a bad, bad season on a price that everybody said, what a hell of a price you got for that.
Well, it started raining, and by God, it kept on raining in that dried-out country.
And there's no place by this time.
It's getting tough to get people in there.
Oil had come in, and every place was taken up with oil then.
So I bought an old camping outfit from the Power Corporation, cook car and two bunk cars,
that could handle about 20 or 25 men.
And then I got an outfit from Edmonton to come down and -
and look after it - to do the cooking.
It cost us $18 a day per man.
And I furnished the camp.
And when that job was finished, the job that everybody thought I was going to make so much money on, in the winter I said to Art, "Go over that job."
I said, "You followed it all, all the way through.
Go through the files and pick up every dollar of expense we had on that job and tell me how much money we made on it."
And when the dust was settled, figuring our overhead - our office overhead and everything on that job, we had come up with 6% net.
That's about as low as you dare go and not lose money.
But that's what can happen with weather.
We were rained out for 30 days that summer that we couldn't go out and work at all.
And then for that 30 days, you can figure there's another 30 days that you're out there
messing around in the mud when you should be sitting at home -
you're out there messing around and getting nothing done, but you're trying.
And that was my last season.
When that job was wound up then, that was the end of it for L.H.A.
And on the last day of the year, I turned the outfit over and sold it to
Art, Bill, George, and Vaughn.
With Art - they set up a company, Achen '65 Limited, and Art was the 51% shareholder.
And they went ahead, and they've never looked back in, what is it, this is the 21st year now.
And they've never been out of work, I suppose.
In the years that Art run the outfit, I never saw his books and never asked him.
But judging from the way prices went, everything, the Power started getting out of it.
They found out it cost them too damn much to build lines, and they were contracting the big jobs of a million dollars and a million and a half dollars at a shot.
And the boys got in on that, and I'm sure come out with some nice profits.
However, that's all beside the point.
I never regretted having stepped out when I did.
Never regretted for one minute that they - the things were good.
That's one thing I said to him.
"Look," I said, "Right now it looks better for the contractor than it ever did, because the power the unions got them so -
so backed up to the wall if they're almost afraid to go out and do anything themselves.
And there'd be more contracting than ever, and how right I was.
But I said, "You saw me burn myself out here in 18 years," and I said, "I'm finished.
I don't want any more."
Well, not 18 years, that's 20 years.
And I got out, and I wouldn't be alive today if I'd have stayed in there and tried to run it.
I got out, I sold, I gave them a good deal.
I never regretted that.
I go down to the office and I'm treated like if I still owned and run the company.
And many of the men are still around there that had worked for me.
Many - Ernie Smith is the oldest now.
He's been there about 26, 27, and 30 years, somewhere in there.
Yeah, it's 55 -
30 - He'd been there 32 or 33 years.
And George Jenny quit and went farming. He called in here the other night to say hello to me see how we're getting along.
He said, he - the 20 years he spent with Achen Construction he said he couldn't have worked a better place or learned more than he did in the years he was there.
Ron Kennedy, as you know got - was electrocuted at Moose Jaw.
Bill Zaharachuk died of a heart attack
just when things were really going good.
So Art took on another partner, Doug McGillivray.
He bought in.
And then Con Bandy, another guy that started working for me in '63, or '62, somewhere in there.
He bought in, but he's dropped out again.
So Art is back running the outfit - or with the shares.
He sold his shares.
to these fellas, or they had options to purchase shares and take over the company.
And I don't know, I never dig into it.
I always figure it's none of my business, and if Art wants to tell me, he'll tell me, and he don't talk much, as the saying goes.
So on the basis of that, I'm gonna cut her off right now and roll this back and see what I've said.
I'll use up the rest of this tape just in generalizing some of the things that took place.
This is July the 21st, 1986.
Ben and Leslie were here from June the 27th and just left yesterday, so I put the taping aside while they were here.
I didn't want to get things mixed up any worse than what I have them.
And we enjoyed having them at any rate.
They're good company.
They had a lot of fun entertaining themselves out in the pool.
Now I'll go back and give you a few things about
my life in the electrical field.
It was a hectic time,
saw it all grow from year to year, something new -
Every day, you might say, was different.
No two days alike.
Never went to work one day, but when I was looking forward to the challenge of the day that might come up, you never knew what might happen.
My years with the companies in the States, where I worked for three different companies, a great experience working with different people, different foremen.
I had about five or six foremen in those two years, and you learned something from each of them.
So when my day came to take charge, you try to put into effect
the good things, the good points that they had, and I think it probably helped me a lot in my years of handling men.

That you - that was the biggest thing about contracting was having your men work for you, whether you were there or not, that they liked you, they liked your attitude and what have you, and they worked.
And that's where you got production.
Production worked into dollars and cents.
And when I had 40, 50 men working for me, they all called me by my first name.
I didn't want to be Mr. Achen amongst them.
I - just Lindy to them.
And I think that goes a long ways.
You can do it in a small outfit.
You can't do it in a big outfit.
If you get big, then you lose contact with the people that are actually making you the money, the guys that are doing the work.
Well, If you look at my whole career, it was a pretty hectic one.
I started out digging holes, and from there worked up to being a lineman.
And I got to be a professional lineman, a hotline.
There was nothing I couldn't do.
And then into service, or into running a crew.
That was always an important job, to be the foreman on the crew.
And then from there I went into service work.
I learned how to read meters and how to serve customers.
Along with that, the years in Estevan were really good educational years.
You had to deal with absolutely everything.
I got acquainted with the transformers.
We used to rewind our own transformers, repair our own transformers,
which not many men in the business that had the experience that I had had that opportunity.
You get to be a lineman, that's all you knew, was be a lineman.
You didn't know anything about transformers or instruments or switchboards or power plants or anything.
Well, I've gone through all of this.
In my years in Estevan - when I left Estevan, I could have written steam papers.

I knew enough about a steam plant, having been associated with one, a small outfit again, where you - when there's trouble, you went down and helped out, and you talked to people, and you got knowledgeable about a steam plant.
Then from that, I went in and I operated a diesel plant.
Well, my knowledge in a diesel plant, it was only a one-cylinder engine, but if you got
a six-cylinder engine, it's the same thing, only six times as big, that's all.
I was in the instrument end.
I could calibrate meters.
I could calibrate switchboard instruments.
I knew all about a switchboard and the workings of it in a power plant.
And all knowledge that you gathered, put on the back shelf, and maybe someday you would use it;
and I did; All through the years that I was contracting things turned up where I could - where I might even be called in to say "We've got a problem here. Can you help us?"

And many times the power people called me and they wouldn't have anybody in the area that was capable of diagnosing a trouble or something, and those are some of the benefits you got through your wide experience in the business.
Yeah when you're a serviceman you had to collect money, you had to take care of complaint, you had to do everything, you got to know -
I had 1200 customers in Estevan and I knew every one of them and they all knew me.
And, in the minefields got a great knowledge of mining, both underground and strip mining
having served the field - for seven years I was in there.
It was a hectic life, yeah.
You worked - you were on call 30 days a month and 24 hours a day.
You were never paid overtime or anything, but you were proud as hell that you had a job, and you knew you could do it.
When the whistle blew, you went out and answered the call and didn't sit back and figure, "Well, how many hours can I get out of this?" or anything.
You'd go out and find the trouble and repair it and get back home and get to bed, finish your sleep so that you could go to work in the morning.
And that's the way it went from one thing to the other.
I think I related earlier that I had started doing some inside wiring way back in 1931.
At the time I built the power system into the village of Midale.
Well later on then, things got slack and I continued to work, and I kept on wiring and wiring and finally the wiring inspector said, "Why don't you write your journeyman's paper?"
So I wrote my journeyman's paper and I think I related to that earlier
I was so darn tickled when I got it because I didn't think I made the exams.
I still carry it today.
And it - while I really never did much wiring, there wasn't anything about it that I didn't know or couldn't sit down and figure out.
And it was something to have that journeyman's paper in your hip pocket.
The one I have now will run until I think it's 1990
before it expires.
I probably won't be around here.
And the company still keeps me on.
Art Miller's company still keeps me on pay on the small retainer, because I have that journeyman's paper. And some of the work that they run into from time to time,
they're supposed to have a journeyman electrician on the job.
Whether he does any work or not, that's another thing.
So therefore, they keep me available in case something turns up where they need a journeyman, and I'm there.
And all very interesting, you know, to be able to walk in and see an electrical layout, know what it's all about, not a puzzle or anything.
To the average layman it is, but it's just as simple as ABC when you
know how, what the principles of it are.
As far as being a technician is concerned, you give me this little tape recorder here and I can't even tell you what makes it work, or telephones or anything.
That was completely out of my line.
The radio was out of my line.
But my life was get the electricity from the generator to the
output where it was going to be used.
That was always the big thing. That's what I specialized in.
However I'm - I'm glad it's all over.
I had one heck of a life in the electrical field.
I got to be pretty well known in Saskatchewan, and uh -
Now they're all dying off.
I'm one of the older guys in the businesses, as -
I doubt if there's maybe two or three fellows in the whole province, of my age that are still living, that have gone through the business from one end to the other or for a period of 35, 40 years.
So I would say to anyone,
it's an exciting life - the life of "service" was what you were in. "Service" is what you're selling. When I was a contractor I was selling service.
And I still say, like my fr - George Jenny, the fellow that worked for me, said, "If I had a son I'd want him to go and work for an outfit just like yours," he said, "Not too big,
where you get to know everything about everything," he said.
"The knowledge I got there and the 20 years I worked there," he said, "Are just unbelievable."
He had worked from a boy on the end of a digging bar up to becoming a journeyman lineman,
and a real good one.
And then he had saved his money, and when he got enough money to buy a farm, he bought the farm and said,
"Now I'm going to semi-retire." And that's what he's doing, really enjoying life.
I don't know if I mentioned anywhere along, the money end of this business.
When I started working in Worthington, Iowa, we were working nine hours a day, and I got 35 cents an hour, paid my own room and board, drove a car,
Model T Ford Coupe.
The foreman came along one day after I'd been there about three weeks.
He said, "I like the way you work."
He said, "I put in an application for a raise to 40 cents an hour, and the head office turned it down." And -
But he said, "I'm going to plug your time enough to bring it up to 40 cents an hour."
That's when I was a groundman, digging holes and doing anything on the ground.
Then when I got to be a lineman, my wages stayed the same, but I was only there for about a month, and I left.
And I think I told you earlier in the story, when I quit in Nebraska and went up to work for my brother Lorie, who was sick at Sioux Falls, South Dakota.
Then when I started work the next time, I was up at
near Aberdeen, South Dakota.
And I started as a second-class lineman.
I was getting 45 cents an hour.

- a lineman, my wages stayed the same, but I was only there for about a month, and I left, I think I told you earlier in the story, when I quit in Nebraska and went up to work for my brother, Lorie, who was sick at Sioux Falls, South Dakota.
Then when I started work the next time, I was up near Aberdeen, South Dakota.
And I started as a second-class lineman.
I was getting 45 cents an hour.
And they paid our room and board up to $3 a day.
Well, you could live on $3 a day quite handy in those days.
So now I was getting -
there was no income tax deductions or anything at that time.
I was getting 45 cents an hour, and I went through two different foremen with the same crew.
The first one died, and the second one was transferred.
The third one was a younger man, and he took a liking to me.
And he come along one day, and he said, "You're only getting 45 cents an hour,"
he said, "I'm going to put you up to 50 cents an hour.
That's a first-class lineman's wages."
He said, "You're doing as much work as any of the other fellows."
He said, "You ever work hotline?"
I said, "No."
"Well," he said, "I'll teach you a little bit about it."
So, you started wearing rubber gloves now, and then he'd take me out to cut in transformers and this and that.
So you familiarized yourself with - protecting yourself is the big thing when you're working around hot wires.
And then from there, when I went up to
Kenmare, North Dakota, that was a year later.
There I got 78 cents an hour, but I had to pay my own room and board.
Then when I came up to Canada, which was the next year, there I got 80 cents an hour - 82 cents an hour, I think it was, and paid my own room and board.
And from there on, things went bad.
And it came back where a lineman was getting 50 cents an hour again, instead of 82.
So I worked in the summer of '34, '35, part of '33 at 50 cents an hour, and paid my own room and board.
But it didn't cost you much for room and board.
You could get keep most places for $1, a dollar and a quarter a day.
Some places they'd even do your - go to a private home.
They'd even do your laundry for you.
So it was all very relative.
And when I started operating the power plant, I was getting $75 a month.
Then when I - when they closed down that plant, which I related to earlier, and I went back to the Dominion Electric power,
There I was getting $135 a month, paid my own keep.
But again, room and board for a dollar a day.
In fact, I was staying at Phyllis' folks' place, and that's where I got room and board and laundry for $1 a day.
And as wages went up then, after I was married, that was about three years later,
I got a $10 raise.
That was my first raise in three years.
I got a $10 a month raise, and then of course you paid an extra $5 a month room and board.
Groceries were going up and what have you.
So it was all very relative.
Well, when I left there, then I started contracting.
I didn't take
any steady wage out of the company.
I just took - drew out money as I wanted to.
But you didn't need much to live on, maybe, oh, $200, $300 a month.
And when I incorporated then in 1953, I didn't know what wages I should draw.
So I asked the auditors, "What wages?
should I take out of this?"
"Well, the rate your company is going, you should have $1,000 a month."

It looked as though it would never go away.

It's about 10 above today.

From 1930 to 1939.

find some discrepancies in our taping because we're not experts at it like you and ben but from now on I'll have grandma read out your question and then we'll answer it that translates here comes question number one how did you make coffee in those days well some of the farmers roasted wheat or roasted barley and boiled it and use it for coffee and I can tell you it was
Plenty hard to take.
But I imagine you did have coffee if you wanted to buy it.
Oh yes, you could buy coffee and it was cheap, but then who had the money to buy it?
Did your family have a Bennett buggy?
No, we never had a Bennett buggy in our family somehow or other.
We escaped being that poor.
How much was milk and bread?
Well, you could buy bread in the stores at 10 cents a loaf and milk at about 5 cents a quart.
In fact, you could get cream, good, thick cream for 10 cents a quart.
And it wasn't pasteurized, was it?
No, no, it was straight from the cows, so to speak.
All right.
How much were wages in those days if you had a job?
Well, many young men had to work on the farms for $5 a month.
Most their wages were about 30 cents an hour.
That would be like $3 a day.
If you were working in a plant or in a coal mine, any labor of that type.
In the stores, if you were a clerk in the store, you probably got $50 a month.
Well, I had a business, and I used to take $8 a week out.
But I lived at home, so I didn't have to pay much board and room.
That would be $32 a month.
But that was enough to buy clothes on.
And I don't remember, we never had a Bennett buggy.
We had a car, but we couldn't afford to buy a license for it.
And I don't remember making a coffee or anything like that, but I know things were pretty tough.
But everybody else was poor, so it didn't seem to hurt too much.
Yeah, everybody was on the same level.
You'd be brought to a dance for 35 cents, and they'd throw in a lunch, and all that stuff.
And the church had a lot of card parties, and they'd serve a lunch after.
And bingo parties for a nickel a game, or whatever.
But anyway, we had a good time, even if we didn't have any money.
And I think maybe we had a better time than the teenagers do nowadays.
Yeah, you invented everything.
You invented your games, and if you had a jigsaw puzzle, when you were through with it, you didn't throw it away.
You'd give it to a neighbor, and they'd give it to another neighbor, and everybody concerned.
We used to do a lot of skating, and we lived around a country where it was hilly, so we did a lot of tobogganing and skiing.
We didn't have the warmest clothes in the world, but we always seemed to manage.
That's for sure.
The clothes we had in those days compared to today were really inadequate.
No such a thing as a parka or a hood.
And I can remember wearing, to high school, wearing long underwear.
Now I can just see the young girls wearing long underwear to high school now, as the saying is, long johns.
Yes, and you had to walk about a full mile to get there, and you went regardless of what the weather was.
Well, anyway, Les, this is about it, and I hope this did you some good for your school work.
During the Depression years, I spent a lot of time on the farm, and wages were not of consideration.
But one winter, I did work for my brother.
He was running the farm.
And I got $8 a month, and I worked from 6 o'clock in the morning to 6 o'clock at night, every day, seven days a week.
We had 60 head of cattle, 50 sheep, about 40 horses, and maybe 60 or 70 pigs, and 100 hens that were laying eggs.
And by the time we got up in the morning and made the rounds and
give them all their breakfast, and then you come in to the house, I can tell you, you're ready to eat your breakfast too, and you didn't question what it was, you ate whatever was prepared for you.
But all in all, we had a lot of fun there.
We used to get together with the neighbors and play cards, and we played poker, and we used matches for money.
No money ever exchanged hands, it was just, everybody was allotted 100 matches, and then from that,
You'd see who was the best card player at the end of the night.
You'd put the matches back in the box when you were finished.
And then we'd get together as families, total families, from 60 years down to six years old.
And you'd play games, all kinds of crazy games that everyone was involved in.
And you had more laughs than a little bit.
You went home feeling happy.
You never spent any money.
You didn't have any money to spend.
If you went to a dance, I said earlier, "A country dance would cost you maybe 35 cents admission."
And along with that, there was a lunch first, because everything was so cheap.
It makes sense.
It's for next to nothing.
Oh, it was a great life.
I was young at that time, and everybody else was young, and you accepted the fact you were all on the same level, and you lived life one day at a time.
